cuisine: 寿司・穴子料理・創作和食
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市西二階町103
geocode_note: GSI geocode resolved to chō level (西二階町) without street number 103; coordinates are approximately 230m from target center, well within 1.15km radius. Actual shop likely within the same block.
neighborhood: 西二階町（姫路駅北・大手前通り沿い）
seats_text: カウンター4席・個室カウンター2席・掘りごたつ個室・2階大個室（最大22名）、合計44席
closed_days: 月曜日（祝日の場合も休業）
cultural_comfort_note: Intimate counter and tatami private-room setting with Japanese-language service only; online reservation possible via Tabelog or AutoReserve but communication with staff will be in Japanese.
vegan_detail: This is a sushi and conger eel specialist. The menu is built around fish, seafood, egg custard (chawanmushi), wagyu, and chicken. Dashi is almost certainly fish-based (katsuo/iriko), and nearly every dish contains animal products. No vegan options are listed, and no indication that accommodations are available. Not suitable for vegans.
gf_detail: Standard Japanese kitchen: soy sauce contains wheat, tempura batter is wheat-based (including the signature conger eel tempura), and dashi may contain fish stock processed at facilities using wheat. Nigiri rice itself is gluten-free, and some sashimi dishes could be requested without soy sauce, but no dedicated GF protocol or allergen disclosures are published. Celiac diners must contact the restaurant directly before visiting — the kitchen has not demonstrated GF awareness and cross-contamination risk is high from tempura frying.

bio.background: Born 1978 in Hyogo Prefecture. Trained at high-end Himeji establishments including hotel restaurants and Michelin-listed counters before opening Wa no Shoku Kanade in March 2022 as owner-chef.
bio.specialty: Steamed conger eel nigiri (蒸し穴子握り) — anago sourced locally from the Seto Inland Sea, steamed and served with a reduction sauce made from the eel's own braising liquid; the texture is described by guests as melting immediately on the palate.
bio.philosophy: A welcoming space (居心地のよい空間づくり) where conversation flows naturally across every seat — counter, sunken-hearth private room, or second-floor banquet — paired with fresh seasonal fish and a hand-picked selection of over 20 Hyogo sake labels.

anecdotes.text: A Tabelog reviewer noted in July 2023 that the owner previously worked at a traditional counter sushi restaurant before striking out on his own, suggesting the Kanade counter format is a deliberate nod to that lineage.

The Friday-to-Sunday lunch, called 奏の遊び膳 (Kanade no Asobizen), rotates monthly and presents seasonal ingredients in playful multi-preparation trays — a format distinct from the more formal dinner omakase.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen actively fries tempura — including the signature conger eel tempura with wheat batter — making shared-fryer cross-contamination a realistic concern. No dedicated GF fryer is mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese shoyu (wheat-containing) is the presumed base for sashimi service, nigiri, and cooked sauces. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is listed on the menu or website.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish dashi underpins virtually every dish in a traditional washoku kitchen of this type; no plant-based dashi option is disclosed.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, no published allergy protocol, and no review mentions of allergy accommodation were found across Tabelog, Hitosara, Gnavi, or the official website. Advance telephone inquiry is strongly recommended.

positives.text: Some sashimi items are naturally free of added sauces and could in principle be ordered with rice only, keeping wheat exposure minimal if the kitchen is briefed in advance.

caveats: Sunday dinner availability is inconsistent across sources (Tabelog lists dinner Tue-Sun while some sources suggest Sunday dinner is closed); hours_raw flags this for verification. Seat count varies between 27 and 44 across sources — 44 is from Tabelog's own listing and likely includes second-floor private capacity. Geocode resolved at chō level only.

name_ja: 讃岐うどん めんめ
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市本町68
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 兵庫県姫路市本町６８番地 returned [134.687363, 34.835873]. Within target box (lat 34.823–34.843, lng 134.679–134.705) and ~583 m from Sanyo Himeji Station per Tabelog.
neighborhood: 本町・山陽姫路駅付近
vegan_detail: This is not a vegan-friendly restaurant. The signature menme udon is built around bonito flakes (鰹節) and egg yolk mixed into the bowl. The dashi broth is fish-based (katsuo). No plant-based or animal-free options are documented anywhere in the menu.
gf_detail: Udon noodles are made from wheat flour — the core product of the restaurant. There is no gluten-free alternative and no dedicated fryer. The dashi soy sauce (だし醤油) used in the signature dish contains wheat. Cross-contamination risk is extremely high given the wheat-only kitchen. Unsafe for celiac or wheat-allergic diners.
seats_text: Counter seating with a small number of tables; no private rooms. Exact seat count not published. Queues are common at peak times.
cultural_comfort_note: A tightly run, cash-only counter shop with no English menu or reservation system; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu board will get you by, but expect to navigate entirely in Japanese.

bio.background: A long-running family operation in Himeji's Honcho district, steps from the approach to Himeji Castle. Accounts from regular visitors describe roughly 40 years of continuous operation, run by a female proprietor with family involvement. The shop follows a simple, old-school rhythm: noodles are hand-made on the premises each day and the kitchen closes when the daily batch runs out, sometimes well before the posted closing time.
bio.specialty: Menme udon — the house signature: a bowl of hand-pulled noodles topped with a generous mound of bonito flakes and a raw egg yolk, finished with dashi soy sauce and green onion; diners mix everything together before eating. The noodles are notably thicker than standard Sanuki udon and softer in texture, giving the bowl a distinctly gentle character.
bio.philosophy: Straightforward, ingredient-led cooking at an accessible price point. The menu is short, the space is unpretentious, and the focus is entirely on the noodles.

anecdotes.text: Regulars note that each bowl includes 'one particularly thick end piece' (1本だけ太い端っこ) — an artifact of hand-cutting the noodles that has become a beloved marker of an authentic Menme experience.
One long-time patron described the owners as having been in operation for approximately 40 years; as of a February 2026 visit the shop was busy with international tourists around 2 PM.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat is the kitchen's only product — udon noodles are made from wheat flour on-site daily. Cross-contamination with wheat is unavoidable throughout the entire operation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature menme udon uses dashi soy sauce (だし醤油), which is wheat-containing. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bonito flakes (鰹節) are a primary topping on the signature dish and are present throughout the kitchen. Fish-based dashi is the foundation of the broth. No vegan-safe preparation path exists.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy menu, allergen sheet, or staff allergy-handling procedure is documented on any source reviewed. Given the language environment (Japanese-only) and the simplicity of the operation, allergy communication would be very difficult.

caveats: Hours may vary seasonally or when the daily noodle supply runs out early — confirm before visiting. The proprietor is actively involved in daily operations so occasional unannounced closures are possible.

name_ja: おもてなしダイニング 福亭
cuisine: 穴子料理・季節海鮮・居酒屋（ハラール対応）
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市亀井町75
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 兵庫県姫路市亀井町75番地 returned [134.692398, 34.830044]; within target box (34.823–34.843, 134.679–134.705) and approximately 340m from the Himeji Castle–Station midpoint.
neighborhood: 亀井町（姫路駅北口5分）
closed_days: 水曜定休（臨時休業あり：7月9日・8月20日・27日）
seats_text: 総席数192席。2階に個室あり（利用時10%サービス料加算）、最大60名まで対応可能。
cultural_comfort_note: Staff have been observed helping foreign diners with menu translation and dietary substitutions; English menus likely available given halal-certified tourist-oriented positioning and TripAdvisor presence.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu exists. One TripAdvisor reviewer reported that a preset meal was successfully adapted for a celiac diner with only a few substitutions, suggesting staff willingness to accommodate. However, standard Japanese cuisine relies heavily on wheat-containing soy sauce (shoyu) and dashi stock that may include soy sauce; tempura batter uses wheat flour; Himeji oden broth is not confirmed wheat-free. Cross-contamination risk is unquantified. Advance reservation and explicit communication in Japanese recommended for any GF request.
vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu. The restaurant is fish-focused (anago/conger eel is the signature) and the halal menu centers on fish and vegetables. One reviewer noted an oden dish was adjusted to tofu and vegetables on request, excluding meat. Hidden dashi (fish stock) is very likely in most dishes; bonito-based dashi is standard in Japanese cooking. Vegetarian icon appears on the Halal Gourmet Japan listing, suggesting vegetable-only options are possible with advance notice, but full vegan accommodation (no dashi, no fish sauces) requires direct confirmation with the kitchen. Advance reservation required.

bio.background: Fukutei is operated under the Seiko Club (清交倶楽部) hospitality group and has been a fixture of Himeji's dining scene for at least a decade, situated a five-minute walk north of Himeji Station along the Miyuki-dori shopping street. The restaurant built an unusual dual reputation: as a go-to neighborhood spot for seasonal Seto Inland Sea seafood, and as one of Japan's early adopters of halal-certified fish cuisine—reportedly the first in Japan to offer halal conger-eel hitsumabushi (a layered rice dish) and halal anago tempura.
bio.origin: 姫路市亀井町、瀬戸内海の恵みを活かした海鮮料理店
bio.specialty: Conger eel (穴子) from Harima-nada in multiple preparations—hitsumabushi, tempura, clay-pot rice with sea bream—alongside daily-selected sashimi and Himeji oden
bio.philosophy: The chef visits the fish market each morning to hand-select the day's catch by sight and touch, refusing to compromise on ingredient quality. The stated mission is to make delicious seasonal fish accessible to as many guests as possible and to create smiles through food (食を通して笑顔になってほしい).

anecdotes.text: Fukutei is cited by Halal Gourmet Japan as serving Japan's first halal conger-eel hitsumabushi and conger-eel tempura, with dates offered as dessert—a nod to Muslim dining customs.

A TripAdvisor reviewer reported that the kitchen successfully adapted a fixed-course meal for a celiac diner with only minor substitutions, and separately that a meat-free oden was arranged on request.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information about dedicated fryers or separate prep surfaces. Tempura (wheat batter) is a menu staple, raising shared-fryer cross-contamination risk for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) is almost certainly used throughout the kitchen; no mention of tamari or GF shoyu substitutes anywhere on the menu or official site.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish-centric kitchen; dashi stock (bonito/fish-based) is standard across Japanese cuisine and likely used as a base in most dishes including oden broth, rice dishes, and sauces.

staff_allergy_handling.text: A TripAdvisor reviewer reported that staff successfully modified a preset meal for a celiac guest with a few substitutions, and adapted an oden dish to exclude meat on request—suggesting genuine willingness to accommodate, but no formal allergy protocol is documented.
Hitosara lists food allergy considerations as accepted (食物アレルギー対応可). Advance reservation and explicit communication required.

positives.text: Halal-certified with a dedicated halal menu (anago hitsumabushi, anago tempura); no pork used in halal dishes; on-site prayer space provided—showing serious dietary-protocol awareness.
Fully non-smoking establishment.
Hitosara lists gluten-free accommodations as available (グルテンフリー対応可).

caveats: GF confidence is rated 'ask' not higher: the Hitosara listing mentions GF accommodation but no formal protocol, dedicated equipment, or tamari substitution is documented anywhere. Wheat-soy-sauce and tempura-fryer risk are real concerns for celiac diners. Vegan status is 'ask' because dashi is almost certainly present throughout the kitchen; vegetarian/fish-free requests need explicit advance coordination. The restaurant is not independent—it is part of the Seiko Club group. Hours marked 'irregular' due to confirmed ad-hoc Thursday closures in summer 2026 (July 9, Aug 20 & 27); always verify before visiting. Seat count of 192 is from Tabelog; usable capacity noted as up to 60 people (likely the largest bookable private room), not total restaurant capacity.

address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市本町68
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 兵庫県姫路市本町68 → 34.835873, 134.687363. Approximately 530m from target center (34.8331, 134.6921), well within 1.15km radius.
neighborhood: 本町 (Honmachi)
cuisine: Izakaya — Himeji oden (ginger soy sauce style) and charcoal-grilled local chicken
seats_text: Counter seating and tatami mat area; currently operating from a temporary outdoor space (parking lot) following a 2024 fire — call ahead to confirm current setup
cultural_comfort_note: Menu, staff, and signage are entirely in Japanese; a phone reservation is expected and essentially requires Japanese, though pointing at dishes once seated is manageable.
vegan_detail: Teishu is a chicken-and-oden izakaya. The oden broth is a long-simmered dashi base; the grilled-chicken menu dominates. No plant-based alternatives are offered, and the kitchen is structured entirely around animal proteins.
gf_detail: Himeji oden is served with ginger soy sauce — conventional Japanese soy sauce contains wheat and is the defining flavoring of this dish. The oden broth itself is soy-sauce-based. Yakisoba is on the menu. No dedicated gluten-free options or alternatives have been documented. Celiacs should avoid unless they can clearly communicate needs in Japanese and staff confirm wheat-free substitution is possible, which is unlikely given the core broth.
caveats: Tabelog shows 掲載保留 (listing on hold) because the restaurant's original building was destroyed by fire in April 2024. The owner rebuilt incrementally and reopened at a temporary outdoor location (parking lot) in May 2025. A June 2026 Tabelog review confirms active operation. The permanent rebuild was pending Cultural Affairs Agency approval as of mid-2025 due to proximity to the Himeji Castle approach path. Hours and seating capacity at the temporary location may differ from historical listings — call ahead (+81-79-222-8592) before visiting.

bio.background: Teishu traces its roots to roughly 60 years ago when it opened as Yougetsu Shokudo (ようげつ食堂) near the Himeji Civic Hall. The founder chose the name 停主 — meaning something like 'a host who bids guests to pause and rest' — to express a philosophy of unhurried welcome. The current owner is the second generation. The shop stood for decades on a lane facing Himeji Castle until an April 2024 fire destroyed the building completely. With no insurance payout (coverage had lapsed due to an adjuster's hospitalization), the owner relied on community support — including a charity T-shirt campaign at ¥4,000 per piece — and his son's help to clear the site and begin reconstruction. In May 2025 he reopened in a makeshift outdoor space on the parking lot, described by visitors as evoking a nostalgic Showa-era beer garden atmosphere. As of June 2026 the kitchen is still serving from that temporary setup while the permanent rebuild awaits Cultural Affairs Agency clearance, required because the site lies along the official approach path to Himeji Castle.
bio.specialty: Himeji oden — twelve varieties of slow-simmered oden presented in a deep, richly flavored broth, finished tableside with ginger soy sauce that balances the sweetness with heat. The other pillar is charcoal-grilled Banshu local chicken: the off-menu matsuba cut (a sliver of collarbone cartilage, one per bird) is a cult favourite, and hineppon — aged hen dressed in ponzu — is the benchmark order.
bio.philosophy: Traditional homestyle cooking rooted in place — the kind of food the founder described as 'okafukuro no aji' (the taste of mother's cooking) — served in an atmosphere meant to make strangers feel like regulars.

anecdotes.text: After the 2024 fire levelled the building, the owner discovered his insurance had lapsed because the adjuster handling renewal had been hospitalised — leaving him without any payout. The community responded: a charity T-shirt sold for ¥4,000 helped fund the rebuild, and a temporary outdoor kitchen opened in May 2025 on the scorched parking lot.
The walls of the original shop were lined with signed photographs from visiting celebrities, including actress Haruka Ayase, testament to the restaurant's draw beyond the local neighbourhood.
A June 2026 Tabelog reviewer described the atmosphere as 'rich and unique to a temporary location' and noted the reopening after the fire, awarding a perfect score.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen prepares yakisoba (wheat noodles) alongside oden and grilled items in what is currently a compact temporary outdoor setup, making cross-contamination a realistic concern. No separation protocols have been documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Himeji oden is defined by its ginger soy sauce finish (生姜醤油). Standard Japanese soy sauce (shoyu) contains wheat as a primary ingredient. The oden broth itself is soy-sauce-based, so gluten is embedded in the dish's identity, not just a condiment.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is built around chicken and dashi-based broth. There is no plant-based cooking line and no documented effort to keep any item free of animal products.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling protocols or English-language allergy communication have been documented for this restaurant. The staff environment is Japanese-only and the kitchen is a temporary setup post-fire.

positives.text: The restaurant accepts credit cards (JCB, AMEX, Diners) and PayPay, reducing one barrier for foreign visitors, and single diners are explicitly welcome at the counter.

name_ja: 亜米麻辣湯 姫路店
neighborhood: 姫路駅周辺・呉服町
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市呉服町40 ヴィークル呉服町1
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI AddressSearch on 兵庫県姫路市呉服町４０番地; coordinates confirmed within target box and 242m from area center.
vegan_detail: The soup base is beef bone broth (牛骨だし) simmered with 20+ medicinal herbs — not vegan. Vegetable and tofu ingredients can be selected, but they cook in the same beef broth. No plant-based broth alternative is listed on any source.
gf_detail: Six noodle types are offered: sweet potato glass noodles, corn noodles, potato noodles, rice noodles (potentially wheat-free), and Chinese wheat noodles plus udon (wheat). The soup base and condiments (black vinegar, Sichuan peppercorn oil, sesame sauce) have unknown soy sauce and wheat content — the chain publishes no allergen sheet. No dedicated gluten-free handling or separate fryer confirmed. Celiac diners should inquire directly before ordering.
seats_text: 56席（2階：カウンター14席＋テーブル12席、3階：テーブル30席）。ソロ向けカウンター席あり。
cultural_comfort_note: Korean chain with QR-code ordering, self-service ingredient selection, and payment via international cards and QR apps (Alipay, WeChat Pay) — visitors without Japanese are likely comfortable here.

bio.background: Korean mala tang chain originally operating approximately 80 locations in South Korea, recognized with the Korean Consumer Brand Award in the mala tang franchise category for two consecutive years (2022 and 2023). The Himeji location, which opened February 21, 2026, is the chain's first Hyogo Prefecture outpost, following Japan entries in Tokyo's Shin-Okubo and Osaka's Tsuruhashi.
bio.specialty: Mala tang — a customizable spicy hot pot where diners pick ingredients from a refrigerated showcase on the ground floor, priced by weight at ¥400 per 100g. The broth is beef-bone stock slow-simmered with over 20 medicinal herbs including jujubes and goji berries, offered across five spice levels from 0 (clear broth, child-friendly) to 4 (extra spicy).
bio.philosophy: Medicinal herb cuisine (薬膳) meets modern build-your-own customization — over 40 ingredient choices let each diner tailor heat, texture, and nutrition to their preference.

anecdotes.text: The chain's signature broth recipe reportedly took approximately one year to develop, blending beef bones with more than 20 medicinal herbs to achieve a depth that builds as you eat.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All ingredients — including those that may contain wheat — are cooked in a shared beef-bone broth pot. No separation of wheat-containing and gluten-free noodles is confirmed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The soup base ingredient list (beef bone broth, 20+ medicinal herbs and spices) is not fully disclosed. Condiments include sesame sauce and Sichuan peppercorn oil of unknown composition; soy sauce presence in the broth cannot be ruled out.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All ingredients are cooked in beef-bone broth; there is no plant-based broth option, so vegan cross-contact is certain.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen sheet or staff allergy protocol is published on Tabelog, HotPepper, or the official website. Diners with allergies should call ahead (090-9615-5959) to discuss their needs.

positives.text: Multiple wheat-free noodle options are available (sweet potato glass noodles, corn noodles, potato noodles, rice noodles) allowing diners to avoid wheat noodles specifically — though the broth wheat status remains unconfirmed.

caveats: Payment: the official website at launch listed cash-only, but Tabelog and HotPepper (updated post-opening) both confirm credit cards and QR payments are accepted — the cash-only policy appears to have been initial/temporary. Hours: all sources agree on 11:00-21:00 daily with no closed days, but last-order time of 20:30 is noted on one source. The chain publishes no allergen sheet for the Himeji location; dietary safety details are inference from ingredient descriptions only.

name_ja: 姫路おでん 地料理 居酒屋 じごろ小廣
cuisine: Himeji oden, Banshu local cuisine, anago (conger eel), izakaya
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市駅前町222 姫路駅前第一ビル 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI AddressSearch on 兵庫県姫路市駅前町222; coordinates place the restaurant approximately 620m south-southwest of the Himeji Castle/Station midpoint, well within the 1.15km target radius.
neighborhood: JR姫路駅前 (Himeji Station front, Ekimae-cho)
seats_text: Counter and table seating; approximately 22 seats total. No children permitted.
hours_raw: Mon–Fri 17:30–23:00 (L.O. 22:30); Sat 11:30–23:00 (L.O. 22:30); Sun/holidays 11:30–22:00 (L.O. 21:30). No regular weekly closing day (不定休); occasional irregular closures.
closed_days: No fixed closing day (不定休); occasional irregular closures throughout the year.
cultural_comfort_note: A small local izakaya near Himeji Station with no English-language menu or signage noted; Japanese communication will be needed to order and ask about ingredients.
vegan_detail: The kitchen is built around bonito-and-kombu dashi, seafood (octopus, conger eel, sea bream, oysters), eggs, and meat. No plant-based alternatives were identified on the menu. Vegan dining is not possible here.
gf_detail: Gluten is pervasive throughout the menu. Himeji oden is served with homemade ginger soy sauce (wheat-based shoyu). The menu includes multiple tempura items, karaage, chikuwa (fish cake with wheat binder), and noodle dishes. The dashi base likely contains bonito and kombu but the soy sauce used in seasoning is standard wheat-containing shoyu. No GF menu, no allergen labeling, and no indication that wheat-free soy sauce is available. Celiac or wheat-allergic diners should avoid this restaurant.

bio.background: Jigoro Kohiro is a small independent izakaya occupying the ground floor of the Himeji Station Front Building, about a two-minute walk north of JR Himeji Station along the Omizo-dori shopping arcade. The restaurant centers its identity on Himeji oden — a regional variant of Japan's beloved hot-pot dish that sets itself apart by being eaten with homemade ginger soy sauce rather than mustard. The ginger-soy tradition has roots in Himeji's early Showa-era waterfront districts and was formally named 'Himeji oden' in 2006 by a local revitalization group.
bio.specialty: Himeji oden served with homemade ginger soy sauce; grilled and tempura anago (conger eel); the 'White Heron Platter' (白鷺盛り) featuring local Murotsu octopus, daikon, and ichikawa-town boiled eggs, with eel steamed then briefly simmered in broth.
bio.philosophy: Showcase Banshu and Himeji's local larder — Murotsu octopus, Ichikawa-town eggs, regional sake from labels such as Okuharima — in a relaxed counter-and-table atmosphere suited to solo diners and couples alike.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's oden broth is built from bonito and kombu dashi and simmered for one to two days before service; the White Heron Platter eel is steamed first, then briefly passed through the broth to achieve tenderness and a cleaner aroma.

Himeji oden's defining ginger-soy sauce tradition likely began in the city's early Showa-period waterfront district, where locals used ginger soy sauce to temper the sweetness of Kanto-style oden — a practice that only a handful of regions in all of Japan share.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Multiple tempura items are listed (conger eel tempura, lotus root tempura, yam tempura, octopus tempura, surume tempura, kisu tempura) indicating active wheat-batter frying; no separate fryer for gluten-free items is noted.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature Himeji oden is served with homemade ginger soy sauce; multiple dishes reference soy-based sauces, ponzu, dashi soy, and wasabi mayo — all made with standard wheat-containing shoyu. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bonito-and-kombu dashi is used as the base broth simmered one to two days; it permeates the oden and likely touches most hot dishes. Fish, seafood, eggs, and meat are cooked throughout the kitchen.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, allergy inquiry system, or staff allergy-handling procedure is documented on any available source. Japanese communication will be required to ask questions, and the kitchen is unlikely to have protocols for celiac-safe preparation.

positives.text: The restaurant is a small owner-operated counter with a focused menu, meaning staff likely have detailed knowledge of their ingredients and preparations — a Japanese-speaking diner may be able to get honest answers about specific dishes.

caveats: Hours vary across sources; the Mon–Fri 17:30 open and Sat/Sun 11:30 open pattern is most consistent. Seat count differs by source (21–30); 22 used as the most cited figure. No official website was found beyond Instagram; operating status confirmed as open as of February 2025 (Hotpepper). Irregular closure days mean checking before visiting is advisable.

neighborhood: 白銀町（山陽姫路駅周辺）
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市白銀町91
geocode_note: GSI geocode matched 兵庫県姫路市白銀町９１番地 exactly; coordinates fall within target box and approx 324m from zone center.
seats_text: Seat count not published; small counter-style soba shop run by a couple.
cultural_comfort_note: A cash-only neighborhood soba shop with no English menu and no reservation system; basic Japanese or pointing at a menu board will get you through.
vegan_detail: No vegan options confirmed. Sarashina-style soba tsuyu is almost certainly made with katsuo (bonito) dashi, which is standard for this style. No plant-based dashi alternative is mentioned on any listing. The menu includes tempura dishes (shrimp, vegetables) indicating mixed animal/plant frying. Nothing on the menu or any review suggests vegan accommodation.
gf_detail: Not safe for celiac or gluten-sensitive diners. Sarashina (更科) soba uses refined white buckwheat flour that is routinely blended with wheat flour — this is not a 100% buckwheat (juwari) shop. The tsuyu broth contains wheat-based soy sauce (standard shoyu). Tempura is battered with wheat flour. There is no dedicated GF fryer, no GF labeling, and no mention of allergen accommodation on any source. Cross-contamination risk is high in a small kitchen making noodles daily.

bio.background: A small, long-established soba shop in Himeji's Shirogane-cho district, run by a husband-and-wife team. The restaurant sits about 240 meters from Sanyo Himeji Station and serves Sarashina-style buckwheat noodles — a refined white soba made from the inner heart of the buckwheat grain, prized for its delicate flavor and smooth texture.
bio.specialty: 天ぷらそば（Tempura Soba）— crispy-battered shrimp, shishito pepper, sweet potato, and carrot over soba in hot broth

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe the atmosphere as clean and homey (清潔でアットホームな雰囲気), with the tempura praised for its light, crispy batter (サクサクの衣).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Sarashina soba typically contains wheat flour mixed with buckwheat; wheat flour is also used for tempura batter in the same small kitchen, creating high cross-contamination risk.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tsuyu (soba broth/dipping sauce) at traditional soba restaurants is made with standard wheat-based soy sauce (shoyu); no tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Katsuo (bonito) dashi is the standard base for soba tsuyu at Sarashina-style shops; shrimp tempura is also on the menu, making animal-product contact unavoidable.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy menu, allergen labeling, or accommodation policy is mentioned on any platform. Cash-only, no-reservation shop with no English materials; allergy communication would require Japanese.

positives.text: Buckwheat (soba) itself is inherently gluten-free, and zaru soba (cold plain buckwheat without tsuyu) could reduce exposure — but staff communication barriers and shared kitchen make this unreliable for celiac diners.

caveats: Hours listed as irregular (不定休) — confirm by phone (079-222-6846) before visiting. Seat count and detailed menu not publicly available. No official website found. Rating is based on a small number of reviews (13 on Tabelog). Dietary safety assessment is based on standard Sarashina soba practices; no direct allergy information was available from the restaurant.

name_ja: ネパールインド料理 ダルバル
cuisine: インド料理・ネパール料理
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市本町68-1153
geocode_note: GSI geocode returned coordinates for 姫路市本町68番地 (block level); unit 1153 not separately resolved. Approximately 530m from the Himeji Castle–Station corridor center — well within 1.15km radius.
neighborhood: 本町・大手前通り（姫路城方面）
seats_text: Seat count not confirmed across any fetched sources.
closed_days: 定休日なし（Tabelog・Note等）、ただしRettyは月曜定休と記載あり。来店前に要確認。
cultural_comfort_note: The restaurant appears to be run by Nepalese and Indian chefs; Japanese is the working language and English is likely limited, but ordering by pointing at the menu and basic Japanese phrases should work fine.
vegan_detail: Vegetable curry (ミックスベジタブルカレー) and dal/lentil curry (ダルマサラ) are on the lunch menu and are likely vegan by default — Indian curry bases here use no dashi or katsuo. However, ghee (clarified butter) is commonly used in Indian cooking and may appear in curry bases or to finish naan; this is not confirmed as absent. Momo (steamed dumplings) wrappers are wheat-based but the filling can be vegetable. Confirm with the kitchen whether any butter or dairy is used in curry prep if strict vegan.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free certification or handling. Rice (白米) and rice-based curry sets are the safest GF choice — rice is served with all set menus. Naan is made from wheat flour in a clay tandoor oven and is not GF. Momo (dumpling) wrappers contain wheat. Pakoda (fritters) use chickpea batter but shared fry oil is likely. Spice mixes used in curry should be wheat-free but cross-contamination from naan flour in the kitchen is unverified. Soy sauce is not a feature of Nepalese-Indian curry cuisine, so shoyu-wheat is not a concern here. Celiacs should ask about shared fryer and kitchen flour handling before ordering.

bio.background: DARBAR is a Nepalese and Indian cuisine restaurant located in the Himekoji building on Otemae-dori, the main boulevard leading from Himeji Station to Himeji Castle. It is run by chefs with origins in Nepal and India. A second location (DARBAR 兼田店) opened in Himeji's Kaneda district in July 2024, suggesting the original Honmachi shop has become established enough to expand.
bio.specialty: Charcoal-baked naan from a large clay tandoor oven — reviewers specifically note the naan as oversized and freshly blistered. Curry sets allow choosing from chicken, mixed vegetable, or lentil dal options.

anecdotes.text: A 2022 reviewer described the interior as 'simple for an Indian-style restaurant, with touches of Japanese design elements in various spots,' finding it an interesting combination.
Another visitor ordered momo (steamed dumplings) and Mix Pakoda, noting the momo resembled 'curry-flavored xiaolongbao' — pointing to the Nepalese influence alongside the Indian menu.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Naan is baked in a clay tandoor that is also used for wheat-flour breads; airborne wheat flour in the kitchen is a realistic concern for celiac diners. No information on separate prep areas was found.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce is not used in Nepalese or Indian curry cooking. No soy sauce wheat risk identified for curry dishes here.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Ghee (clarified butter) is commonly used in Indian restaurant cooking and may appear in curry bases or applied to naan; no source confirms it is absent from the vegetable curries. Ask the kitchen directly.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy menu, allergen chart, or staff allergy-handling protocol was found on any source. Direct inquiry in Japanese is necessary before ordering.

positives.text: Indian and Nepalese curry bases are naturally free of Japanese soy sauce (shoyu), dashi, mirin, and other common Japanese hidden-wheat or hidden-fish ingredients. Rice is available with all set menus as a wheat-free starch alternative to naan.

caveats: Hours conflict across sources: Tabelog and the building directory cite 11:30–21:00 continuous service; an older blog cites 11:00–15:00 / 17:00–22:00 split hours. Closed days also conflict (most say none; Retty says Monday). Verify directly before visiting. The phone number also differs across sources (079-280-8009 vs 079-282-8788) — the Tabelog and Yahoo Maps listing consistently shows 079-280-8009.

address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市魚町55-6 グランファースト魚町 2F
geocode_note: GSI geocode on 兵庫県姫路市魚町５５番地 returned [134.685974, 34.832233]; approximately 0.57 km from target center, well within 1.15 km radius.
neighborhood: 魚町 / 山陽姫路駅
seats_text: Counter 8 seats + 2 tables (8 seats); restaurant caps service at 30 meals per day.
cultural_comfort_note: Counter soba specialist in a small, intimate 10-tsubo space; no English materials confirmed, but the course-based lunch format is navigable by pointing at a menu.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Courses include duck (charcoal-grilled option), tempura (including seafood kakiage), and dashi broth that reviewers describe as freshly made — almost certainly bonito-based (鰹だし) in the traditional style. No plant-based alternatives are mentioned in any source.
gf_detail: Not safe for celiac or gluten-avoiders. The soba is a 9:1 buckwheat-to-wheat flour blend (いちきゅうそば / ichi-ku soba) — wheat is a deliberate ingredient. Tempura is also served, adding further wheat exposure. The kitchen is a shared small space (~33 m²) with no dedicated GF area. No gluten-free claims or allergen accommodations appear in any source.
caveats: Opened June 11, 2025; still a new restaurant with only 7 Tabelog reviews as of research date. Hours confirmed across multiple sources but may evolve. The evening operation (割烹 一意専心) is a separate entity sharing the same premises — this record covers lunch only. Reservation is effectively required given high demand reported one month post-opening.

bio.background: Taniguchi Takuya previously worked at soba restaurants and sought to open independently. He connected with Masato Imanishi (今西政人), owner of the evening kaiseki venture 割烹 一意専心, who proposed a dual-operation model: Imanishi would teach Taniguchi Japanese cooking in exchange for Taniguchi running the daytime soba service in his space.
bio.specialty: Ichi-ku soba — a 90% buckwheat / 10% wheat blend chosen to maximize the grain's natural aroma while keeping a smooth texture; served in multi-course lunches starting with nine small appetizers and tempura before the soba itself.
bio.philosophy: Taniguchi wanted customers to experience soba through fragrance and mouthfeel rather than noodles alone, building a course format that frames the bowl as a destination rather than a standalone dish.

anecdotes.text: The shop uses a reversible sign that physically flips between the daytime soba identity and the nighttime Japanese cuisine identity — a literal two-faced storefront that has become a talking point among Himeji diners.
Despite opening in June 2025, the restaurant was already extremely difficult to book within the first month, with one food blogger noting they 'barely managed a reservation' after trying repeatedly.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen is a shared, compact space (~33 m²) serving wheat-containing soba and tempura; no dedicated GF zone or fryer is mentioned anywhere.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard soy-based tsuyu is used for soba dipping broth. One review notes 'salt and wasabi' as an optional seasoning (塩と山葵で), but ordinary wheat-containing soy sauce is implied as the default.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Freshly made dashi (作りたてのお出汁) is cited by a reviewer — in this traditional context almost certainly bonito (鰹節) based. No plant-based dashi alternative is mentioned.

cuisine: Small Japanese kaiseki-style cuisine, seasonal washoku
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市魚町120 ぶーりんビル 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI AddressSearch on 兵庫県姫路市魚町120. Result: [134.6884, 34.83205]. Distance from target center ~0.36 km, well within 1.15 km radius.
neighborhood: 魚町 (Uocho), Himeji — approx. 7–10 min walk from JR Himeji Station
seats_text: 14 seats: 6 counter, 8 table. Private group bookings up to 20.
cultural_comfort_note: Intimate counter-focused small restaurant where the chef converses actively with guests in Japanese; no English-language accommodations indicated.
vegan_detail: Menu is firmly built around fish, seafood, and meat. Dashi base is almost certainly katsuobushi (bonito) or fish-based given the cuisine style; no plant-based dashi mentioned. Saikyo miso marinade uses Kyoto white miso, which may be vegan in itself, but is applied to fish. Vegetable dishes exist (salads, eggplant dengaku, seasonal clay pot rice) but no vegan labeling or accommodation noted anywhere. Guests would need to inquire in Japanese.
gf_detail: Traditional Japanese cuisine using wheat-containing soy sauce (standard shoyu) throughout marinades, glazes, and dipping sauces. Miso-marinated fish (西京漬け) uses Kyoto white miso — miso itself is usually wheat-free but cross-use with soy sauce is the norm here. Fried dishes (karaage, croquette, oyster fry, tempura) use wheat batter; no dedicated fryer or GF frying oil noted. No GF labeling, no allergen menu published. Not a safe destination for celiac diners without detailed advance coordination in Japanese.

bio.background: Born 1964 in Himeji into a family where parents, relatives, and siblings all worked in the food industry. Initially uncertain of his own path, he eventually followed family tradition into professional cooking. He trained across multiple Japanese culinary disciplines — traditional washoku restaurants, yakitori, and sushi — accumulating over 43 years of professional experience before opening his own creative restaurant in 2000. The current incarnation as 小料理 葉月 emerged through a subsequent relocation and renovation.
bio.specialty: Himeji lotus root dumpling with crab ankake sauce (姫路れんこん饅頭 蟹あんかけ), and A5 Japanese black beef fillet steak. Also known for house-made Saikyo miso-marinated fish using fish the chef catches himself.
bio.philosophy: Deliberately named the restaurant 小料理 (small cuisine) rather than 割烹 (formal kaiseki) to signal an intention to transcend rigid genre boundaries. The chef aims to actively converse with guests and explain ingredients personally, treating each dish — down to garnishes — as handcrafted.

anecdotes.text: The chef makes his own Saikyo miso marinade using Kyoto-sourced white miso and applies it to fish he has caught himself — a detail he shares openly with guests at the counter.
When asked about the name, the chef explained he chose 小料理 over 割烹 precisely because he wanted freedom to move across culinary genres and serve whatever is excellent that season.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fried dishes including karaage, croquette, oyster fry, and tempura are on the menu with no mention of a dedicated fryer or separate oil for GF cooking.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard wheat-containing shoyu is the baseline seasoning in this style of Japanese cuisine; no tamari or GF soy sauce alternatives are mentioned on any published menu or listing.
Saikyo miso marinade (西京漬け) uses Kyoto white miso — likely wheat-free — but is used exclusively on fish, and cross-contact with regular soy sauce during prep cannot be ruled out.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No plant-based dashi is indicated; standard Japanese dashi using katsuobushi (bonito flakes) is the expected stock base for sauces, soups, and braised dishes.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No published allergen menu, GF protocol, or allergy-handling statement found across Tabelog, Hitosara, HotPepper, or Gurunavi listings. Guests with serious dietary restrictions would need to call or inquire in person in Japanese.

positives.text: The chef personally engages with counter guests and explains ingredient sourcing in detail — making direct, in-person communication about dietary needs more feasible than at a large impersonal restaurant.

Vegetable-forward dishes exist on the menu (Hazuki-style vegetable salad with housemade dressing, eggplant dengaku, seasonal clay pot rice with mountain vegetables) that may be adaptable with communication.

caveats: Hours cross-referenced across Tabelog (17:30-22:30, LO 22:00) and HotPepper (17:30-22:30, LO 22:00), consistent. Seat count varies slightly across sources (14 per Tabelog/HotPepper vs 18 per Hitosara); 14 used as it is the more specific and more consistently cited figure. No official standalone website found — Hitosara profile used as best available. No dietary accommodation information published anywhere; all GF/vegan assessments are inferred from menu content and cuisine type.

name_ja: イタリアン食堂 ピザマリア 姫路店
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市南町82
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI AddressSearch on 兵庫県姫路市南町82; result [134.689224, 34.829762] is within target box (lat 34.823–34.843, lng 134.679–134.705), ~450m from center anchor.
neighborhood: 南町・山陽姫路駅近
seats_text: 64席（カウンター席含む）、3F最大30名の貸切パーティールームあり
cultural_comfort_note: Standard local Italian bar with no English menu found; staff comfort with English is unknown, though a simple pizza order environment with a QR ordering system should be manageable.
vegan_detail: The menu is dairy- and meat-heavy throughout. The Marinara pizza (tomato, garlic, oregano, olive oil — no cheese listed, ¥1,100) is the one potentially vegan item, but the kitchen uses cheese and cured meats extensively and no vegan designation exists. Confirm at time of order whether anchovies or lard are in the dough or sauce.
gf_detail: All pizzas use standard wheat-flour Neapolitan dough baked in a shared wood-fired stone oven. All pasta dishes contain wheat. No gluten-free dough alternative is listed anywhere on the menu or any third-party source. Cross-contamination in the shared oven is near-certain. Not safe for celiac diners.
caveats: Closed-day conflict: Tabelog and the official site listed no regular closure; Gnavi (confirmed by direct fetch) lists Thursday as the closed day. Treat Thursday closure as the operative fact but verify before visiting. Seat count varies by source (28 / 38 / 64); 64 from Gnavi is taken as most complete figure including all floors.

bio.background: Opened September 2015 in Minamicho, a short walk from both JR Himeji Station and Sanyo Himeji Station. The restaurant occupies three floors, with a casual bar-style ground floor, intimate table dining on the second floor, and a party room on the third.
bio.specialty: Wood-fired Neapolitan pizza baked at 400–500°C in a Vesuvius-stone oven — roughly 40 varieties including a buffalo-mozzarella upgrade option.
bio.philosophy: Casual Italian shokudo (dining hall) ethos: accessible price points, broad pizza selection, and an all-you-can-drink wine and cocktail menu.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All pizzas share a single wood-fired stone oven; wheat flour from dough will be present on the oven floor throughout service — cross-contamination for anyone avoiding gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy sauce or Japanese dashi-based elements identified in the Italian menu, but standard Italian sauces and dressings have not been confirmed allergen-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Cheese, cured meats, and anchovies are used throughout the kitchen; vegan cross-contact risk is high and no vegan protocols were identified.

name_ja: 花そば・料理 ゆう
cuisine: 手打ち蕎麦・和食 (Hand-made buckwheat soba, Japanese cuisine, oden)
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市西二階町36
geocode_note: GSI AddressSearch on 兵庫県姫路市西二階町３６番地 returned [134.689545, 34.832165] — within target box (lat 34.823–34.843, lng 134.679–134.705) and approximately 0.3 km from the Castle–Station midpoint, well inside the 1.15 km radius.
neighborhood: 西二階町商店街 (Nishi-Nikai-cho Shopping Arcade), near Sanyo-Himeji Station
seats_text: 50 seats total across two floors (roughly 24 downstairs, 20+ upstairs); includes sunken kotatsu seating and sofa seating; private dining available for up to 34 guests
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–14:00 (L.O. 13:30) daily; Dinner 17:30–22:00 (L.O. 21:30) except Thursday evenings closed
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional Japanese soba-ya in a historic shopping arcade; menus and signage primarily in Japanese, though the refined setting and unhurried pace are welcoming — basic Japanese phrases and pointing at the menu will get you far.
vegan_detail: Almost every dish is built around dashi made from bonito (katsuo) and kombu; the house kaeshi (soy tare) is fish-based. The closest vegan-adjacent items are chilled tofu (冷奴), daikon salad, and tofu salad, but cross-contact with fish-based broth throughout the kitchen is very likely. Ask staff specifically; do not assume any soba broth dish is vegan.
gf_detail: Soba noodles are buckwheat but the house tsuyu/dipping sauce uses a wheat-based soy sauce (kaeshi) aged in-house. Tempura batter contains wheat. Virtually all broth dishes contain both dashi and wheat-soy shoyu. No dedicated gluten-free preparation or separate fryer is mentioned anywhere. Celiacs should inquire directly and should treat all soba broth and dipping sauces as containing wheat unless confirmed otherwise.

bio.background: The proprietor is a second-generation master of the Hanshu Shizushizugama ceramic kiln. About 17 years ago he took over a site that had been a traditional Japanese clothing accessories shop for over 100 years and transformed it into a handmade soba restaurant, intending to keep Japanese cultural traditions alive in the historic Nishi-Nikai-cho arcade. He hand-throws most of the ceramic tableware used in the restaurant.
bio.specialty: 手打ち蕎麦 — freshly hand-pulled buckwheat soba made every morning and evening from seasonally rotated flour sourced from Hokkaido, Fukui, and Hitachi; the 千姫御膳 (Senhime Gozen) set is the signature lunch
bio.philosophy: Prioritising aroma and freshness (香りや打ちたてにこだわって); flour is chosen by fragrance, noodles are made twice daily, and the ceramic vessels are made by the same hands that pull the soba.

anecdotes.text: The owner participates in the neighbourhood 西二階町まつり festival and has cooked local Himeji speciality 華姫さわら (Spanish mackerel) live on stage for the community.

The house kaeshi (soy tare base for the dipping sauce) is aged for two months in-house, blended with Rishiri kombu and bonito stock — a labour-intensive process typical of high-end Tokyo soba-ya imported here to provincial Himeji.
The restaurant is listed in the Michelin Guide Hyogo and has received Five Stars Hyogo certification, rare recognition for a soba-ya outside the Kobe/Osaka axis.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free fryer or preparation area is mentioned in any source. Tempura (wheat batter) is a core menu item prepared in the same kitchen as all other dishes.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house kaeshi (soy tare) is made with wheat-containing soy sauce, aged two months in-house, and used as the base for all dipping sauces and many broth dishes.
Virtually all soba broth and dipping sauces on the Tabelog menu listing explicitly contain dashi and soy sauce; wheat-free alternatives are not mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dashi made from bonito (katsuo) and kombu is used across the entire menu including hot soups, noodle broths, oden, ankake sauces, and side dishes. Even tofu preparations (agedashi tofu, hot tofu) are served with dashi-based sauce.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, allergy-handling policy, or staff training for dietary restrictions is mentioned on the official website or any third-party review source reviewed. Phone reservation line is available (079-285-0888) and is the recommended channel for dietary queries.

positives.text: Soba noodles themselves are made from buckwheat flour — the base noodle is gluten-free grain — so the wheat risk is in sauces and accompaniments, not the noodle itself.
The chilled plain soba (もりそば) served with dipping sauce on the side allows some control over sauce exposure, though the sauce still contains wheat-soy kaeshi.

caveats: Hours confirmed from Tabelog (as of research date); Thursday dinner closure is the only confirmed closure. Payment: cards accepted (VISA/MC/JCB/AMEX/Diners) plus IC/QR. GF and vegan safety assessment is based on menu ingredient analysis only — no direct staff inquiry was made. Celiacs and strict vegans must call ahead (079-285-0888). Tabelog URL in the task prompt (R8523/rstLst/RC0104) appears to be a search/list URL rather than a direct restaurant page; the canonical single-restaurant Tabelog URL is 28021337 as used here.

name_ja: 英洋軒 姫路駅前店
cuisine: 餃子・中華料理（立ち飲み）
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市駅前町301
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 兵庫県姫路市駅前町３０１番地 returned [134.692093, 34.829098]. Approximately 0.45 km from target center (34.8331, 134.6921) — well within 1.15 km radius.
neighborhood: 姫路駅前・共和商店街
seats_text: カウンター立ち飲みとテーブル2卓で約12席
cultural_comfort_note: Compact standing bar with verbal ordering and handwritten bills — no English menu or English-speaking staff noted; one TripAdvisor reviewer flagged a staff interaction issue, so non-Japanese speakers should be prepared for a fully Japanese-language environment.
vegan_detail: The entire menu revolves around pork-filled gyoza, karaage (fried chicken), and egg omelette. No vegetarian or vegan dishes have been documented. Banshu (播州) soy sauce is listed as a key flavoring agent, and all signature items contain meat or egg. Vegan diners have nothing to eat here.
gf_detail: Gyoza wrappers contain wheat flour. Karaage (fried chicken) uses wheat-based batter. Banshu soy sauce (播州しょうゆ) is standard wheat-containing shoyu. No dedicated fryer, no gluten-free alternatives, and no allergen information is published. Cross-contamination risk is high in a small open kitchen. Not safe for celiac diners.

bio.background: 英洋軒の創業は1982年（昭和57年）、姫路市余部の本店が起源。駅前店は2012年10月に創業者の夫が開いた支店で、現在は創業家による家族経営のもと姫路市内に4店舗を展開する。共和商店街の一角に構える屋台のような立ち飲みスタイルが特徴。
bio.specialty: 焼き餃子（Tabelog百名店選出）と手焼きオムレツ
bio.philosophy: 安くてうまい、気軽に立ち寄れる街の中華立ち飲み屋として地元に根付いてきた。
roles: 家族経営（創業者一族が各店舗を分担）

anecdotes.text: 餃子はTabelog Dumplings「百名店」に2019・2021・2024年と3度選出されており、姫路を代表する餃子の名店として全国的な知名度を持つ。
2017年に訪れた東京餃子通信の記者は「きれいな焼き色が付いた餃子で、ガツンとしたニンニク風味ではなく上品な味わい」と評している。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Small open kitchen with no separation between gyoza (wheat wrappers) and other items; cross-contamination must be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Banshu soy sauce (播州しょうゆ) is explicitly cited as a core seasoning — this is standard wheat-containing Japanese soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All dishes involve pork (gyoza filling), chicken (karaage), or egg (omelette); no plant-only cooking surfaces or ingredients documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen information is published and no allergy-handling procedures are documented. Staff communication is entirely in Japanese.

caveats: Hours sourced from Tabelog (Mon/Wed-Sun 11:30-21:00, Tues closed) — confirm directly as standing-bar hours can shift seasonally. No official website found. Founding year discrepancy: one source says 1982 (Showa 57), another says Showa 57 = 1982, and blog references confirm 1982 as the Amabe main-branch founding year, with the station branch opening in October 2012.

address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市魚町126
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 兵庫県姫路市魚町１２６番地 returned [134.6884, 34.83205]. Approx 350m from target center — well within 1.15km radius.
neighborhood: 魚町 (Uomachi) — Sanyo-Himeji Station area
seats_text: 23 seats: 6 table seats and 17 sunken kotatsu-style (horigotatsu) seats
hours_raw: 月～土 11:30～14:00（L.O.13:30）、17:00～20:00（L.O.19:30）／日曜・祝日定休。7月はうなぎ夏季メニューのみ、7～8月はランチセット休止あり。
closed_days: 日曜日・祝日・年末年始（12/31〜1/3）
cultural_comfort_note: An English menu is available and staff have been described as friendly and accommodating to foreign visitors, though the restaurant is conducted in Japanese.
vegan_detail: This is a dedicated eel and oyster restaurant. All signature dishes are animal-based; no plant-based mains or sides are listed. Dashi is fish-based (katsuobushi standard for Japanese restaurants of this type). There are no vegan options.
gf_detail: The restaurant's core product is unagi no kabayaki, which relies on a house tare sauce containing wheat-based soy sauce (shoyu) that has been continuously replenished since the founding in 1848. Standard Japanese wheat shoyu is used throughout Japanese eel cuisine. No gluten-free alternatives, dedicated fryer, or allergen protocols are documented anywhere. The restaurant has never advertised GF options and no review or source mentions any accommodation. Not safe for celiac diners.

bio.background: Founded in Kaei 1 (1848) by a man known as Jugesuke, who learned the eel trade in Edo's Fukagawa district — then the center of Tokyo's unagi culture — before returning west to open in Himeji under the patronage of clan officials. The restaurant has operated continuously for over 175 years across seven or more generations, making it one of the oldest dining establishments in Hyogo Prefecture.
bio.specialty: Unagi no kabayaki prepared in the Kansai style: grilled directly over charcoal without pre-steaming, producing a crispy, caramelized exterior and a soft interior. The house tare (glaze) has been continuously replenished — '継ぎ足し' — since the restaurant's founding, giving it extraordinary depth. Winter brings seasonal oyster dishes (late October to late March).
bio.philosophy: Faithfulness to Edo-period technique and an unbroken house sauce are the throughlines. The restaurant has resisted modernizing its core method across generations.
bio.origin: Himeji, Hyogo, Japan — originally near Sakamoto-cho, now at Uomachi 126

anecdotes.text: When Emperor Meiji visited Himeji, Moriju was designated an official imperial kitchen supplier and prepared eel for the imperial party — a distinction the family still references.
The restaurant's Meiji-era customer ledger reportedly includes the name of Sakamoto Ryoma, the celebrated Tosa samurai and modernizer.
Moriju was awarded a Bib Gourmand in the Michelin Guide Hyogo 2016, recognizing quality at a reasonable price point.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information available on cross-contamination controls. The kitchen handles wheat-based soy sauce throughout all cooking.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature tare (glaze) used on all eel dishes is wheat-based soy sauce that has been continuously replenished since 1848. Wheat soy sauce is inherent to the product; no tamari or GF alternative is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All dishes are animal-based (eel, oyster, fish dashi). There is no plant-based cooking track and therefore unavoidable animal cross-contact throughout.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling protocols or allergen menus are documented on any source including the official website, Tabelog, or Gnavi listings.

caveats: Hours are irregular: summer (July-August) switches to a limited "summer menu" with no lunch sets; winter oyster dishes run late October to late March only. Closed days include Sundays, all public holidays, and year-end/New Year (Dec 31-Jan 3). The Michelin Bib Gourmand listing is from 2016 (Hyogo edition) and current status in the guide was not confirmed. Seat count varies slightly between sources (23 per Tabelog English, 30 per Gnavi); 23 used as primary Tabelog figure.

address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市塩町152 白欧館 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned 34.831951, 134.685867 for 兵庫県姫路市塩町１５２番地 — approximately 0.58 km from target center, well within 1.15 km.
neighborhood: 塩町 (Shiocho), Sanyo-Himeji Station area
seats_text: 6 counter seats plus 3 tables seating approximately 16
cultural_comfort_note: A small, late-night counter izakaya run by a solo owner with no English menu; basic Japanese will help, but regulars are welcomed warmly at the counter.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu exists. Oden broth and most sauces rely on katsuobushi (bonito) dashi. Sashimi and some obanzai (daily small plates) may be animal-product-free in ingredients, but hidden fish stock is nearly certain across hot dishes. Ask the owner before ordering.
gf_detail: Handmade soba is the signature dish, but no information on wheat ratio (二八 vs 十割) has been confirmed — standard izakaya soba often contains wheat. Wheat-based soy sauce is used throughout the kitchen (marinades, dipping sauces, oden). Fried items (e.g. chikuwa isobe-age) are on the menu with no dedicated GF fryer mentioned. No allergen menu or GF designation exists. Celiac-unsafe without direct confirmation from the owner.

bio.background: Saito Eriko opened 五臓六腑 on May 9, 2024, realizing a long-held ambition to run an establishment dedicated to fine Japanese sake and handmade soba. The 14-tsubo room on the ground floor of the Hakuokan building in Shiocho sits in Himeji's late-night entertainment district, where Saito noted that fewer places had kept late hours after the pandemic — a gap she set out to fill.
bio.specialty: 〆蕎麦 (finishing soba): hand-cut noodles whose flour origin rotates seasonally, served as a set (¥1,610) to close out an evening of sake drinking.
bio.philosophy: Centered on quality nihonshu — 150 labels including sought-after bottles like Juyondai and Niimata — paired with honest izakaya cooking: daily obanzai small plates, fresh sashimi, oden, and soba to finish.

anecdotes.text: Saito positions herself as helping restore the vitality of Himeji's after-hours dining scene: 'After the pandemic, fewer establishments maintained late hours,' she said at opening, pointing to the 6 PM – 3 AM schedule as a deliberate commitment to night-owl diners.
A soba-making station is visible from the entrance, making the hand-cutting process part of the ambiance rather than hidden in a back kitchen.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fried items (chikuwa isobe-age) are listed on the menu with no mention of a dedicated fryer or GF protocols. Cross-contamination with wheat via shared frying oil is likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No allergen menu exists. Standard wheat-containing soy sauce is expected throughout izakaya cooking — in sauces, dipping broths, marinades, and oden tare.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Oden broth and most cooked dishes at a traditional izakaya rely on katsuobushi (bonito flake) dashi. No plant-based broth option is documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy menu, staff allergy training, or special dietary accommodation policy has been documented for this restaurant. The owner-operated format means direct conversation with Saito Eriko at the counter is the only route to clarifying ingredients.

positives.text: Counter seating directly in front of the owner allows for direct, personal conversation about ingredients — easier than at a larger izakaya chain.
Sashimi (fresh fish and local chicken tataki) are naturally wheat-free in their base form, though dipping sauces (ponzu, soy) typically contain wheat.

caveats: Hours (18:00-03:00 Mon-Sat, closed Sun) confirmed via Tabelog and Himeji Keizai Shimbun (May 2024 opening article). No cash-only confirmation found — payment method unconfirmed. Official website not confirmed; Instagram handle @unkno_an2021 was noted on Tabelog but could not be independently verified as active. No GF or vegan dietary information is available from any source; all dietary assessments are inferences from standard izakaya practices.

name_ja: 小さな町のなかのレストラン bistro
name_en: Chiisana Machi no Naka no Restaurant Bistro
cuisine: ビストロ・イタリアン（フレンチ・イタリアン混合）
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市立町58 アルテミスビル3F
geocode_note: GSI AddressSearch matched 兵庫県姫路市立町５８番地 → [134.689621, 34.831341]. Approximately 300m southwest of the Himeji Castle–Station corridor center; well within the 1.15km radius.
neighborhood: 立町（山陽姫路駅北）
vegan_detail: No vegan options identified. The menu centers on charcuterie (prosciutto, pâté, rillettes), meat mains, pasta with egg-based or cream sauces, and cheese. No plant-based or vegan labeling found on any source.
gf_detail: No gluten-free options or allergen accommodation noted anywhere. Pasta is a centerpiece of the menu; bread (focaccia, baguette), and flour-heavy dishes are standard. No dedicated fryer information, no GF soy sauce, no staff allergen protocols mentioned. Wheat is pervasive throughout the menu. Not suitable for celiac diners without direct prior contact with the restaurant.
hours_raw: ディナー 火〜日・祝 17:30〜23:00（料理LO 22:00、ドリンクLO 22:30）。カフェ営業（2025年2月〜）月・水・木・金 12:30〜16:00。定休日：月曜日（ディナー）
closed_days: 月曜日（ディナー定休）。カフェ営業は火・土・日が定休
seats_text: 26席（カウンター6席、4人テーブル×4、個室4席）。最大着席20名・立食30名
cultural_comfort_note: Japanese-language menus and interface; no English menu or staff language explicitly noted, though the cuisine style (Italian/French bistro) and a la carte ordering are internationally familiar formats.

bio.background: オーナーシェフは東京のイタリアンレストランで長年修業を積んだ後、故郷の播磨地域に戻り、2023年4月12日に立町のアルテミスビル3階でこのビストロをオープンした。ビストロ形式を選んだのは、習得した料理の腕を活かしながら自分のペースで働けるスタイルを求めてのことで、「気取らず本当に美味しいものを楽しむ」という哲学を体現している。
bio.specialty: 焼きたてフィナンシェ（6個 ¥1,500、オーダーから15〜30分）と「貧乏人のパスタ 白トリュフの香り」（カチョエペペ風、¥1,580）
bio.philosophy: 手が届く価格で本格的な料理を。シェフとゲストの距離感を大切にした親密なダイニング体験を提供する。ワインは100種以上を揃え、ソムリエ的なセレクションが可能。

anecdotes.text: オーナーはフィナンシェのレシピを徹底的に研究し納得のいく配合にたどり着いた。注文を受けてから焼き上げる焼きたてのフィナンシェは、カフェ営業開始後も看板商品となっている。
2025年2月からカフェ営業（月・水〜金 12:30〜16:00）をスタート。ディナー専業だった隠れ家ビストロが昼間も開くようになり、スイーツやカフェ限定ドリンクを楽しめるようになった。

caveats: カフェ営業時間（月・水〜金 12:30〜16:00）は2025年2月開始の新設。Tabelogの掲載時間はディナーのみのため、hours_weekは複数ソースを統合して構成。グルテンフリー・ヴィーガン対応の記載はいずれのソースにも存在せず、celiac/vegan渡航者には不適。

cuisine: プラントベース・マクロビオティック
address_ja: 〒670-0923 兵庫県姫路市呉服町18
geocode_note: GSI AddressSearch on 兵庫県姫路市呉服町18 returned [134.693665, 34.830647]. Within target box (lat 34.823–34.843, lng 134.679–134.705). Approx 0.3 km from target center.
neighborhood: 呉服町（姫路城付近・山陽姫路駅から徒歩6–7分）
vegan_detail: 100% plant-based across the entire menu. No meat, fish, eggs, dairy, or honey used in any dish or sweet. Macrobiotic philosophy underpins the cooking. Brown rice or 5-grain rice served with set meals. Soy milk used for puddings and gelato. Menu rotates monthly (lunch) and seasonally (sweets). Natural food shop on the same premises stocks the same organic ingredients used in the kitchen.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free dedicated. Wheat is present: documented menu items include お麩のフライ (fried wheat gluten), organic carrot cake made with whole wheat flour, and vegan pancakes whose flour base is unspecified. Macrobiotic cooking routinely uses wheat-based products (fu, seitan, whole wheat). No GF certification or GF-specific menu found across any source. Cross-contamination risk is unquantified. Celiacs should contact the restaurant directly before visiting.
hours_raw: Shop 10:30–18:30; Café 11:30–18:30 (last order 17:30, lunch last order 15:30). Closed Mondays and the 3rd Sunday of each month.
closed_days: 毎週月曜日・毎月第3日曜日
seats_text: Counter and table seating across two floors; exact count not published
cultural_comfort_note: English menus are available; the owner specifically set out to serve international visitors coming to nearby Himeji Castle.
caveats: Sunday hours: the official site specifies closure only on the 3rd Sunday of each month; Tabelog lists all Sundays as closed — verify directly. Lunch last order is 15:30; the café itself stays open until 18:30 for sweets and drinks. Hours shown above reflect café opening (11:30); the shop opens at 10:30.

bio.background: The business traces its roots to 1972, when the current owner's family opened a natural foods store in Himeji's Gofukucho shopping district. The present operator — the shop's third-generation owner and a qualified macrobiotic cooking instructor — relaunched the space as a plant-based café called il riccio in November 2019, grafting a dining room onto the existing retail operation.
bio.specialty: Seasonal vegan Japanese-style set lunch (季節のランチ): a teishoku of rice (brown or 5-grain), miso soup, a main dish, and two or three sides, priced around ¥1,650–¥1,980. The main rotates monthly — past examples include chickpea curry croquette and soy-meat dishes.
bio.philosophy: Macrobiotic principles guide every dish: organic, naturally farmed, or low-pesticide vegetables; seasonings free of synthetic additives. The owner's stated aim is to let visitors to Himeji Castle experience Japanese home-style vegan cuisine rather than defaulting to sandwiches or foreign food. The name 'il riccio' is Italian for hedgehog — a hedgehog figure marks the storefront.

anecdotes.text: The café grew directly out of the owner's macrobiotic cooking classes: students who attended the classes encouraged her to open a place where they could eat the food she taught. She recognised that Himeji, a major UNESCO World Heritage destination, had almost no vegan-friendly Japanese dining, and set out to fill that gap.

The interior features a handmade chandelier crafted from vintage cutlery, and the café uses grass straws for beverages — small details that reflect the broader commitment to low-impact, considered living.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information about dedicated fryers or separation of gluten-containing and gluten-free preparation is available from any source reviewed. The kitchen uses wheat-containing ingredients (wheat gluten, whole wheat flour) alongside rice-based and vegetable dishes.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce use is not documented in any source, but it is common in Japanese macrobiotic cooking. The restaurant avoids synthetic additives but makes no claim of tamari or wheat-free soy sauce. Assume wheat-based shoyu may be in use unless confirmed otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is 100% plant-based with no animal products; vegan cross-contact from kitchen operations is not a concern here.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No formal allergy protocol is described in any source. For celiac or wheat-allergy guests, direct contact (079-288-2439 or Instagram @ilriccio.himeji.plantbasedcafe) is strongly advised before visiting.

positives.text: Entire menu is 100% free of all animal products — no hidden dashi, no fish stock, no dairy, no eggs, no honey — making it one of the most reliably vegan spots in Himeji.
Ingredients are sourced organically or from low-pesticide natural farming; seasonings contain no synthetic additives. The attached shop stocks all the same products used in the kitchen.

name_ja: ローマピッツァと窯焼きイタリアン 姫路バル
name_en: Roma Pizza to Kamayaki Italian Himeji Baru
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市紺屋町78 伊藤ビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 兵庫県姫路市紺屋町78番地 → 34.830376, 134.693542. Approx 330m from target center (34.8331, 134.6921), well within 1.15km radius.
neighborhood: 紺屋町・おみぞ筋商店街 (Sanyo Himeji Station area)
seats_text: 19 seats total: 7 counter, 12 table; up to 20 standing for events
cultural_comfort_note: A casual Italian wine bar in a local shopping arcade; no English menu confirmed, but the casual bar format and simple pizza-focused menu make ordering manageable with basic Japanese or pointing.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or plant-based options listed anywhere. Standard Italian bar fare — pizzas contain cheese and meat toppings by default; course menus include ham, shrimp, Caesar salad (likely anchovies/dairy). A plain margherita may be possible without cheese if requested, but nothing is confirmed. Hidden animal ingredients (dairy, meat) are the norm here. Contact before visiting.
gf_detail: Pizza is the core product — dough is made in-house with custom yeast, but wheat flour is the base. No gluten-free dough option is advertised or confirmed anywhere. Stone oven is shared with all pizzas, so cross-contamination from flour is near-certain. No dedicated fryer information found. Italian wine bar context: pasta and other wheat-based dishes likely present. Not safe for celiac without direct confirmation from the restaurant.

bio.background: 姫路バル is one of the shops within Himeji's Omizo-suji shopping arcade (おみぞ筋商店街), operated by 株式会社otto. The company runs multiple dining concepts under the otto brand. The restaurant opened as a Roman-style pizza and wine bar concept, positioning itself as a neighbourhood Italian casual spot in the Sanyo Himeji station area.
bio.specialty: Roman-style (thin, crispy) stone-oven pizza — dough fermented in-house with a proprietary yeast blend and adjusted rise times for a distinctively crispy base. Individual pizzas start at ¥500 in-house.
bio.philosophy: Affordable, handcrafted Roman pizza paired with a sommelier-curated wine list spanning over 50 labels from around the world, aimed at making wine and quality pizza an everyday pleasure in Himeji.

anecdotes.text: The pizza dough is made entirely by hand, using the restaurant's own yeast culture and adjusted fermentation timing to produce the signature thin, crispy Roman-style crust baked in an authentic stone oven.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Pizza is the core product and all dough is wheat-based. A stone oven shared across all pizzas means ambient flour and gluten contact is unavoidable. No gluten-free crust option has been found.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Standard Italian bar menu — cheese, meat, and seafood are central to the menu. No vegan-safe section or plant-based alternatives confirmed.

caveats: Cash-only per Tabelog (HotPepper lists cards accepted — discrepancy; confirm before visiting). All-smoking premises. Guests under 20 not admitted. No gluten-free or vegan information exists anywhere online — this place is not suitable for celiac diners and is a poor fit for vegans without direct contact. Independent field note: operated by 株式会社otto (multi-unit company), so marked independent=false.

name_ja: ビストロ料理とワインのお店 NicolaS
name_en: Bistro Ryori to Wine no Mise NicolaS (Nicolas)
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市駅前町222 姫路駅前第一ビル B2F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI AddressSearch on 兵庫県姫路市駅前町222; coordinates fall within the target box (lat 34.823–34.843, lng 134.679–134.705).
neighborhood: 駅前町 (Ekimae-cho) — Himeji Station Front
seats_text: 9 seats total: 6 counter seats and 1 table; max party 10; private use possible up to 20
hours_raw: Tue–Thu 17:30–23:00 (L.O. 22:00); Fri–Sun & holidays lunch 11:30–15:00 (L.O. 14:00), dinner 17:30–23:00 (L.O. 22:00); closed Monday
cultural_comfort_note: Intimate 9-seat basement hideaway run solo by the owner-chef; English signage is unlikely, but the counter format invites natural interaction if you can manage basic Japanese.
vegan_detail: The menu is built around meat, seafood, house-cured charcuterie, cheese, and butter-based French-Italian preparations. No vegan options are advertised or noted in any source. Hidden animal products are pervasive (butter, cream, stock, cured meats). Vegans should contact the chef directly before visiting — given the small-kitchen, solo-chef format, custom accommodations may be difficult.
gf_detail: This is a classic bistro. House-made bread (focaccia and French bread), pasta (tagliatelle, lasagna), quiche, and wheat-dusted sauces are core menu items. No gluten-free options are advertised anywhere. There is no dedicated fryer information, no wheat-free soy sauce, and no cross-contamination protocol documented. Diners with celiac disease should contact the restaurant before visiting — wheat is fundamental to the menu and the kitchen's prep style.

bio.background: The owner-chef, known by the surname Shinoda, ran an earlier bistro of the same name in Himeji's Nibancho district roughly twenty years before reopening. After leaving Himeji he worked as a head chef at a French restaurant in the Kanto region and was involved in food-product development at a manufacturer. He returned to his hometown determined to cook again, and opened this iteration of NicolaS on December 12, 2022 in the basement of Himeji Ekimae Dai-ichi Building.
bio.specialty: Roast Australian lamb; duck thigh confit (carried over from the original restaurant); pâté de campagne; house-made focaccia and French bread
bio.philosophy: 丁寧に作る — meticulous preparation as a guiding principle. The chef blends French and Italian elements rather than adhering strictly to one tradition, and frames the restaurant as a casual wine-and-small-plates destination.

anecdotes.text: The lamb roast draws repeat visitors specifically to reorder it — one blogger noted returning 'to eat the lamb again' as the driving motivation.

The chef engaged a solo diner in detailed conversation about the differences between French and Italian culinary technique during service, illustrating his passion for explaining the craft behind each dish.
When explaining his pâté de campagne, the chef noted that 'the size of the meat cuts and the cooking temperature differ with each establishment' — a detail that signals his attention to technique.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on cross-contamination protocols is documented in any source. The kitchen is run solo by one chef in a compact basement space, and house-made bread is prepared on-site, raising surface/utensil contamination concerns for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No source mentions wheat-free soy sauce or any soy sauce substitution. French-Italian bistro cooking typically uses wheat-containing sauces, pasta, and bread — no gluten-free accommodations are documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Animal products (butter, cream, lard, stock, cured meats) are core to every menu category; vegan cross-contact risk is high and no plant-only prep area or labeling is mentioned.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No reviewer or source mentions allergy awareness, allergen menus, or staff training. Diners with serious allergies should phone ahead (079-244-9082) and discuss needs directly with the chef.

positives.text: The restaurant is run by a single owner-chef who cooks to order in a small kitchen — this one-cook format means custom requests can be discussed directly with the decision-maker, rather than relying on front-of-house staff to relay information.

caveats: The target brief described the restaurant as "2nd floor" but all sources including Tabelog confirm it is B2F (basement 2nd floor). No official website exists. The chef's given name was not confirmed in any fetched source — only the surname Shinoda (篠田) appeared in secondary reporting. Hours shown reflect multiple consistent sources but the restaurant opened in late 2022 so should be verified before visiting.

address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市二階町18 ANGELO二階町 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI AddressSearch for 兵庫県姫路市二階町18; result 34.831291, 134.693832 — approximately 255m from the Castle-Station corridor center, well within the 1.15km target radius.
neighborhood: 二階町商店街 (Nikaichō Shopping Street), Himeji
seats_text: Seating capacity not published; Tabelog notes counter and table seating available.
closed_days: 月・火・水・木・金・日（土曜のみ営業）
cultural_comfort_note: A newly opened, youth-staffed neighborhood café with no English signage noted; staff described as energetic and friendly, but no English-language service confirmed.
vegan_detail: No vegan labeling on the menu. Specialty drinks include lattes and a matcha affogato (likely contains milk). No dairy-free or plant-milk option is documented in any source. Ask about milk alternatives before ordering.
gf_detail: Primarily a drinks-and-sweets café. No gluten-free labeling, dedicated fryer information, or allergy-handling policy has been published anywhere. Sweets may contain wheat. Cross-contamination risk is unknown. Ask staff directly before ordering any food item.
caveats: Opened June 27, 2026 — extremely new; only 3 Tabelog reviews at time of research. Saturday-only hours (13:00–22:00) are unusual and should be confirmed via Instagram (@hey.__cafe) before visiting. Phone number is not yet listed. All dietary and payment details are unconfirmed.

bio.background: Hey. opened on June 27, 2026 inside the ANGELO二階町 building on Nikaichō Shopping Street, about 464m from Sanyo-Himeji Station. It operates on Saturdays only, positioning itself as a relaxed evening destination in the covered arcade near Himeji Castle.
bio.specialty: MELT LATTE — a creamy espresso-based latte described by early visitors as the signature drink, alongside a cheese latte and matcha affogato.
bio.philosophy: A calm, unhurried café atmosphere described by early visitors as a hidden-gem retreat within a busy shopping street.

anecdotes.text: An early reviewer noted that the cheese latte's richness paired excellently with the coffee flavor, and praised the young staff as energetic, bright, and courteous.

name_ja: 大衆酒場 フレンチマン 姫路店
cuisine: カジュアルフレンチ居酒屋
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市駅前町188-1 姫路フェスタ地下1階 グランフェスタ2番街
geocode_note: Geocoded to 兵庫県姫路市駅前町１８８番地 via GSI address search; coordinates are within the target lat/lng box and approximately 620m from the Himeji Castle–Station midpoint.
neighborhood: 姫路駅前 グランフェスタ地下街
seats_text: 16席カウンター（コの字型）＋10席テーブル、計26席
cultural_comfort_note: カウンター中心の活気ある居酒屋スタイルで、英語メニューや英語対応スタッフの情報はなし。日本語のみで対応する可能性が高い。
vegan_detail: メニューはフォアグラ・ローストビーフ・ウニ・イクラなど動物性食材が中心。野菜小皿料理も一部あるが、フレンチのソースやバターに動物性素材が含まれる可能性が高い。ヴィーガン対応の明記なし。注文時に直接確認が必要。
gf_detail: フレンチ居酒屋のためパスタ・サンドイッチ・ソースに小麦が広く使用される。醤油使用の有無は不明だが、フレンチベースのため醤油より小麦粉（ルー・パスタ生地・パン粉）のリスクが高い。グルテンフリー対応の明記なし。専用フライヤーの有無も不明。セリアック病の方は来店前に電話確認（079-280-4555）を強く推奨。

bio.background: 有限会社らくちん（京都市中京区）が運営。代表の渡辺哲也氏は26歳で独立し、京都で20年以上居酒屋経営を経験。約20業態を試みた末、「フレンチはまだ大衆化されていない」という市場の空白を見つけ、フレンチ×大衆酒場という新ジャンルを開拓。総料理長・吉丸清孝氏がメニューを考案。2020年に新大阪駅構内から「大衆酒場 フレンチマン」業態をスタートし、姫路店は2025年3月14日に兵庫県で神戸三宮に次ぐ2店舗目としてオープン。
bio.origin: 京都発祥。2015年JR京都駅近くに「ビストロ フレンチマン」を開業し、2020年新大阪店から大衆酒場業態に発展。
bio.specialty: フォアグラとローストビーフ（¥2,178）、ウニとイクラの冷製パスタ（¥2,728）、ホールチーズクリームパスタ（¥880）
