vegan_detail: Tabelog lists vegan/vegetarian/halal options as available, but this is a platform checkbox with no corroborating menu or review evidence. Standard kaiseki and sushi formats rely heavily on bonito dashi (katsuo), soy sauce, mirin, and fish roe — none inherently vegan. Any accommodation would need to be pre-arranged directly with the restaurant.
gf_detail: No gluten-free certification or dedicated GF menu found. Traditional soy sauce (contains wheat) is standard in Japanese kaiseki and sushi preparations, including dipping sauce, marinades, and seasoned rice vinegar. Reviews mention dashi made from "5 types of natural kombu and bonito," which is GF, but wheat-bearing soy sauce appears in most courses. No allergen menu or documented accommodation protocol found. Celiacs should contact the restaurant in advance.

bio.background: Opened May 21, 2024 in the Hanamura Building in Uomachi, Himeji's historic fish-market district, a short walk from Sanyo Himeji Station. The space is fitted entirely in hinoki cypress wood with an open counter kitchen designed so diners watch every step of preparation.
bio.specialty: Seasonal omakase courses built around Setouchi seafood, with a lunch sushi service (鮨 未十) and an evening kaiseki program — late night shifts to à la carte including creative dishes such as a Mijuu-style Korean-seasoned crab and a Japanese-inflected Peking duck.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen focuses on drawing out the natural umami of each ingredient — sourcing fish from Nagasaki, condiments from Kyoto — and pairs roughly 20 rotating local sake varieties at a fixed price alongside the meal.

anecdotes.text: A blog visit documented the signature counter atmosphere: red-vinegar shari (rice seasoned with aged red vinegar) crafted individually for each piece, with sea urchin served hand-to-mouth.
Tabelog reviewers note the kitchen opens with a layered dashi made from five types of natural kombu and bonito, anchoring each meal's umami base.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard wheat-containing soy sauce is integral to Japanese kaiseki and sushi — used in dipping sauce, shari seasoning, and course marinades. No wheat-free soy sauce alternative (tamari/GF shoyu) is documented for this restaurant.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No allergen menu, dedicated GF prep area, or cross-contamination protocols were found across Tabelog, HotPepper, or any review sources.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bonito (katsuo) dashi is a core building block of the menu per diner reviews; all courses likely contain fish-based stock unless a special accommodation is arranged in advance.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No documented allergy-handling protocol or staff allergy training was found. The restaurant's online reservation notes ask guests to avoid strong fragrances, but no dietary restriction pre-screening process is mentioned.

positives.text: Dashi base is made from natural kombu and bonito — kombu portions are inherently gluten-free and iodine-rich; advance notice to the kitchen may allow adjustment of fish-stock components.

caveats: Opened May 2024 — relatively new with a limited review footprint. Hours and course offerings may evolve; verify before visiting. Vegan/halal checkboxes on Tabelog are self-reported and unverified by any specific review or menu documentation. No English-language in-person service confirmed.

address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市西駅前町51-5
geocode_note: GSI geocode returned coordinates for 姫路市西駅前町51番地; within target box (lat 34.823–34.843, lng 134.679–134.705).
neighborhood: 西駅前町 (Sanyo-Himeji Station area)
seats_text: Counter seats only; exact number not published. Very small intimate space under the Sanyo railway overpass.
hours_raw: Lunch only (exact times not published in any confirmed source); evening bar operation. Closed Mondays confirmed via Yahoo Maps.
cultural_comfort_note: Tiny counter-only local shop with no detected English presence; expect Japanese-only menus and staff interaction.
vegan_detail: The soba noodles themselves are 100% buckwheat and vegan, but soba tsuyu (dipping broth) at Japanese soba restaurants almost always contains dashi made from katsuobushi (bonito flakes), which is not vegan. No vegan-specific options or dashi alternatives are mentioned in any source. Ask the kitchen whether a plant-based tsuyu is possible.
gf_detail: The noodles are declared 100% buckwheat (十割そば) with no wheat flour added — a positive signal. However, soba tsuyu typically uses koikuchi soy sauce which contains wheat, and no allergen management protocols or dedicated GF claims are documented for this shop. Cross-contamination risk is unknown. Celiac diners should confirm tsuyu ingredients and kitchen practices directly before ordering.

bio.background: Opened April 2022 under the Sanyo Electric Railway overpass in the Nishi-Ekimae-cho district, a short walk from Sanyo-Himeji Station. The shop operates as a soba counter at lunch and shifts to a bar in the evening.
bio.specialty: Juwari soba — noodles made to order from 100% domestic buckwheat flour, kneaded and extruded by machine right in front of the customer, with strong chewy texture. Cold zaru soba with optional toppings (including plum-grated yam) is the signature.
bio.philosophy: Make-to-order from raw flour: customers watch their noodles born from buckwheat powder at the counter. This results in longer waits but fresher noodles.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers note that because noodles are made from scratch using a machine after each order, the wait is considerable — those without time flexibility are advised to plan accordingly.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No source confirms what soy sauce brand or style is used in the tsuyu; standard koikuchi soy sauce contains wheat. Celiac diners must ask.

positives.text: Noodles are stated to be 100% buckwheat (十割) with no wheat flour blended in, which is a prerequisite for any gluten-free consideration.

caveats: Exact lunch hours were not published in any fetched source — only "lunch only" is confirmed. Call ahead (079-283-2510) to confirm current hours before visiting. Cash only confirmed via Tabelog; PayPay may also be accepted (Yahoo Maps mention, unconfirmed). Seat count not published. No allergen documentation exists for this shop; all GF/vegan assessments are based on general soba-restaurant norms, not shop-specific confirmation.

cuisine: シェアカフェ・甘味処（古民家・野菜系）
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市八代本町1-10-23
geocode_note: GSI geocode first-hit coordinates. Lat 34.843327 sits at the very northern edge of the target box (upper bound 34.843) but is approximately 1.14km from the castle–station midpoint, just within the 1.15km radius. Coordinates confirmed plausible for Yashiro Honmachi 1-chome.
neighborhood: 八代本町（清水橋バス停徒歩5分・姫路城北側）
seats_text: 4人テーブル×1、2人テーブル×3、カウンター4席
cultural_comfort_note: English signage is not confirmed; communication likely in Japanese, though the rotating share-cafe format with multiple small vendors may vary by operator.
vegan_detail: The cafe emphasizes vegetable-forward, healthy cuisine. The anchor tenant obann (オバン) serves obanzai-style Japanese home cooking centered on organic vegetables. Individual vendors rotating through the space vary — some offer bibimbap, Dutch baby pancakes, and somen noodle dishes. Hidden dashi (fish stock) is common in obanzai and Japanese home cooking and is not ruled out here; no vendor has explicitly advertised vegan or dashi-free options. Somen noodles (a Tanabiki Seimen specialty) typically contain wheat. Ask each vendor directly on the day of your visit.
gf_detail: This is a shared-kitchen space with 10+ rotating vendors; no vendor has published gluten-free menus or dedicated allergen information. The building owner is Tanabiki Seimen, a dried-noodle (somen/wheat) manufacturer, and noodle-based dishes are among the offerings. Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-based) is likely used in obanzai cooking. No dedicated fryer status is known. Diners with celiac disease should contact the specific vendor operating on their intended visit day well in advance.
caveats: This is a share cafe with 10+ rotating tenants; the menu, hours, and dietary offerings change by day and by operator. Always verify which vendor is operating on your visit date via Instagram (@cafe_eni_himeji) before going. The Tabelog hours reflect a general composite and may not match any given day's actual tenant.

bio.background: 縁（エニ）は、明治30年（1897年）創業の老舗乾麺メーカー「田引製麺」の旧本社社屋をリノベーションした古民家シェアカフェ。建物は明治〜大正期の洋風木造建築で、漆喰壁・ステンドグラス（6枚、同社の歴史を描く）・格天井など当時の意匠を保ちながら約8ヶ月の保存改修工事を経て2020年9月にオープン。第12回姫路市都市景観賞（建築部門）を受賞。
bio.specialty: テナント各社が日替わりでおばんざいランチ・ダッチベイビー・クロッフル・そうめん料理などを提供するシェアカフェ形式。固定アンカーテナントのobann（オバン）は有機野菜を使ったおばんざいランチで知られる。
bio.philosophy: 建物オーナーは「先人や地域への愛情のかたち」として歴史建造物の保存を重視し、地域の人々が集まる場づくりを目指している。食においては農薬不使用・有機野菜を重視した「体に優しい」料理を志向するテナントが多い。
roles: share cafe anchor tenant: obann（有機野菜おばんざい）
building owner: 田引製麺株式会社

anecdotes.text: 田引製麺は明治30年創業の123年超の老舗乾麺メーカー。旧工場・本社を「歴史的建造物保存プロジェクト」として自社でリノベし、地域の食文化発信拠点に転換した。
2024年7月、第12回姫路市都市景観賞（建築部門）を受賞。漆喰・窓・木製建具など当時の素材を活かした改修が評価された。
2020年11月のオープン記念イベントでは子どもたちが親のためにそうめん料理を作る「キッズレストラン」を開催し、大人は姫路城見学へ招待するという地域交流型企画を実施。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Building owner Tanabiki Seimen is a wheat noodle (somen) manufacturer; noodle-based dishes are served in the space. Cross-contamination risk from wheat flour in a shared kitchen environment cannot be ruled out.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Obanzai-style Japanese home cooking (the anchor tenant's specialty) routinely uses wheat-based soy sauce and mirin. No gluten-free soy sauce alternative has been confirmed by any vendor.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No vendor has published vegan certification or confirmed separation of animal and plant ingredients. Japanese dashi (fish or bonito stock) is standard in obanzai cooking and is not explicitly excluded.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu or staff allergy-handling protocol has been published by any rotating tenant. Contact the specific vendor operating on your planned visit day via Instagram (@cafe_eni_himeji) well in advance.

positives.text: The anchor tenant obann emphasizes organic, pesticide-free vegetables and a 'body-kind' cooking philosophy, suggesting some sensitivity to ingredient quality and dietary awareness.
Vegetable-forward menus and seasonal produce are consistently highlighted across multiple tenants, indicating plant-based options are likely available on most days.

name_ja: 炉端とおでん 呼炉凪来 姫路店
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市駅前町315 ドレミビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 姫路市駅前町315; coordinates 34.829098, 134.692093 — approximately 445m from target center, well within 1.15km radius.
neighborhood: 駅前町・山陽姫路駅近（徒歩約4分）
seats_text: 70席（最大80名まで対応可、個室あり）
closed_days: 不定休（ビルメンテナンスによる臨時休業あり）
cultural_comfort_note: Standard Japanese izakaya environment with Japanese-language menus; some online reservation platforms may show English, but in-house communication is Japanese.
vegan_detail: A handful of vegetable robata items exist — grilled corn with butter and soy, whole grilled peppers, mountain yam, young corn, edamame — but these are incidental and not flagged vegan. Oden broth is fish-based (bonito/konbu dashi) and most oden pieces contain fish paste or shellfish. The kitchen is not oriented toward plant-based diners and hidden animal ingredients (dashi, soy-glazes) are pervasive. Eat-all-you-can oden contains octopus, shumai, fish cake, and hanpen as standard items.
gf_detail: No gluten-free declarations anywhere on the menu or restaurant materials. Standard wheat-containing soy sauce (shoyu) is used throughout — in robata glazes, yakitori tare, and almost certainly the oden broth seasoning. Udon (wheat noodles) is on the menu. Fried items are present (fried rice, karaage likely). No dedicated fryer information available. Celiacs should contact the restaurant directly before visiting; safe options cannot be confirmed without direct staff inquiry.

bio.background: 呼炉凪来 is a chain brand operated by Foolish Co., Ltd., which opened this Himeji location in March 2025 as an expansion of a concept that originated in Shinjuku, Tokyo. The Shinjuku original reportedly ranked first in access rankings on reservation platforms, driving the brand to expand regionally.
bio.specialty: The signature draw is an all-you-can-eat oden counter at the center of the dining room, offered at ¥550 per person as a built-in appetizer service. Oden items include daikon, konnyaku, egg, thick fried tofu, shirataki, octopus, shumai, fish cake, chikuwa, and hanpen. Charcoal robata grilling of seasonal seafood and vegetables rounds out the menu.
bio.origin: Shinjuku, Tokyo (parent brand); Himeji location opened March 13, 2025.

anecdotes.text: The Shinjuku original location ranked #1 in access rankings on major Japanese reservation platforms before the chain expanded to Himeji.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated GF preparation area or allergen separation is mentioned. Standard izakaya kitchen handles wheat-containing items (udon, yakitori tare, soy-glazed robata) alongside all dishes.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard wheat-containing soy sauce (koikuchi shoyu) is presumed throughout — used in robata glazes (corn butter-soy, yam isobe-yaki), yakitori tare, and oden broth seasoning. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternatives are mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Oden broth is fish-based (bonito and/or konbu dashi). All vegetable robata items are cooked on the same grill used for meat and fish. No separation of cooking surfaces or utensils for plant-based diners is mentioned.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen handling policy is published on any platform reviewed. Direct contact with the restaurant (079-280-8684) is recommended before visiting with serious dietary restrictions.

positives.text: Plain sashimi (tuna, salmon, yellowtail, sea urchin) is available and would be naturally wheat-free and gluten-free if served without soy sauce or with tamari requested — confirm with staff.
Several vegetable robata items (whole grilled peppers, grilled corn, young corn) could theoretically be requested without soy glaze — ask staff about preparation method.

caveats: This is a regional chain izakaya (Foolish Co.) with no GF or vegan program. GF confidence is "ask" because standard wheat soy sauce is used throughout and no allergen information is published. Vegan diners face fish-based oden broth and shared cooking surfaces. Hours noted as irregular due to building maintenance closures. Friday open time discrepancy noted: Tabelog says 16:00, HotPepper says 15:00 — HotPepper used for Friday hours as more recently updated source. Verify current hours before visiting.

address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市本町68（家老屋敷館）
geocode_note: Coordinates from GSI AddressSearch for 兵庫県姫路市本町６８番地. Lat 34.835873, lng 134.687363 — within target box and ~531 m from the Himeji Castle–Station midpoint.
neighborhood: 本町 / 姫路城前
cuisine: 日本料理（えきそば・丼・天ぷら・スイーツ）
vegan_detail: All core dishes contain animal products. The ekisoba broth is built on bonito and mackerel powder dashi; the noodles are Chinese-style wheat noodles. The oyakodon uses chicken and eggs. No plant-based or vegan alternatives are listed anywhere on the menu or official materials. Hidden fish-based dashi is present in every savoury item.
gf_detail: The signature ekisoba noodle is made from wheat flour with kansui (alkaline water) — it is a wheat noodle product. The broth contains wheat via powdered soy sauce. Tempura coatings add additional wheat. No gluten-free alternatives, dedicated fryer, or allergen-accommodation procedures are documented. The product ingredient list confirms wheat in noodles, broth, and tempura; allergens listed as wheat, shrimp, soy, pork, mackerel, sesame. This venue is not safe for celiac or gluten-sensitive diners.
hours_raw: 10:00–18:00 (L.O. food 17:45); open year-round except on days when Himeji Castle is closed; hours may extend during castle events
closed_days: Closed only on days when Himeji Castle is closed; otherwise year-round
seats_text: Counter seating on the ground floor; long-table seating on the second floor with views toward Himeji Castle. Exact count not published.
cultural_comfort_note: Located inside a tourist-oriented facility directly in front of a UNESCO World Heritage site, with QR-code payments, takeout, and a menu clearly aimed at international visitors; English signage and menus are likely present.

bio.background: Opened July 25, 2025 in the Karoyashiki-kan building directly in front of Himeji Castle, this dining room is the newest outpost of Maneki Foods (まねき食品株式会社), a company founded in Meiji 21 (1888) that invented the Himeji ekisoba concept in 1949. The castle-front venue was conceived partly to extend the company's popular 'Ultimate Ekisoba' dish that debuted at the 2025 Osaka-Kansai Expo.
bio.specialty: Ekisoba — Himeji's local soul food of Chinese-style wheat noodles in Japanese-style dashi broth, served here in premium forms including a limited Kobe beef sukiyaki version and a Banshu Hyakka chicken oyakodon set.
bio.philosophy: Celebrate Himeji's food heritage for both residents and overseas visitors while partnering with artisans recovering from the 2024 Noto Peninsula earthquake — the Expo edition of the dish was served in Wajima lacquerware by Taya Shikki.

anecdotes.text: The 'Ultimate Kobe Beef Sukiyaki Ekisoba' that launched the restaurant was first served at the Osaka-Kansai Expo 2025 in special Wajima lacquerware bowls made by craftspeople rebuilding after the earthquake. The castle-front version substitutes standard tableware but keeps the same broth recipe.

Ekisoba — the unlikely pairing of Chinese ramen-style noodles with Japanese bonito dashi — has been sold by Maneki at Himeji Station since 1949 and is considered a point of local pride, with residents describing it as 'soul food' that feels completely natural despite the culinary mismatch.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen prepares tempura (wheat-battered) alongside all other dishes. No dedicated fryer or allergen-separated preparation area is documented. Cross-contamination with wheat must be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The ekisoba broth contains powdered soy sauce as a listed ingredient. All savoury dishes served at this venue use the same broth base, meaning wheat via soy sauce is present throughout the menu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bonito and mackerel powder are core broth ingredients listed in the ekisoba product; there is no plant-based broth option. Every savoury dish contacts fish-based dashi.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen-handling procedures or staff allergy training information is published on the official website or in any press materials reviewed.

positives.text: Accepts credit cards and QR payments; non-smoking throughout; child-friendly; takeout available for packaged ekisoba and souvenir items.

caveats: Hours may extend during Himeji Castle special events and close on the rare days the castle itself is shut. The Kobe beef sukiyaki ekisoba was a limited August 2025 promotion; regular menu items are standard ekisoba varieties, oyakodon sets, curry, and matcha sweets. The restaurant is run by a well-established food company (Maneki Foods, est. 1888), not an independent operator.

cuisine: 串揚げ・串かつ (kushikatsu / deep-fried skewers)
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市西二階町13
geocode_note: GSI address-search on 兵庫県姫路市西二階町13 returned [134.690445, 34.831982]; approximately 195m from the target anchor point, well within the 1.15km radius.
neighborhood: 西二階町商店街 (Nishi-Nikaimachi Shopping Street), Himeji castle-town district
hours_raw: ランチタイム 11:30〜14:00 / ディナータイム 17:00〜21:00 / 定休日：日曜・祝日・月曜
closed_days: 日曜・祝日・月曜 (Sundays, public holidays, Mondays)
seats_text: Counter seating on the ground floor (approx. 9 seats, fried in front of you); relaxed group dining on the second floor.
cultural_comfort_note: A long-running neighbourhood kushikatsu counter in a traditional covered shopping arcade; mostly local regulars; English signage is not expected, and phone reservations would be in Japanese.
vegan_detail: Kushikatsu is deep-fried in a wheat-breadcrumb batter and the core menu is built around meat and seafood skewers. No vegetable-only or plant-based options are advertised, and the shared frying oil means animal products are present throughout service. Dashi or bonito-based seasoning cannot be ruled out in side items. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
gf_detail: Kushikatsu by definition uses panko (wheat breadcrumbs) as its coating — gluten is a core ingredient in every skewer. The restaurant fries all items in a single shared fryer (no dedicated GF fryer mentioned or implied). Dipping sauces and any seasoned accompaniments almost certainly contain wheat-based soy sauce. No GF options or allergy-awareness statements appear anywhere on the official site or in any review. This venue is not safe for celiac disease or serious gluten sensitivity.

bio.background: もぐらや opened in 1998 in Himeji's Nishi-Nikaimachi covered shopping arcade, a narrow two-storey streetscape that has served the castle-town's residents for generations. According to one blog account, the shop's mole-themed name (もぐら = mole) is said to have been borrowed from a previous owner's favourite Osaka restaurant, though whether that forerunner also served fried skewers is uncertain. Owner Hashizume Tsunanori has run the kitchen for over two decades.
bio.specialty: 串かつ (kushikatsu) — wheat-battered, freshly fried skewers served piping hot straight from the oil to the customer across the counter. The lunch set (串かつ定食, ¥800) offers seven skewers with rice, miso soup, potato salad and pickles; à la carte skewers range ¥100–¥300 at dinner.
bio.philosophy: The house motto is to fry each skewer in front of the guest and plate it as fast as possible — heat and crunch are everything. Lunch is brisk and affordable; dinner moves at a more leisurely pace, with beer, sake and shochu on hand.

anecdotes.text: A 2017 blogger noted the owner's warm hospitality — offering to time the beer pour to arrive with the first skewer — and described a serving plate decorated with a rabbit motif despite the restaurant's mole name, a small quirk that charmed regulars.
The shop's name is reportedly derived from a previous owner's wife's favourite restaurant in Osaka, though the connection to fried skewers is unclear.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All kushikatsu are coated in wheat panko and fried in a shared fryer. No dedicated GF fryer exists. Cross-contamination with gluten is certain for every item on the menu.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy-sauce specifics are disclosed, but Worcestershire sauce is used as the standard dipping condiment (contains wheat) and conventional Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat) is standard in this type of establishment.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Meat and seafood skewers are fried continuously in shared oil. No plant-only fryer or cooking station is available.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling policy, allergen menu, or dietary-accommodation statement appears on the official website or in any review found. Phone enquiry in Japanese would be required.

caveats: Hours from the official Jimdo site (closed Sun/Mon/holidays, dinner until 21:00) differ slightly from Tabelog which lists Mon–Sat open and dinner until 22:00; the official site is treated as authoritative. Seat count (9) is from a single 2014 Tabelog review and may not reflect current layout. The second phone number (090-1139-0792) appears on Tabelog/Yahoo Maps and may be a mobile line used for reservations alongside the landline 079-284-2913.

name_ja: 天ぷらと小鉢料理 だん乃
cuisine: 天ぷら、小鉢料理、居酒屋
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市駅前町363番1 姫路グランフェスタ5番街
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 駅前町363番地 / 188番地 returns [134.690842, 34.827621], directly under JR Himeji Station, well within the 1.15 km target radius.
neighborhood: 駅前町 (JR姫路駅直下・グランフェスタ)
seats_text: 37 seats total: 9 counter seats facing the open fry station, 28 table seats
cultural_comfort_note: Counter-style tempura izakaya with a convivial atmosphere; staff have been described as welcoming, but no English-language service or English menus are mentioned anywhere.
gf_detail: Tempura batter is wheat-flour-based — the core of every dish. Dashi is almost certainly made with katsuobushi (bonito), and standard soy sauce contains wheat. No gluten-free batter alternative, dedicated fryer, or allergen menu has been mentioned in any source. This restaurant is not safe for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: The menu is built around seafood and fish-based preparations. Dashi likely uses katsuobushi (bonito flakes). Vegetable tempura items exist, but they share fryer oil with seafood and are cooked in the same batter. No plant-based or vegan menu track is offered.

bio.background: Head chef Oba Takuya brings 30 years of Japanese cuisine experience to the kitchen. The restaurant is managed by Matsuoka Kanako. The name 'だん乃' derives from the owner's family name.
bio.specialty: Freshly fried tempura of same-day-caught seafood from nearby Murotsu, Boze, and Shikama fishing ports, including the signature 鯛の姿天 (whole sea bream tempura). Small dishes (kobachi) such as Himeji oden with conger eel and ginger soy sauce round out the menu.
bio.philosophy: The owner's stated vision is a place 'where both customers and staff can spend time smiling,' with an open counter so diners can watch ingredients — displayed in a showcase — being fried to order.
bio.origin: Opened November 27, 2025 in the Grand Festa underground shopping street directly beneath JR Himeji Station (5番街).

anecdotes.text: Fresh seafood is sourced daily from Murotsu Port, Boze Port, and Shikama Port near Himeji; the name 'だん乃' comes from the owner's own surname.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All tempura — seafood and vegetable alike — is fried in the same oil at an open counter. No dedicated GF fryer is mentioned in any source.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No wheat-free soy sauce (tamari) is mentioned. Standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat, is the baseline ingredient across sauces, dipping broths, and oden broth.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable tempura shares fryer oil with shrimp, fish, and other seafood items cooked to order in the same vessel at the counter.

positives.text: Open counter seating lets diners see exactly what is being fried and in what order, which at least allows direct conversation with the chef about specific requests.

caveats: Hours split (Sun-Thu 21:00 last order vs Fri-Sat 22:00) sourced from a secondary search result summary; Tabelog lists a flat 11:00-22:00. Confirm directly before a late visit. The restaurant opened November 2025 and reviews are sparse; dietary safety assessments are based on the cuisine type and menu descriptions rather than staff-confirmed allergy protocols.

name_ja: カフェ&レンタルスペース カリカリちゃん
vegan_detail: Lunch is frequently served in vegan style (ベジ仕様) using plant-based proteins such as soy meat and oatmeal patties, with no animal products on those days. However, the menu changes daily and animal ingredients occasionally appear; confirming ahead is advised. Sweets (brown rice flour muffins, okara-oat cookie sandwiches) are plant-based. Hidden dashi/bonito risk exists on non-vegan lunch days — ask when ordering.
gf_detail: Snacks and sweets are explicitly made from brown rice flour (玄米粉) and oatmeal instead of wheat flour, making them gluten-free by ingredient. However, the lunch menu changes daily and may include wheat-containing items (soy sauce, grains). There is no dedicated GF kitchen and no formal celiac protocol confirmed. Cross-contamination risk cannot be ruled out. For celiac safety, stick to the labeled GF sweets and ask staff about daily lunch ingredients.
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市東延末1-69
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 兵庫県姫路市東延末一丁目６９番地 returned [134.686188, 34.82497]. Calculated distance from target center (34.8331, 134.6921) is approximately 1.05km — within the 1.15km threshold. The shop sits southwest of Himeji Station along Kazarima-kaido (飾磨街道), outside the strict Otemae/castle-town corridor but within radius.
neighborhood: 東延末 / 飾磨街道沿い (Higashi-Nobesue / Kazarima-kaido)
hours_raw: 水〜土 11:30〜17:30、日月火 定休。レンタルスペース 9:00〜21:00（火曜定休）
seats_text: 12席（靴を脱いでくつろげるレンタルスペースが奥にあり、カフェメニューの持ち込み可）
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming, community-oriented neighborhood spot run by a maternal team; counter service with daily specials written in Japanese only — some basic Japanese or a translation app is helpful, but the relaxed atmosphere and simple ordering make it approachable.

bio.background: カリカリちゃんのオーナーはマクロビオティック（穀物・野菜を中心とした伝統的な日本食の考え方）との出会いをきっかけに2010年頃からお菓子作りをスタート。2017年には姫路子供劇場の事務所内でワークショップ販売の場として活動し、2020年4月1日に飾磨街道沿いの現住所（東延末1-69）に旧旅館を改装したカフェ&レンタルスペースとしてリニューアルオープンし、10周年の節目を迎えた。「人と人がつながり、心も体も元気になれる場所」をコンセプトに、地元の有機農家グループ『姫路有機野菜の会』から仕入れた旬野菜を使った手作りランチと無添加おやつを提供している。
bio.specialty: 玄米粉・おからのクッキーサンド（グルテンフリー）と日替わり野菜ランチ
bio.philosophy: 素材の味を活かしたシンプルな味付け、無添加、マクロビオティックの考え方に基づいた「体への負担が少ない」食を大切にしている。

anecdotes.text: 旧旅館の客間をリノベーションした靴脱ぎ式のレンタルスペースは、赤ちゃん連れでも安心して使えるファミリー向けの空間として地域に愛されており、コンサートや展覧会など多目的に活用されている。
ランチはある日、人参と塩麹の豆乳スープ、もち麦サラダ、ヤングコーンの塩焼き、重ね煮野菜のカレー、オートミールハンバーグという動物性食品不使用のベジ仕様で提供された。

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated GF kitchen or formal cross-contamination protocol has been confirmed. The café is a small space handling both wheat-free snacks and daily-changing lunch menus that may include wheat-based ingredients. Celiac diners should exercise caution.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No explicit information found on whether wheat-based soy sauce (醤油) is used in the lunch cooking. Standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat; macrobiotic kitchens sometimes use tamari but this has not been confirmed here.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The lunch menu changes daily and animal products (dairy, egg, fish) occasionally appear in dishes. On vegan-style days the menu is fully plant-based, but cross-contact from shared equipment on other days is unconfirmed.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff are described as willing to address food allergy and vegetarian preferences upon request, but no formal allergen management system or written allergen menu has been confirmed.

positives.text: All food and sweets are additive-free (無添加) since the business began — no artificial preservatives, colorings, or flavor enhancers.
The signature snacks — brown rice flour (玄米粉) mini muffins and okara-oat cookie sandwiches — are made without wheat flour and are explicitly gluten-free by ingredient.
Lunch frequently uses organic vegetables from the local Himeji Yuuki Yasai no Kai (姫路有機野菜の会) cooperative, with simple seasoning to let ingredients shine.

caveats: Hours from Tabelog show Wed-Sat 11:30-17:30 with Sun/Mon/Tue closed, but one source mentioned Mon and 3rd Sunday closed with hours until 18:30 — the schedule may vary or have changed. The official Jimdo site returned 403. Recommend confirming current hours via Instagram (@karikarikiyoshiro) or phone (079-224-8145) before visiting. The spot is technically southwest of the castle/station axis along Kazarima-kaido, not in the Otemae area proper, though within 1.05km of the target center.

name_ja: こばやし茶店（抹茶喫茶 存外）
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市二階町66
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI AddressSearch API for 兵庫県姫路市二階町66番地. Coordinates lat 34.831959 / lng 134.692184 place the shop approximately 130m from the target center point, well within the 1.15km radius.
neighborhood: 二階町商店街 (Nikaimachi Shopping Arcade)
seats_text: 6 seats in a compact tatami-adjacent tearoom inside the main tea shop
vegan_detail: Matcha itself is vegan. Traditional wagashi sweets are generally plant-based (rice flour, adzuki bean paste, agar), but exact recipes from the three local confectioners (Kineya, Ichifuji, Iseya) are not published — hidden egg or dairy is possible in some types. The matcha soft serve (Green Soft) is ice cream and almost certainly contains dairy. No vegan labeling or staff allergy-communication policy is documented. Confirm with staff on the day.
gf_detail: Matcha tea is inherently gluten-free. However, some wagashi types (manju, dorayaki) are made with wheat flour, and the shop sources sweets from three local confectioners whose ingredient lists are not published. The matcha soft serve's stabilizers/mix are unknown. No dedicated gluten-free preparation, dedicated fryer, or allergen disclosure is documented anywhere. Celiac-level safety cannot be assumed — ask staff about each specific wagashi item before ordering.

bio.background: Founded in Meiji 8 (1875), Kobayashi Chamise — operating as 株式会社小林松涛園 (Kobayashi Shoutouen Co., Ltd.) — has devoted itself exclusively to Japanese tea for 150 years, making it one of Himeji's oldest specialty tea shops. The main store sits in the Nikaimachi covered shopping arcade, roughly halfway between Himeji Station and Himeji Castle. Inside, a tiny tearoom called 存外 (Zongai — meaning 'unexpectedly good') offers just six seats where guests can slow down, choose their own tea bowl from a rotating display of styles from across Japan, and have matcha whisked for them tableside.
bio.specialty: Matcha and wagashi set (¥660) featuring seasonal sweets from three local Himeji confectioners, rotated daily; Green Soft — a matcha soft-serve ice cream that has been sold for over 50 years and occasionally sells out before closing
bio.philosophy: お茶一筋 — devoted entirely to tea, serving the Japanese tradition of matcha without pretension, so anyone can walk in from the castle road and experience a quiet moment with a bowl of frothy tea

anecdotes.text: The Green Soft matcha ice cream was developed collaboratively among regional tea shops seeking new ways to use matcha beyond drinking. It has been sold for over 50 years and on busy days sells out before the shop closes.
The name 存外 (Zongai) is a Japanese idiom meaning 'unexpectedly / better than expected' — the tearoom's name is itself an invitation: come in expecting nothing fancy, leave pleasantly surprised.
Guests select their own tea bowl — shapes, colors, and clay types vary — before staff whisk the matcha, giving each visit a subtly personal character without requiring any knowledge of tea ceremony.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information about cross-contamination practices, shared surfaces, or separation of gluten-containing wagashi from other items is available from any source.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce is not a feature of this menu. Primary concern is wheat in some wagashi types (manju skin, dorayaki batter). Ingredient lists for the rotating sweets from local confectioners Kineya, Ichifuji, and Iseya are not publicly available.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No information on shared equipment between dairy-containing soft serve and other items. Matcha is prepared with whisking in selected bowls — no cross-contact concern for matcha tea itself, but wagashi ingredients are unverified.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy communication policy or allergen menu is documented at this establishment. The shop does not appear to have any published process for handling dietary restriction inquiries. Direct verbal inquiry at the counter is the only avenue.

positives.text: Matcha tea itself (plain, whisked) is naturally gluten-free and vegan — the signature experience of this tearoom can be enjoyed as matcha alone.
Many traditional wagashi are plant-based (adzuki bean paste, rice flour, agar), and the matcha-and-wagashi set at ¥660 may be suitable — but verification of each day's specific sweets is required.

cultural_comfort_note: Primarily a Japanese-language environment in a local shopping arcade; no English menu is documented, though the experience is simple (point to bowl, receive matcha) and non-intimidating for non-Japanese speakers.
caveats: Hours confirmed as 10:00–18:30 / closed Tuesdays across multiple sources (Tabelog, TripAdvisor, official site, Himeji tourism board) but the official site lists 10:00–18:00 on one page and 18:30 on another — treat as approximately 18:00–18:30 and verify on arrival. Seat count of 6 from Tabelog; tiny space means a wait is likely on weekends. Green Soft occasionally sells out before closing. No allergen disclosure exists — dietary suitability of individual wagashi must be confirmed verbally on the day.

address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市本町68 家老屋敷跡 ろの屋敷
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI AddressSearch for 兵庫県姫路市本町68番地; coordinates [134.687363, 34.835873] place this approximately 530m from the target center (34.8331, 134.6921), well within the 1.15km radius.
neighborhood: 本町 (Himeji Castle front)
cuisine: 和菓子・甘味処 (Japanese Sweets / Sweet Tea Room)
vegan_detail: Several items are incidentally plant-based: kuzu mochi (kuzu starch, sugar, water), warabi mochi (bracken starch), and amazake (fermented rice drink) contain no animal products. However, the shop also sells taiyaki, obanyaki, and mitarashi dango; mitarashi glaze uses soy sauce which typically contains wheat and may contain hidden animal-derived ingredients. No vegan labeling or staff communication about vegan status confirmed. Diners should ask specifically about each item.
gf_detail: This is a mixed wagashi shop — not a dedicated gluten-free establishment. Several products are naturally wheat-free (kuzu mochi, warabi mochi, plain mochi, rice-based daifuku), but wheat-containing items are also sold in the same space: taiyaki and obanyaki use wheat-flour batter, and fu manju (麩まんじゅう) is made from wheat gluten. Mitarashi dango glaze uses soy sauce, which typically contains wheat. No dedicated GF kitchen or fryer separation confirmed. Cross-contamination risk is real; celiac diners should ask individually about each product and exercise caution.
hours_raw: 10:00–17:00, closed Thursdays (TripAdvisor; official site unreachable for verification — confirm before visiting)
seats_text: Small eat-in counter / table space inside the shop for enjoying sweets on-site; primarily a takeout confectionery. Exact seat count not published.
cultural_comfort_note: A compact traditional wagashi counter with very limited English; menus and signage are in Japanese only, but the sweets are visual and pointing/gesturing works fine for ordering.
caveats: Official website (http://www.shirasagido.jp/) returned a self-signed certificate error and could not be fetched; hours are sourced from TripAdvisor and may not reflect current schedule. No allergen or dietary labeling data found from any source — celiac and vegan diners must ask staff directly. Cash only confirmed across multiple sources.

bio.background: Shirasagido Honpo is a small independent wagashi (Japanese traditional sweets) shop set within the 家老屋敷跡 ろの屋敷 — the restored grounds of a former chief retainer's residence directly in front of Himeji Castle, a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The location gives the shop an atmospheric, old-town character that fits the historic setting.
bio.specialty: Kuzu mochi and warabi mochi made with Tamba black beans (丹波黒大豆) — plump, earthy, chewy sweets served chilled; also known for kuzu manju, matcha sets, and seasonal kakigori (shaved ice).
bio.philosophy: Seasonal, castle-front wagashi using quality regional ingredients, particularly prized Tamba black beans from Hyogo's hinterland, presented in a setting that echoes Himeji's feudal heritage.

anecdotes.text: Customers describe the kuzu mochi as 'プルプル' (bouncy and gelatinous) with the gentle sweetness of Tamba black beans — a classic cool-down snack after walking Himeji Castle.
One visitor noted the shop's 'refined sweetness' (上品な甘さ) and described it as having a traditional confectionery feel with a clean, elegant storefront directly facing the castle.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free kitchen confirmed. The shop prepares and sells wheat-containing items (taiyaki, obanyaki, fu manju) alongside naturally wheat-free items (kuzu mochi, warabi mochi). Cross-contamination risk cannot be ruled out.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Mitarashi dango is listed as a menu item; mitarashi glaze is made with soy sauce, which in Japan typically contains wheat. No tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternative noted.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No information on whether dairy or egg is used in any products. Taiyaki batter traditionally contains egg and milk. No vegan-specific handling protocol found.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen handling information, staff training documentation, or allergy communication protocols found across any reviewed source. Recommend calling ahead at 0120-11-3977 to ask about specific items.

positives.text: Core items like kuzu mochi and warabi mochi are made from naturally gluten-free, plant-based starches (kuzu root, bracken fern) with sugar and water — inherently free of wheat, dairy, and eggs when made traditionally.

name_ja: マーガレット スプーン
cuisine: スコーン・焼き菓子専門
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市本町144
geocode_note: GSI AddressSearch on 兵庫県姫路市本町144 returned [134.690643, 34.832657]. Approximately 135m from target center — well within 1.15km radius.
neighborhood: 本町・大手前通り（姫路城へ向かう城下町メインストリート）
hours_raw: 平日・土曜 10:00〜17:00、日曜・祝日 11:00〜17:00。売り切れ次第閉店。定休日：月曜・第2火曜。
closed_days: 月曜日、毎月第2火曜日（臨時休業あり、売り切れ次第閉店）
seats_text: テイクアウト主体。店内にカウンター席あり（イートインスペースは徒歩30秒の別棟）。個別テーブル席なし。
cultural_comfort_note: テイクアウト専門の小さな焼き菓子店で、注文はInstagram DMのみ受付。英語対応の記述なし。
vegan_detail: スコーンはすべて北海道産小麦粉・よつ葉発酵バター・生クリームを使用した乳製品ベース。ヴィーガン対応メニューの記載なし。
gf_detail: 主力商品のスコーンはすべて小麦粉使用。唯一の例外として米粉を使ったグルテンフリーのカヌレが存在することが1記事で言及されているが、常時販売かどうか不明。専用設備・交差汚染対策の情報なし。セリアック病の方には向かない。

bio.background: オーナーは約25年前に英語の料理本でスコーンに出会い、独学で製法を研究。輸入レシピ本と何百回もの試作を重ねてレシピを確立した後、地元の農産物直売所で焼き菓子を販売する形で始め、2014年12月に姫路城へ向かう大手前通り沿いの本町144に実店舗をオープン。
bio.specialty: 厚さ5cmを超えるニューヨークスタイルの極厚スコーン。定番のプレーンスコーンはよつ葉発酵バターの香りと風味を前面に出したベストセラー。約20種類のスコーンが日替わりで並ぶ。
bio.philosophy: 生地をこねすぎず、水分を少なめに保つことで独特のザクザク食感を生み出す。素材は小麦粉・バター・ナッツ・ドライフルーツを自ら吟味して仕入れる。英国式アフタヌーンティーの格式ではなく、ニューヨーク流のカジュアルで遊び心あるスコーンを目指す。

anecdotes.text: 店名「マーガレット」（デイジー）を選んだ理由は、「目立たないけれど、確かな存在感がある花」だから。この哲学が菓子づくりの姿勢にも表れている。
姫路城下町という歴史的な街並みの中に、ブラックを基調とした都会的な外観とオレンジのネオン管が映え、通りかかった人が焼き菓子の甘い香りに引き寄せられる光景が名物になっている。

gf_cross_contamination.text: スコーンはすべて小麦粉使用の厨房で製造。専用グルテンフリー設備や交差汚染対策についての記述は確認されていない。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 全製品に乳製品（発酵バター・生クリーム）が使用されており、ヴィーガン向け製品との分離製造は行われていないとみられる。

staff_allergy_handling.text: アレルギー対応や食物制限に関するスタッフ対応についての情報は公式・ブログ・レビューいずれにも見当たらない。来店前にInstagram DMで問い合わせることが推奨される（電話注文不可）。

positives.text: 米粉を使ったグルテンフリーのカヌレ（外はカリッと、中はふわふわ）が少なくとも一時期提供されていたことが確認されている。

caveats: 営業時間はソースによって微差あり（Tabelog: 10:00-17:30 vs 他記事: 平日17:00/日祝11:00開始）。売り切れ次第閉店のため実際の閉店時刻は変動する。第2火曜以外にも臨時休業があり、訪問前のInstagram確認を強く推奨。米粉カヌレのGF情報は2022年記事の1件のみで、現在の定番メニューかどうか未確認。

address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市立町71 清和立町ビル1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 兵庫県姫路市立町71; coordinates land ~360m southwest of the Castle-Station midpoint, well within the 1.15km radius.
neighborhood: 立町 (Tatemachi), ~262m from Sanyo-Himeji Station
cuisine: Harima regional izakaya — Anago (conger eel), Seto Inland Sea seafood, Himeji oden
vegan_detail: A seafood-forward izakaya with no declared vegan menu. Nearly every dish centers on fish (anago, sashimi), shellfish (shrimp shumai), or meat (Tajima chicken). Himeji oden is simmered in a dashi broth almost certainly made from fish stock. No plant-based or vegan option is advertised anywhere. Inquire before visiting; realistic prospects are slim without advance arrangement.
gf_detail: Standard izakaya kitchen with no gluten-free declaration. Soy sauce (wheat-containing) is foundational to virtually every dish including oden broth, sashimi accompaniments, and marinades. Karaage (fried chicken) and anago tempura involve wheat-flour coatings. No dedicated fryer confirmed. Cross-contamination risk is high in a small izakaya kitchen. Guests with celiac disease should contact the restaurant directly before visiting; safe options cannot be assumed.
seats_text: 20 seats total: 2-top counter/table seats x2, sunken kotatsu tables for 4 x4. Full private hire possible for groups of 16+.
closed_days: 日曜定休（月曜が祝日の場合は営業）
cultural_comfort_note: A local izakaya catering primarily to Japanese regulars and domestic tourists; no English menu or English-speaking staff is advertised, though the atmosphere is friendly and the menu is approachable for curious visitors.

bio.background: A small, independently run izakaya in Himeji's Tatemachi district, a short walk from both the JR and Sanyo Himeji stations. The restaurant positions itself as a showcase for 播磨うまいもん — the authentic flavors of the Harima region — sourcing seafood directly from Murotsu fishing port on the Seto Inland Sea and vegetables from contracted local farms.
bio.specialty: Anago (conger eel) prepared multiple ways — grilled, as tempura, and as ochazuke (tea-poured rice) — alongside Himeji oden served in the local style with ginger soy sauce, and house-made shrimp shumai.
bio.philosophy: Direct sourcing from a contract fishing port and local farms to bring genuine Harima flavors to the table at an accessible price point, in a lively late-night izakaya setting.

anecdotes.text: The interior is lined with autographed celebrity photos, giving the small space the feel of a well-loved local institution that has welcomed a wide cross-section of guests over the years.
The proprietor personally recommends the house-made shrimp shumai as the restaurant's signature item — a dish visitors consistently single out alongside the anago preparations.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Small izakaya kitchen serving fried dishes (karaage, anago tempura, tuna-skin karaage) alongside non-fried items. No dedicated fryer confirmed; shared-oil cross-contamination with wheat-coated items is likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Wheat-based soy sauce is central to virtually all cooking here: oden broth, sashimi dipping sauce, marinades for grilled anago, and karaage batter all conventionally contain wheat. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish dashi is the backbone of Himeji oden and likely used in multiple dishes. Shared cooking surfaces in a small kitchen handling fish, shellfish, and meat throughout service make vegan isolation impractical without direct advance arrangement.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling policy, allergen menu, or dietary accommodation statement is published on the official site, Tabelog, HotPepper, or Gurunavi listings. Direct phone inquiry (079-224-3999) before visiting is strongly advised for any dietary restriction.

positives.text: Fresh seafood sourced directly from Murotsu fishing port means dishes are made to order rather than pre-processed, which in principle allows ingredient-level discussion with staff for guests who can communicate in Japanese.

caveats: Hours data from Tabelog and official site agree on Mon-Thu until 01:00 and Fri-Sat until 03:00, but one Gurunavi listing showed food orders closing at 22:00 — this may reflect an older or inaccurate entry; confirm directly. Seat count varies slightly across sources (16 vs 20); 20 is the figure from Tabelog and HotPepper. No English-language reviews found; all dietary safety assessments are inferred from dish types, not confirmed by staff statements.

cuisine: 日本料理（おまかせランチ）
neighborhood: 小利木町（城西・船場川沿い）
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市小利木町47
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 兵庫県姫路市小利木町４７番地 returned [134.691055, 34.84024]. Approx 800m north of target center — within 1.15km radius.
seats_text: カウンター10席、テーブル12席（4人×3卓）、個室6席
cultural_comfort_note: 公式サイトおよびメニューは日本語のみ。英語対応の記載なし。電話予約のみのため、日本語または通訳が必要。
vegan_detail: おまかせランチのみの一本勝負。みそ汁はみょうが入りが確認されており、一般的な和食の出汁（かつお・煮干し）の使用が強く推測される。植物性出汁のみの対応可否は公式に一切記載なし。事前に電話で要確認。
gf_detail: 標準的な日本料理のおまかせコースのため、醤油（小麦含有）・みりん・味噌など小麦を含む調味料の使用が想定される。グルテンフリー対応に関する記載はウェブサイト・口コミいずれにも存在しない。コース一本のため代替対応の柔軟性は低い可能性あり。セリアック・重度小麦アレルギーの方は必ず電話で事前確認を。

bio.specialty: おまかせランチ（¥3,300税込）— 旬の素材を少量多皿で盛り合わせたプレート形式。漬け物・ドレッシングはすべて手作り。
bio.philosophy: 「日常のなかにある、ゆったりとした落ち着いた時間」を提供することを理念とし、食材の旨みを引き出す調理を重視する。

anecdotes.text: 2024年6月15日オープン。船場川沿いの古民家を改装した空間で、カウンター席からは姫路城の天守閣とお堀を望む。姫路城のビューを楽しめるランチとして開店直後から人気を集めた。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: おまかせ和食コースのため醤油・みりん・味噌など小麦含有調味料の使用が一般的に想定されるが、具体的な使用調味料の情報は公式・口コミいずれにも開示なし。

vegan_cross_contact.text: みそ汁にみょうがを使用した記録あり。出汁はかつお等の動物性素材の可能性が高いが未確認。

staff_allergy_handling.text: アレルギー・食事制限対応についての情報は一切公開されていない。問い合わせは電話（079-297-8388）のみ受付。

positives.text: 漬け物・ドレッシングをすべて手作りし、食材の風味を引き立てることを意識した調理スタイルを採用。添加物への意識が伺える。

caveats: おまかせランチ一本勝負のため食事制限への柔軟な対応は期待しにくい。グルテン・ヴィーガン対応の可否は一切公開されておらず、訪問前に必ず日本語で電話確認が必要。不定休があるため公式サイトまたは電話で営業日を確認すること。

cuisine: Izakaya / Harima Local Sake & Small Plates
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市駅前町323 橋本ビル 2F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 兵庫県姫路市駅前町３２３番地 returned [134.692093, 34.829098]. Approximately 445m from target center (34.8331, 134.6921) — well within the 1.15km radius. Tabelog confirms 234m from Sanyo-Himeji Station.
neighborhood: 駅前町・山陽姫路駅近
seats_text: 12席（カウンター7席、テーブル2卓）。こぢんまりとした2階の空間。
cultural_comfort_note: Reservations are made via Instagram DM in Japanese; no English signage or menu is documented, and the setting is a small local counter bar where Japanese is the working language.
vegan_detail: No vegan-specific menu exists. Small plates (yamaimo, assorted appetizers, chorizo) are typical izakaya fare; hidden dashi (katsuobushi/bonito) is standard in Japanese cooking and very likely present in most dishes. No vegan claims found anywhere.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or accommodations documented. Wheat is present in standard soy sauce used throughout Japanese izakaya cooking, and most sauces, marinades, and dashi-based dishes will contain gluten. No dedicated fryer information available. Not suitable for celiac without direct staff consultation.

bio.background: Opened October 3, 2025 on the second floor of the Hashimoto Building in Himeji's Ekimae-cho. The venue is run by a wife-and-husband team: the wife manages the izakaya upstairs while her husband operates a ramen shop on the floor below, creating a small family food complex in the heart of the station neighborhood.
bio.specialty: Harima local sake (播磨地酒) served at affordable prices alongside simple izakaya small plates. Signature sake pours include Okuharimanma Junmai (¥500) and Senchuuhassaku (¥600).
bio.philosophy: A casual, down-to-earth neighborhood bar — the name 蒼 (sou, meaning 'blue/azure') reflects an understated simplicity. The atmosphere leans relaxed, with Okinawan-inflected music and a counter where regulars can drink well for little money.

anecdotes.text: An early visitor noted the opening 'didn't feel like a typical restaurant launch' — the entrance was low-key, almost residential in feel, yet inside a curated slate of Harima sake awaited at prices well below city-center bars.

A German guest happened to be present during one early visit, prompting a spontaneous conversation about why Japanese sake is priced so accessibly compared to Europe — the owner served as impromptu cultural guide.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information about cross-contamination controls is available. This is a 12-seat counter izakaya with a compact kitchen; dedicated fryer or allergen segregation is not documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard soy sauce containing wheat is used throughout Japanese izakaya cuisine. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternatives are mentioned anywhere in available sources.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Typical izakaya kitchen where fish-based dashi (katsuobushi/bonito) is standard in sauces, soups, and cooking bases. No vegan preparation protocols documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling policy or English-language accommodation is documented. Reservations are made via Instagram DM in Japanese; allergen queries would need to be raised there in advance.

positives.text: Small, owner-operated venue where direct communication with the proprietor is possible, making advance allergy enquiries via Instagram DM (@sou.taishusakaba) feasible before visiting.

caveats: Opened October 2025; only a handful of Tabelog reviews exist (score 3.05, 5 reviews). Hours are based on Tabelog listing and may shift as the business settles. Smoking is permitted at all seats — relevant for sensitive diners. No official website; Instagram DM is the only reservation channel. The blog-reported 8 counter seats vs Tabelog's 7 counter seats is a minor discrepancy; Tabelog's 12 total is used as authoritative.

address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市本町68 家老屋敷館「は」の屋敷内
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 兵庫県姫路市本町68番地. Coordinates lat 34.8359, lng 134.6874 are approximately 530m north-northwest of the target center — well within the 1.15km radius.
neighborhood: 本町（大手前・姫路城門前）
seats_text: 20 seats total: 6 counter seats and 4 tables
hours_raw: 11:00〜17:00（ラストオーダー16:30）、無休（時期により変動あり）
closed_days: 無休（年末年始・お盆等は要確認）
cultural_comfort_note: English and Chinese menus are available; touch-panel queue system at the entrance; popular with international tourists visiting Himeji Castle, so staff are accustomed to non-Japanese visitors.
vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around eggs — TKG (tamago kake gohan), thick omelets, egg custard, egg sandwiches. The signature thick omelet is made with bonito broth, mackerel flakes, and Rishiri kombu dashi. The miso soup served with sets almost certainly uses fish-based dashi. No plant-based dishes are offered. This is not a viable stop for vegans.
gf_detail: The core dish (plain rice with raw egg) could in principle be wheat-free, but the restaurant's proprietary tamago kake shoyu (custom-developed soy sauce) almost certainly contains wheat, as standard Japanese soy sauce does. The thick omelet uses bonito-and-kombu dashi — dashi ingredients are typically gluten-free, but cross-contamination risk in a shared kitchen is unknown. No gluten-free claims, no dedicated fryer information, and no allergen menu have been found on any source. Anyone with celiac disease should contact the restaurant before visiting.

bio.background: Tamagoya is operated by Fujihashigumi, a poultry farm that has raised chickens in the Yumesaki and Shikito districts of Himeji since 1925. The farm opened this egg-dedicated dining room in 2009 to put its own produce directly in front of diners at the gates of Himeji Castle.
bio.specialty: Tamakake meshi — a bowl of warm Himeji rice topped with a raw Yumeo or Yume-sodachi egg from the farm's own flocks, served with their proprietary tamago kake shoyu, pickles, and miso soup; eggs and rice are refillable.
bio.philosophy: Raise the chickens carefully on custom 31-ingredient feed, then let the egg speak for itself with minimal cooking and unlimited refills so every guest leaves satisfied.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's 'Yumeo' eggs won three consecutive national championships at the Tokyo Tamago Kake Gohan Festival and became the inaugural entrants into its Hall of Fame.
Celebrity visitors dine incognito; signed photographs from Japanese celebrities cover the interior walls.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on cross-contamination practices is available from any source reviewed. Kitchen setup and shared equipment are unknown.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant uses a proprietary tamago kake shoyu co-developed with a manufacturer. Standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat; no source confirms a wheat-free or tamari version is used.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Not applicable — the entire menu is egg-based and includes fish-derived dashi; no vegan options exist to assess cross-contact for.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No information found on allergy handling procedures. English-language menu is available but no allergen chart or staff allergy-handling protocol has been documented by any reviewer.

positives.text: The core raw egg on plain rice is a simple preparation; if the soy sauce can be confirmed wheat-free, the base dish has minimal gluten risk.

caveats: Hours listed as 11:00-17:00 (LO 16:30) across all sources but the Rurubu listing notes seasonal variation; verify around holidays, Golden Week, and Obon. The touch-panel queue system means waits can be long without a reservation slot. Cash only — no IC card or credit card accepted.

address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市呉服町18
geocode_note: GSI AddressSearch on 兵庫県姫路市呉服町18 returned [134.693665, 34.830647]; ~308 m from target center, well within 1.15 km radius.
neighborhood: 呉服町 (Gofukucho), near Himeji Castle
vegan_detail: Strictly 100% plant-based — no meat, fish, eggs, dairy, or honey in any dish. The kitchen follows macrobiotic principles and sources organic, naturally farmed, pesticide-reduced local vegetables and additive-free seasonings. Both the café menu and the attached shop stock only plant-based goods. Note: some menu items use fu (wheat gluten), so purely wheat-free vegans should check the day's menu.
gf_detail: No gluten-free certification and no dedicated GF protocol documented anywhere. The kitchen explicitly states food is "not allergy-free" and asks guests with allergies to inform staff in advance. At least one menu item (車麩 kuruma-fu, a wheel-shaped wheat gluten) is a recurring main dish, confirming wheat is actively used on the menu. The soy sauce type (wheat-bearing standard shoyu vs. wheat-free tamari) is unspecified in all sources reviewed. Cross-contamination risk cannot be ruled out. Celiacs and those with serious wheat allergy should contact the restaurant before visiting: 079-288-2439.
hours_raw: Shop 10:30–18:30; Café 11:30–18:30 (last order 17:30, lunch last order 15:30); Closed Mondays and 3rd Sunday of each month
seats_text: Two floors: ground floor has one four-person table and six counter seats; second floor has four two-person tables and two counter seats. A custom chandelier made from cutlery is a distinctive design feature.
cultural_comfort_note: English menus are available and the café is accustomed to international visitors, making it accessible without Japanese language skills.

bio.background: The story behind il riccio stretches back roughly half a century. The predecessor natural food shop traded on Himeji's Miyuki-dori shopping street for around 48 years before closing that location in November 2019 and reopening — reimagined as a plant-based café with an attached retail shop — on November 27, 2019. The date was deliberately chosen: it fell on a new moon and an auspicious day (新月の大安). The new home is a two-storey space in Gofukucho 18, steps from the historic arcade near Himeji Castle, in what was previously the C.T. CLUB premises.
bio.origin: Himeji, Hyogo Prefecture; relocated from Miyuki-dori after ~48 years
bio.specialty: Seasonal lunch set (一汁三菜) — one miso soup and three vegetable dishes over organic Hyogo-grown rice or brown rice, with a rotating plant-based main such as soy-meat cutlet, miso demi-glace hamburger, or glazed wheat-gluten; vegan desserts (gateau chocolat made with tofu and rice flour, soymilk pudding with blueberry sauce) change monthly
bio.philosophy: Macrobiotics meets 100% plant-based cooking: every ingredient is organic or naturally farmed, pesticide-reduced, and seasoned with additive-free condiments sourced — and also sold — in the shop downstairs. The guiding idea is that food should nourish 'beauty and energy from within.' The owner also teaches seasonal macrobiotic cooking classes on Italian, side-dish, and bento themes.

anecdotes.text: The café's name comes from the Italian word for hedgehog (il riccio), and a hedgehog sculpture greets guests at the entrance — a nod to the slow, deliberate pace the kitchen takes with its ingredients.
The reopening on November 27, 2019 was timed to coincide with a new moon falling on an auspicious day (新月の大安), reflecting the owners' holistic sensibility.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The café explicitly states its food is not allergy-free and that guests with allergies must notify staff in advance. No information on dedicated preparation surfaces or fryers is published.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The soy sauce type used in cooking is not disclosed in any published source. Standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat; whether the kitchen uses wheat-free tamari is unknown and must be confirmed directly with staff.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All menu items are 100% plant-based by design — no animal products enter the kitchen — so cross-contact with meat, fish, dairy, or eggs is not a concern.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant asks guests with any allergy to inform staff before ordering. No structured allergen matrix or written protocol is publicly available.

positives.text: Entirely animal-product-free kitchen eliminates the hidden-dashi and hidden-katsuo risks that are common pitfalls for vegans at Japanese restaurants.
Ingredients are organic, naturally farmed, and free from synthetic additives — the same goods are sold in the attached shop, giving transparency into sourcing.

caveats: Hours marked irregular because the closure pattern (every Monday plus only the 3rd Sunday) means Sunday hours vary by week — the hours_week array shows typical open days but Sunday is conditionally closed. The seat count of 18 is derived from the opening-week report (1F: 1×4-top + 6 counter; 2F: 4×2-tops + 2 counter) and may have changed. Wheat-gluten (fu) dishes appear on the rotating menu, so gluten-free suitability must be confirmed per visit. The predecessor shop founding year of ~1971 is inferred from "approaching 48 years" as of November 2019.

address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市本町68 家老屋敷跡 はの屋敷 1F・2F
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 兵庫県姫路市本町68 returned coordinates [134.687363, 34.835873], placing it directly in the castle precinct — well within the 1.15 km target radius.
neighborhood: 本町68（Honcho）- 姫路城前
seats_text: 40 seats on the 2nd floor; tables are closely spaced; window seats overlook Himeji Castle
cultural_comfort_note: Cafeteria-style service with no English signage confirmed; staff are part of a Japanese welfare organization — basic Japanese or pointing at a menu will be necessary.
vegan_detail: The menu is built around traditional Japanese comfort food — Himeji oden (simmered in a dashi broth typically made with katsuobushi/bonito), anago don (conger eel), bokkake don (braised beef tendon), katsu-meshi (breaded pork cutlet), akashi-yaki (octopus balls), and somen/nyumen noodles. No vegan options are listed or mentioned in any source. Hidden animal-derived dashi is near-certain in oden broth and noodle soups.
gf_detail: No gluten-free options confirmed in any source. The menu includes multiple high-gluten items: katsu-meshi (breaded pork cutlet), akashi-yaki (wheat batter), somen/nyumen noodles, and oden broth almost certainly seasoned with regular wheat-containing soy sauce (shoyu). No dedicated fryer information available. Cross-contamination risk is high in this cafeteria kitchen. Not safe for celiac diners.

bio.background: レガーレ (Regare) opened in April 2003 as the restaurant arm of ひめじ授産振興センター — a vocational and welfare support center run by NPO法人姫路地区手をつなぐ育成会, an NPO established to support people with intellectual disabilities and their families in the Himeji area. The center occupies はの屋敷 (Hano Yashiki), a historic building on the grounds of the former chief retainer's (家老) residence, directly opposite the main gate approach to Himeji Castle. The 1st floor serves as a welfare goods shop selling crafts and products made by local disability support facilities; the 2nd floor is the cafeteria restaurant.
bio.specialty: 姫路おでん (Himeji oden) — simmered oden served with a distinctive ginger-soy sauce dipping condiment unique to this city; also anago don (conger eel rice bowl) using local Seto Inland Sea eel
bio.philosophy: Community welfare through employment: the restaurant provides a meaningful workplace for people with disabilities while serving local heritage cuisine to castle visitors.

anecdotes.text: The center officially opened as 'レガーレ' in April 2003 (Heisei 15) under the NPO 姫路地区手をつなぐ育成会, combining a welfare goods retail space on the first floor with a local-cuisine restaurant on the second.
Diners on the 2nd floor can look directly out at Himeji Castle's white walls while eating — described by visitors as the closest place to the castle where you can eat Himeji oden.
The shop counter on the 1st floor sells 20+ flavours of soft-serve ice cream, with apple mango noted as a standout; the ice cream is takeout-only and popular with castle tourists.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information available on cross-contamination practices. This is a small cafeteria kitchen serving wheat-heavy dishes including battered akashi-yaki and breaded katsu-meshi — assume shared surfaces and utensils.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Himeji oden is served with ginger-soy sauce as a condiment; standard Japanese soy sauce (koikuchi shoyu) contains wheat. Oden broth itself is also likely seasoned with soy sauce. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternatives are mentioned in any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Oden broth is typically made with katsuobushi (bonito flakes) dashi. Somen and nyumen soups will contain fish-based broth. No plant-based broth option mentioned in any source.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling information found in any source. The center is run as a social welfare facility; there is no indication of allergy-aware staff training or menus.

positives.text: Soft-serve ice cream sold on the 1st floor (plain flavours such as vanilla or sesame) may be suitable for some dietary needs — but ingredients are unconfirmed.

caveats: No official website exists for this establishment. Hours from multiple sources are consistent at 10:00-15:30 (LO 15:00) but the irregular closure schedule (不定休) means it is best to call ahead: 079-283-2303. Cash-only status is unconfirmed — assume cash preferred given the welfare-center context. The Ekiten listing shows hours up to 17:00 but Tabelog and the blog review consistently show 15:30 LO; 15:30 close is treated as authoritative. Seating noted as 40 on Tabelog; tables are closely packed per blog reviewer.

address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市本町68-4
geocode_note: GSI geocode returned 兵庫県姫路市本町６８番地; coordinates fall ~530m north of the Station↔Castle midpoint, well within the 1.15km radius.
neighborhood: 本町 (Honmachi shopping arcade, east side of Eagle Himeji, Otemae-dori)
seats_text: Counter seats plus table seating on multiple sides; exact count not published. Solo-friendly counter available.
vegan_detail: Standard izakaya menu built around fish-based dashi, animal proteins, and egg. Documented dishes include shrimp mayo, chicken liver, sashimi, hamburger, wonton, and anchovy potato fries — no plant-only section is advertised. Some items may be incidentally plant-based (fried eggplant with grated yam appears on the menu), but hidden dashi and shared prep surfaces make vegan verification essential. Staff must be consulted dish-by-dish; full vegan accommodations are unlikely.
gf_detail: No GF claims made anywhere. Wheat is pervasive: standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) is used as a glaze on sashimi; tempura (seaweed tempura noted) appears on the menu; wonton uses wheat wrappers; fried items share a common fryer. No dedicated fryer and no allergen menu. Celiac diners should phone ahead; the kitchen cannot currently be considered safe without direct staff confirmation on each dish and potential cross-contamination from a busy one-person counter operation.
cultural_comfort_note: Solo owner-operated counter bar with a lively local regulars' atmosphere; no English signage or menu has been noted in any source, but the welcome is warm and non-intimidating for respectful visitors.

bio.background: Opened 24 April 2023 in the covered Honmachi shopping arcade in central Himeji, a short walk from Otemae-dori toward the castle. The restaurant is run by a single proprietor who manages the counter alone, cooking and serving simultaneously even during full-house sittings.
bio.specialty: The kitchen leans into inventive small plates — standouts noted across multiple visits include fried soft tofu in dashi (胡麻豆腐の揚げだし), black fried rice (黒チャーハン), a three-item omakase set that changes with the season, and a rotating sashimi special finished with soy glaze and seaweed tempura. Sake and shochu pairings are a particular strength, with rare unfiltered and ori-garami selections.
bio.philosophy: Creative izakaya cooking that prizes original flavour combinations and careful presentation without pretension; the atmosphere is intentionally dim and relaxed, with classic Japanese punk and new-wave playing in the background.

anecdotes.text: On opening evening the seats filled immediately; one blogger described the energy as 'an energetic festival bar atmosphere' with the lone proprietor fielding a full counter's worth of orders without help.
A spring visit captured the omakase trio: fluffy shrimp tempura, chicken liver stew, and dried persimmon with mascarpone — paired with Kōeikiku 'Anastasia Green' unfiltered raw sake.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated fryer confirmed. Tempura (seaweed tempura documented) and other fried items appear to share cooking oil. Cross-contamination from wheat flour is a realistic risk.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard soy sauce (wheat-containing) used as a glaze on sashimi and likely throughout the menu. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternative has been noted in any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish-based dashi is standard in Japanese izakaya cooking and is presumed present in broths, fried tofu dishes, and sauces. Shared prep surfaces in a one-person kitchen increase cross-contact risk.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, allergy card acceptance, or allergy-handling protocol has been documented in any review or listing. Restaurant contact: 079-263-7973.

positives.text: Owner-operator cooks everything from scratch to order, meaning ingredients are freshly combined rather than prepackaged — in principle this allows ingredient-level questions to be answered by the person who actually cooks the dish.

caveats: Single proprietor kitchen; hours may vary on holidays. No payment method specifics confirmed — assume card accepted but verify. Seat count not published on any platform. GF and vegan details rely on menu inference from review photos and blog reports; no direct allergen disclosure from the restaurant was found.

name_ja: ごはんとおバンザイ ジグザグ
cuisine: おばんざい, 家庭料理, 食堂
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市西二階町101-1
geocode_note: GSI AddressSearch returned [134.690567, 34.83231] for 兵庫県姫路市西二階町１０１番地. Approximately 165m from target center — well within 1.15km radius.
neighborhood: 西二階町
seats_text: カウンター席とテーブル席あり。詳細な席数は非公開。
cultural_comfort_note: A casual neighbourhood shokudo with no English signage or menu; Japanese literacy or pointing at dishes is needed, though the relaxed atmosphere makes solo visitors feel welcome.
vegan_detail: The 16-item rotating obanzai set includes around 10 vegetable-based small dishes alongside 4–6 meat or fish preparations. No vegan designation exists; miso soup is made with dashi likely containing bonito (katsuo), and several dishes will include fish, meat, or fish-based seasoning. Individual vegetable-only obanzai may be plantbased but are not labelled as such and shared kitchen contact is likely.
gf_detail: Traditional Japanese obanzai cooking relies heavily on soy sauce (shoyu, which contains wheat) and dashi. No gluten-free claims or accommodations are mentioned in any source. Dishes vary daily, making it impossible to predict wheat-free options without asking. Fried items appear on the rotating menu (cross-contamination risk from shared fryer likely). Those with celiac disease should ask staff in detail before ordering.

bio.background: Opened July 14, 2023 in Himeji's Nishinikaimachi shopping street — the second location from the team behind ごはんとプレート ジグザグ in the Higashiyama district. While the first shop leaned toward Western-style plate lunches, this branch pivots fully to obanzai, the Japanese tradition of home-style small dishes.
bio.specialty: 16種日替わりおばんざい定食 — a set of 16 rotating seasonal small dishes with rice and housemade miso soup, priced at around ¥1,980 and offered in limited quantities each day.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen describes its ethos as 'housewives pursuing the possibilities of obanzai with enthusiasm' — emphasising that ingredient selection alone can expand the dish infinitely. The owner sources produce by visiting vegetable vendors directly and uses inherited Bizen pottery from the family home to serve the meal.

anecdotes.text: One blogger reported that the shop sign is invisible from the street — the entrance sits deep inside a building — yet on a weekday morning the room was already over 90% full, with an overwhelmingly female clientele.
The miso soup is made from a blend of three different miso varieties combined with fresh dashi prepared daily, a detail highlighted by the Kiss Press review of the tofu hamburger set.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated GF fryer or preparation area is mentioned in any source. Fried items (e.g. fried chicken) appear on the rotating obanzai menu, making shared-fryer cross-contamination a realistic concern.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (shoyu, containing wheat) is a baseline ingredient in obanzai cooking; no tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternatives are mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Miso soup dashi is prepared daily and described as rich-flavoured, most likely containing bonito (katsuo). Meat and fish dishes share the kitchen with vegetable obanzai; no separation is noted.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No sources mention staff allergy training, allergen menus, or any protocol for dietary requests. Lunch operates without reservations, limiting pre-visit communication.

positives.text: The menu is built around seasonal vegetables, with roughly 10 of the 16 daily dishes being vegetable-based, giving plant-leaning diners more choice than a typical teishoku restaurant.

caveats: Hours include irregular closures beyond the fixed Thursday holiday — check Instagram (@ziguzagu.nishinikaimachi) before visiting. The 16-item set is limited quantity and sells out. Dinner service runs Wednesday, Friday, and Saturday only. GF and vegan status are inferred from the cuisine type; no official dietary labelling exists and the daily-rotating menu makes advance verification essential for anyone with strict dietary needs.

neighborhood: 本町 (Honcho), near Himeji Castle
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市本町68-100
geocode_note: GSI geocode on 兵庫県姫路市本町68番地 returned [134.687363, 34.835873]. Approximately 530m from target center (34.8331, 134.6921) — well within 1.15km radius. Address shares the historic Honmachi 68 block with Himeji Castle grounds.
seats_text: 16 seats total; intimate counter and table seating in a small wood-toned interior
cultural_comfort_note: A small, reservation-only French bistro where prior booking by phone is standard; some English may be possible but the environment and menus are primarily Japanese.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or options are advertised. The kitchen centers on French bistro cooking with prominent meat and fish dishes — Himeji wagyu, conger eel, smoked salmon, chicken, pâté. Hidden animal products (butter, cream, fish stock) are routine in this style. A guest with vegan needs would need to contact the restaurant well in advance; the omakase format may allow some flexibility but nothing is confirmed.
gf_detail: No gluten-free claim or accommodation is mentioned anywhere. French bistro cooking relies on wheat flour in sauces (roux), pastry (canelés are a signature item), and bread service. Ethnic-influenced dishes such as Bánh Mì and Nasi Goreng may add further gluten exposure. No dedicated fryer information found. Soy sauce use is plausible in the ethnic-fusion element of the menu but not confirmed. Guests with celiac disease should contact the restaurant directly before visiting; cross-contamination risk in a 16-seat open kitchen is non-trivial.

bio.background: Asami grew up in Himeji and began his culinary career at an ethnic cuisine restaurant in the city. He then spent approximately eight years training at a Michelin-starred French restaurant in Osaka (reported as ad hoc). A mentor's advice — "young chefs should return to their hometown, go to its market, and cook for its people" — drew him back to Himeji to open his own place rather than remain in Kobe or Osaka.
bio.origin: Opened January 22, 2020, in the Honmachi district immediately south of Himeji Castle.
bio.specialty: Herb- and spice-forward neo-bistro cooking that layers French technique with ethnic influences. Omakase courses change daily around peak-season local produce: vegetables from Yahomina Farm in Yumesaki, fish from Harima-nada waters, Tajima chicken, and Himeji wagyu. Signature items include pâté de campagne with candied kumquat, grilled conger eel, and Bordeaux-style canelés.
bio.philosophy: Asami aims to fulfill hearts as well as stomachs, offering a moment of genuine joy. He resists orthodox categorisation, blending French structure with international flavour references so that no single style dominates. Menus are justified course by course — each dish must have a reason for existing.
roles: sommelier (partner/wife, trained under Osaka natural-wine specialists)

anecdotes.text: The name Aigrette comes from the French word for white heron, a direct homage to Himeji Castle's poetic name Hakurojo (White Heron Castle). Asami drew a parallel to chef Fernand Point, who named his legendary La Pyramide after the Roman ruins beside his restaurant — rooting the restaurant's identity in the landscape it inhabits.
The restaurant shares the famous Honmachi 68 address with Himeji Castle itself — historically significant real estate that places the dining room on the edge of the castle's own grounds.
Each month on the 22nd (the restaurant's opening anniversary date) a special eggplant curry dish appears on the menu, and on the 20th a natural wine evening event is held.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information is available about cross-contamination controls. With only 16 seats and an open counter kitchen, shared preparation surfaces for wheat-containing dishes (pastry, flour-thickened sauces, Bánh Mì bread) are likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu blends French and ethnic Asian influences (Hainanese chicken rice, Nasi Goreng, Bánh Mì), making standard soy sauce a plausible ingredient, though its specific use has not been confirmed in any source reviewed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All reviewed sources confirm the menu is built around meat, fish, and classical French dairy. No vegan-safe preparation protocol is mentioned.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling procedures or staff training information appears in any source. Reservations are made by phone the day before, which is the logical opportunity to communicate dietary needs.

positives.text: Omakase course format (menu decided day-of based on freshest available ingredients) may allow ingredient-level customisation if communicated clearly at reservation time.

caveats: Hours sourced from Tabelog (11:30-15:00/18:00-21:00 with L.O. 13:00/19:00) and corroborated by Gnavi/tanosu; the tanosu article listed slightly different L.O. times (12:30/19:30) which may reflect an earlier operational period. Cash-only flag from myhimejimap blog conflicts with Tabelog's indication that cards are accepted — Tabelog marked as credit cards accepted so cash_only=false, but guests should verify at booking. Seat count of 16 from Gnavi. Price range has shifted upward over time; TableCheck lists current lunch ¥5,000–¥10,000 and dinner ¥8,000–¥12,000, while older Tabelog/tanosu references show lower figures. No dietary (GF/vegan) information found in any source.

address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市二階町32
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 兵庫県姫路市二階町32 returned lat 34.831528, lng 134.693314 — within the target box (34.823–34.843, 134.679–134.705) and well inside the 1.15km radius from the Himeji Castle–Station corridor.
neighborhood: 西二階町・お溝筋商店街（山陽姫路駅から約423m）
seats_text: カウンター3席、4人テーブル2卓（計11席）の小さな店
cultural_comfort_note: A small neighbourhood izakaya tucked into an arcade; no English menu noted and staff interaction is casual Japanese — a few words of Japanese and pointing will get you through.
