vegan_detail: Standard izakaya cooking with dashi-based broths, soy-based sauces, sashimi, tempura, and grilled fish all prominent. No vegan menu or labelling found. Chawanmushi (egg custard) and katsuobushi-likely dashi are standard. Vegan diners should enquire dish-by-dish; hidden fish-based stock is the main risk.
gf_detail: This is a conventional izakaya. Wheat appears throughout: standard soy sauce (含小麦) is almost certainly used in marinades and sauces, tempura batter is wheat-based, and tatsuta-age (fried chicken) uses wheat starch. No dedicated gluten-free menu or fryer information was found in any source. Celiac diners must enquire specifically before ordering; cross-contamination risk is significant in a small open kitchen.

bio.background: 酒ト飯 みすずは2023年12月8日、姫路市のお溝筋商店街にオープンした小さな居酒屋。カウンター3席と4人テーブル2卓という11席の小空間で、平日は日替わりランチ（1,000円）も提供する。
bio.specialty: 日替わりランチ（刺身・天ぷら・茶碗蒸しなど）と夜の居酒屋メニュー

anecdotes.text: Tabelog reviewers noted the owner has a relaxed but skilled presence, with one visitor praising the 'heartfelt, health-conscious flavours' of the daily lunch.
When draft beer ran out during one visit, staff offered bottled beer at the same draft price — a small gesture that earned reviewer praise.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura and tatsuta-age (wheat-battered or wheat-starch fried chicken) are menu staples; no dedicated fryer information was found, so shared-fryer cross-contamination must be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce mentioned in any source; standard wheat-containing soy sauce is the baseline assumption for marinades, dipping sauces, and braised dishes.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Chawanmushi (egg custard) and dashi-based broth dishes are standard on the menu; fish-based dashi stock is the default in Japanese izakaya cooking and likely present across multiple dishes.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling policy or allergen menu was found in any source reviewed. Diners with dietary restrictions should call ahead (079-263-7936) or enquire in Japanese on arrival.

positives.text: Reservations accepted by phone or online, giving diners the opportunity to ask about ingredients in advance.

caveats: No chef name or owner background confirmed from any source. Hours and closed days are as listed on Tabelog/Hitosara as of research date; spot-confirm before visiting as the restaurant is independently operated and hours may shift. Cash only confirmed across multiple sources. No English-language menu or allergy documentation found.

cuisine: Coffee specialist — siphon-brewed coffee, almond toast, Japanese kissaten (coffee house)
address_ja: 〒670-0922 兵庫県姫路市二階町49
geocode_note: GSI address-search on 兵庫県姫路市二階町４９番地 returned [134.692871, 34.831478]. Within target box (lat 34.823–34.843, lng 134.679–134.705). Approximately 383m from Sanyo-Himeji Station, within 1.15km of Himeji Castle.
neighborhood: 二階町 (Nikacho) — Miyuki Street shopping arcade
hours_raw: 7:00〜17:00（L.O. 16:30）、定休日：木曜日（祝日の場合は営業）。Official site lists closing at 17:00; some sources list 17:30 or 18:00 — verify on official site before visiting as hours may vary seasonally.
closed_days: 木曜日（祝日の場合は営業）— Thursdays; open if Thursday falls on a public holiday
seats_text: 18 counter seats facing the siphon bar, 18 table seats; all non-smoking
cultural_comfort_note: An English menu is available; the counter-seat format with visible siphon brewing is visually self-explanatory and welcoming to first-time visitors.
vegan_detail: No vegan options. The morning set (7:00–11:00) includes a boiled egg with any drink order. Almond toast uses butter in the almond spread. Standard kissaten menu with no plant-based alternatives noted. Coffee itself is vegan, but all food items contain eggs or dairy.
gf_detail: No gluten-free options. The signature almond toast is wheat bread topped with almond butter, almond powder, diced almonds, and sliced almonds. The morning set includes wheat toast. This is a wheat-centric kissaten with no GF alternatives noted anywhere. Celiac diners should avoid all food items; siphon-brewed black coffee is the only naturally GF option on the menu.

bio.background: Hamamoto Coffee opened in 1975 on Himeji's Miyuki Street arcade. The shop began not as a cafe but as a greengrocer — the founding family ran a vegetable stall until the spread of supermarkets made it unviable. Rather than close, they pivoted to coffee, bringing the same emphasis on fresh sourcing and quality materials that defined their vegetable trade. The second-generation owner, Takuya Hamamoto, has carried this philosophy forward, summarising it as: "materials matter, just as with vegetables."
bio.origin: Himeji, Hyogo, Japan — family business now in its second generation
bio.specialty: Siphon-brewed Hamamoto Blend coffee and Himeji's iconic almond toast — wheat bread spread with a house-made almond butter of almond powder, diced almonds, and sliced almonds, then toasted to a fragrant finish
bio.philosophy: Light-roast, fresh beans sourced globally to preserve the coffee's native bitterness, acidity, and sweetness. Over 200 seasonal original blends have been rotated quarterly across 30-plus years. The owner adjusts water temperature daily so that each cup tastes consistent yet never tiring.

anecdotes.text: The almond toast now beloved across Himeji traces its origins to Narita Coffee, a local bean retailer that obtained the recipe from a pastry shop over 30 years ago and shared it with kissaten clients — Hamamoto Coffee became one of its most celebrated interpreters.

The L-shaped marble counter and a world map on the wall set against copper engravings of Brazilian coffee farms give the interior a distinctly Showa-era atmosphere that regulars say feels unchanged across decades.

The owner is said to recognise regulars' coffee preferences by sight alone, adjusting their cup before they order, embodying a "trust with customers above all else" ethos that has sustained the shop for 50 years.
Selected for the national "Kissaten 100 Famous Shops 2021" (名曲喫茶100選) list, cementing its status as one of Japan's benchmark traditional coffee houses.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated GF preparation area or allergen segregation noted anywhere. This is a bread-and-toast-centric kissaten; wheat flour is central to the menu and cross-contamination must be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy sauce in the menu items identified. Coffee shop menu (coffee, toast, egg) does not appear to use soy sauce. However, no allergen declaration is publicly available.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Eggs and dairy (almond butter spread) are used in core menu items throughout the kitchen; no plant-based separation noted.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling policy, allergen menu, or staff allergy training information was found across any source. Phone contact (079-282-2233) is the only avenue to inquire.

positives.text: Siphon-brewed black coffee (no milk, no soy sauce, no gluten ingredients) is naturally gluten-free and vegan as a standalone item — the safest choice for restricted diners who want to experience the shop.

caveats: Closing time varies across sources (17:00 on official site, 17:30 on Tabelog, 18:00 on tourism board). The official site (hamamoto-coffee.com) should be treated as authoritative; seasonal changes are noted. The harimap.com source mentioned Tuesdays as a possible closed day, but every other source (Tabelog, official site, tourism board, travel blogs) confirms Thursdays — Tuesday is likely an error in that one source. Hours marked irregular to flag the discrepancy.

address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市本町68
geocode_note: GSI address search on 兵庫県姫路市本町68番地 returned lat 34.835873 lng 134.687363 — approx 514m from target centre (34.8331, 134.6921), well within 1.15km radius.
neighborhood: 姫路城南（山陽姫路駅徒歩10分）
seats_text: 11席（カウンター7、テーブル4）
hours_raw: 11:30〜16:00（売り切れ次第閉店）、木・金定休
cultural_comfort_note: Small counter shop with limited English; welcoming to foreign tourists (vegan bean curry was explicitly created with international visitors in mind), but staff communication will be in Japanese.
vegan_detail: One of the three rotating daily curries — a thick fried tofu and bean/spinach curry — is explicitly made without any animal products and was developed with vegan and allergy-conscious tourists in mind. The chicken and shrimp green curries are not vegan. No hidden-dashi or bonito details are confirmed for accompaniments (rice, pickles, papad), so ask on arrival.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or certification. The curries are spice-based rather than roux-based, which reduces some GF risk, but no explicit claims are made about wheat-free preparation. Soy sauce (containing wheat) may be used in side items such as hinepon (chicken ponzu) or pickles. Shared kitchen with no stated dedicated fryer or cross-contamination controls. Celiac diners should call ahead or ask staff directly.
caveats: Hours confirmed 11:30–16:00 but the shop closes earlier when sold out, which is common; one source (4travel) listed evening hours suggesting possible format change — verify current hours via Instagram or phone before visiting. Exact seat count and floor layout vary slightly across sources.

bio.background: The owner trained across Italian, creative Japanese cuisine, and yakitori before finding his calling in spice curry. He opened spiceスエヒロ in 2015 near Himeji Castle, handcrafting every piece of the interior himself — counter, tables, wall-mounted spice racks — and designing the shop's signage and silk-screen printed T-shirts. The name 'Suehiro' was not chosen for style but to honor an elderly woman who ran a modest bread and grocery shop in the Aboshi district of Himeji for 40 years; the owner wanted no pretension in the name. He has travelled to Sri Lanka but not India, and that South Asian sensibility filters into his South Indian-inflected spice style.
bio.specialty: 三種スパイスカレー（チキン・グリーン・豆カレー）のあいがけ
bio.philosophy: Curry that hits with impact but can be eaten every day without tiring of it — built on slow-caramelised Awaji Island onions and over ten hand-blended spices, balanced so sweetness arrives first and the heat follows.

anecdotes.text: The owner described his creative process with disarming simplicity: 'I just cooked it in my head, tried it, and made it so I could eat it.' The resulting chicken curry uses South Awaji caramelised onions as its base, cooked long and slow before the spice blend is added.

The vegan tofu and bean curry was deliberately developed to serve foreign tourists visiting Himeji Castle who have dietary restrictions or follow a plant-based diet — a rare act of intentional inclusivity for a tiny 11-seat shop.
Everything in the dining room was made by the owner's own hands: counter, tables, and the wall of spice bottles that greets you at the door, giving the compact space a handmade warmth.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on dedicated fryer or separate preparation surfaces. Papad (Indian lentil cracker) is served as a topping — traditional papad is naturally gluten-free, but shared kitchen risk is unknown.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hinepon (chicken ponzu) and pickled accompaniments may contain soy sauce with wheat. No explicit wheat-free soy sauce or tamari use is stated anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The vegan bean curry is served alongside chicken and shrimp curries in a shared kitchen. No statement about separate utensils or prep surfaces for the vegan option.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling policy or staff training is documented. The shop is a tiny owner-run counter with 11 seats — direct communication with the owner-chef is the realistic path for dietary questions.

positives.text: The tofu and bean curry was explicitly created without any animal products for vegan and allergy-aware tourists — demonstrating owner awareness of dietary needs, unusual for a small Japanese curry counter.
Spice-based curries (rather than Japanese roux blocks) avoid the wheat-laden roux found in most Japanese curry restaurants, reducing one common gluten source.

address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市魚町1
neighborhood: 魚町 (Uocho)
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI AddressSearch on 兵庫県姫路市魚町１番地; coordinates fall within target box (lat 34.823–34.843, lng 134.679–134.705) and approximately 336m from target center point.
hours_raw: 17:00–23:00 daily; closed days not confirmed across any source
closed_days: Not confirmed — no regular closed day listed on Tabelog, HotPepper, or Hitosara; call ahead to verify
seats_text: Counter of around 10 seats encircling the charcoal grill, plus two table sets seating 8 each; intimate and communal
cultural_comfort_note: A no-frills neighbourhood izakaya filled with Himeji regulars; staff unlikely to speak English and menus are Japanese only — pointing or gesturing at other diners' dishes works fine.
vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around charcoal-grilled fish, meat, and chicken skewers, plus oden simmered in a dashi broth almost certainly made from bonito and kombu. No plant-only dishes were mentioned in any review or listing, and the kitchen has no track record of accommodating vegan requests.
gf_detail: Standard Japanese soy sauce (containing wheat) is the base condiment for both the robata glazes and the oden broth. No gluten-free soy sauce alternative, no dedicated fryer data, and no staff allergy-handling history is documented anywhere. Celiac or seriously wheat-sensitive diners should avoid.

bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled fish (hokke/atka mackerel, saury, shishamo) and oden, served counter-side by the grill

anecdotes.text: A blogger described the counter seats around the hearth as perfect for groups of eight, with grilled rice balls glazed in ginger-soy sauce as a regular order and dishes delivered theatrically on the long robata paddle.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information available; a shared kitchen grill environment handling soy-marinated fish and meat presents high cross-contamination risk for wheat-sensitive diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Robata-yaki marinades and oden broth in Japanese izakayas standardly use wheat-containing soy sauce (koikuchi shoyu); no tamari or GF alternative has been documented for this restaurant.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All grilling surfaces and broth pots are shared with fish and meat; no vegan cross-contact mitigation documented.

caveats: Closed days are unconfirmed across all sources — Tabelog, HotPepper, and Hitosara all list none, but this may reflect missing data rather than truly no closure. Hours (17:00–23:00) are consistent across sources but should be confirmed by phone before visiting. The most recent Tabelog review is from October 2025, confirming operation at that date. No official website was found. Seat count is estimated from blog descriptions (counter ~8–10 + two 8-seat tables).

name_ja: まねきのえきそば 在来線下り店
name_en: Maneki no Ekisoba Zairaisen Kudari Ten
cuisine: えきそば・立ち食いそば
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市駅前町188-1 JR姫路駅 山陽本線 下りホーム
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI AddressSearch on 駅前町188 Himeji; result lands inside JR Himeji Station concourse, well within the 1.15 km radius of Himeji Castle.
neighborhood: 駅構内（JR姫路駅 在来線下りホーム）
vegan_detail: The broth is a fish-based Japanese dashi made with bonito (katsuobushi) and mackerel (saba) powder, confirmed in the frozen retail product ingredient list. Soy sauce (which contains wheat) is also in the broth. There are no plant-based broth options, and no vegan menu items are listed. Kitsune (aburaage tofu) topping exists but sits in the fish dashi.
gf_detail: The signature noodle is a Chinese-style alkaline (kansui) noodle made with wheat flour — wheat is listed as the primary ingredient. The broth contains powdered soy sauce, which is wheat-based. Tempura toppings are battered in wheat. Shrimp tempura cross-contact is noted even in the retail version. There is no gluten-free alternative on the menu. This is not safe for celiac or gluten-sensitive diners.
hours_raw: 06:00–24:00 daily (no regular closing day); source: Maneki Foods official shop list and Tabelog listing
seats_text: Standing (tachigui) counter only; approximately 13 people at once. No chairs.
cultural_comfort_note: A fast-paced standing noodle counter inside the train station; ordering is self-service at the counter, no English menu, and the format assumes familiarity with Japanese station dining conventions.

bio.background: Maneki Foods (まねき食品株式会社) has operated at JR Himeji Station since 1888, originally as a railway bento seller. In the immediate post-war years of 1949, with wheat flour tightly rationed, the company's cooks experimented with konnyaku and buckwheat powders to make a noodle that could be served quickly to passengers. Through trial and error they landed on an unconventional pairing: yellow Chinese-style alkaline (kansui) noodles served in a traditional Japanese fish dashi. The dish was named 'ekisoba' (station soba) and launched on October 19, 1949 on the station platforms.
bio.specialty: Ekisoba — yellow kansui noodles in a proprietary bonito-and-mackerel dashi broth, priced under ¥500. Tempura ekisoba and kitsune (fried tofu) ekisoba are the perennial favourites.
bio.philosophy: Speed, affordability, and a sense of place: a bowl for the commuter with minutes to spare and a long journey ahead.
bio.origin: Himeji, Hyogo, Japan — JR Himeji Station platforms since 1949

anecdotes.text: The dish is often described as a deliberate 'mismatch' — Chinese noodles in a Japanese broth — yet that unlikely combination became Himeji's most enduring soul food, outlasting fashions for 75 years.
To celebrate 75 years in March 2024, the Zairaisen Kudari store reopened after renovation and offered a limited gold-leaf shrimp tempura ekisoba for ¥750, capped at 75 bowls a day for seven days.
Afternoon discounts (14:00–17:00) shave ¥40 off the standard bowl, a legacy perk that has quietly survived every renovation.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat is the primary ingredient in the noodles (wheat flour, starch, salt per retail ingredient declaration). The broth contains powdered soy sauce, which is also wheat-based. Tempura toppings are wheat-battered. Gluten is present throughout every component of the dish.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dashi broth contains 粉末醤油 (powdered soy sauce), confirmed in the product ingredient list: '食塩、粉末醤油、砂糖、節粉末（かつお、さば）、昆布粉末'. Standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is made from bonito (katsuobushi) and mackerel (saba) powder; no plant-based broth alternative exists. Kitsune tofu topping is cooked in the same fish dashi. Shrimp (ebi) is listed as a declared allergen in the product.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling information, allergen substitution, or dietary accommodation policy has been found for this counter. It is a high-throughput standing noodle station with no à la carte modification culture.

positives.text: The shop has been transparent about ingredients through its retail product line, which publishes a full allergen declaration (wheat, shrimp, egg) and a complete ingredient list, giving diners a clear picture of what is in the bowl.

caveats: Hours sourced from Tabelog and Maneki official shop list; Tabelog shows 06:00–23:00 while the official shop list shows 06:00–24:00. The official site figure (24:00) is used as primary. The retail frozen product ingredient list was used as the best available proxy for on-site allergen composition; live station broth may vary slightly. No dedicated allergen sheet for the in-station counter was located.

name_ja: オーガニックラボ リベルテ
cuisine: オーガニック野菜・ジビエ料理（有機野菜と鹿肉料理）
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市本町68
geocode_note: GSI address search for 兵庫県姫路市本町68 returned coordinates lat 34.835873, lng 134.687363 — within target box (34.823–34.843, 134.679–134.705).
neighborhood: 本町（Honmachi）
seats_text: 10席（カウンター席のみ）
cultural_comfort_note: 10-seat counter-only room with no English signage or staff language indicated; basic Japanese needed to navigate the daily menu and ordering.
vegan_detail: Game meat (venison) is the signature and central focus of the menu — deer menchi-katsu, deer hamburger, deer meatball cream pasta, deer spice tomato curry. Not a vegan or vegetarian restaurant. Organic vegetable sides are present but accompany meat-based dishes.
gf_detail: No gluten-free claims are made. Menu includes pasta (wheat), fried chicken (likely wheat-battered), and daily soups of unknown composition. Soy sauce is almost certainly used in a restaurant at this level without explicit GF labeling. Counter-only 10-seat format offers an opportunity to ask, but no dedicated GF protocols are documented.

bio.background: Opened August 5, 2022 in the Honmachi district of Himeji, roughly 583 meters from Sanyo-Himeji Station. The restaurant operates out of a small 10-seat counter space with a hidden-gem character.
bio.specialty: Venison (deer) dishes prepared with organic vegetables — including deer menchi-katsu, hand-formed deer hamburger, deer meatball cream sauce pasta, and deer spice tomato curry.
bio.philosophy: Centers on organic sourcing, pairing locally-sourced wild game (ジビエ) with organic vegetables and organic products.

anecdotes.text: The single Tabelog review (November 2022) describes an organic daily lunch of fried chicken with ponzu, salad, side dishes, egg soup, and rice — reflecting the restaurant's flexibility beyond the venison-focused dinner menu.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No information published on soy sauce type used. Menu includes pasta and likely battered items, indicating standard wheat-containing soy sauce is probable.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Venison is the restaurant's signature ingredient and is present across the main menu; cross-contact with game meat is unavoidable for vegan diners.

positives.text: Organic vegetable sourcing is a stated commitment; the small 10-seat counter format allows direct conversation with whoever is cooking, potentially enabling custom requests.

caveats: Only one Tabelog review exists (from November 2022, shortly after opening). No official website or social media account was confirmed. No gluten-free, vegan, or allergen handling information has been published by the restaurant. The menu prominently features wheat-containing dishes (pasta, likely battered items). Cash only with PayPay as the only cashless option.

name_ja: ガネーシャダイニング
cuisine: インド・ネパール料理
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市南駅前町125 ビエラ姫路
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 兵庫県姫路市南駅前町１２５番地 returned [134.690079, 34.826347]; 773m from target center — within 1.15km radius and inside target lat/lng box.
neighborhood: 駅前町（ビエラ姫路内、山陽姫路駅南1分）
seats_text: 37席（カウンター・テーブル席あり）
cultural_comfort_note: Operated by South Asian staff in a busy station-side mall setting; Japanese menus with some basic English labeling typical of Indian curry chains; non-Japanese visitors have reported positive experiences but English fluency is not confirmed.
vegan_detail: Several plant-based curry options exist on the menu — vegetable curry (ベジタブル ¥820), chickpea masala (チャナマサラ ¥970), lentil dal (ダルフライ ¥905), eggplant-potato (アルベイガン ¥920) — but dairy-based curries such as spinach paneer and butter paneer are also listed. Naan contains dairy (butter). Whether ghee or dairy is used in nominally vegan curries is not confirmed; ask staff before ordering.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or certification found. Naan is wheat-based; rice is available as an alternative. Standard Indian curry bases (spices, tomato, cream) can be wheat-free, but no allergen chart was published and cross-contamination risk from shared naan prep surfaces is unknown. Soy sauce does not appear to be a primary ingredient in this cuisine style, but no staff allergy protocol is documented. Celiacs must ask in detail before ordering.

bio.background: ガネーシャダイニングは2022年7月、JR姫路駅直結のビエラ姫路商業施設内にオープンしたインド・ネパール料理専門店。ガネーシャとはヒンドゥー教の知恵と繁栄の神の名で、店名にその精神が込められている。
bio.specialty: 22種以上のカレー（辛さ5段階調整可）と石窯焼きナン
bio.philosophy: 本格スパイスカレーを手軽なセットメニューで提供し、昼夜・家族・一人客を幅広く迎える姿勢。スパイス少なめのネパール風カレーも用意し、辛さが苦手な人にも対応する。

anecdotes.text: 2022年6月の地元メディア報道では、オープン前の店舗工事中にカレーやナン、タンドリーチキンのポスターが貼られ、地元民の期待を集めた。

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information found about dedicated preparation surfaces or separate utensils for gluten-free dishes. Naan is baked in a tandoor oven and prepared with wheat dough; shared kitchen risk cannot be excluded.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce does not appear to be used in the standard Indian/Nepalese curry preparations here, but no allergen chart has been published so this cannot be confirmed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy (butter, cream, paneer) is used extensively in several curries. Whether plant-based curries share cooking vessels with dairy-based ones is unknown; staff should be asked.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Tabelog notes 'allergy information available' but no published allergen matrix was found during research. Recommend asking staff directly in Japanese.

positives.text: Multiple plant-based curry options (vegetable, chickpea, lentil, eggplant-potato) are standard menu items, not special requests, making vegetarian ordering straightforward.

caveats: Hours for Saturday/Sunday split differs from weekdays (afternoon session starts 16:30 not 17:00). Year-end/New Year closures vary annually — confirm before visiting. No allergen chart found online; celiac/vegan diners should call ahead (079-284-0371).

address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市南町59
geocode_note: GSI address-search hit for 兵庫県姫路市南町５９番地; coordinates 34.829456,134.690475 fall ~430m from the Castle-Station midpoint, well within the 1.15km radius.
neighborhood: 南町 / 山陽姫路駅すぐ
seats_text: 17席（カウンターのみ）
cultural_comfort_note: Counter-only, Showa-era shop with no English menu or signage; Japanese ordering is expected but the staff are described as courteous and the environment is welcoming to solo diners.
vegan_detail: The broth is built on chicken bones (鶏ガラ) with a small amount of pork bones and is seasoned with salt and soy sauce. Toppings include house-made chashu pork. Gyoza contain meat. No plant-based options are offered or advertised.
gf_detail: The ramen uses custom thin wheat noodles. The broth contains standard Japanese soy sauce, which is brewed with wheat. No gluten-free noodle substitutes or allergen accommodations are available. The kitchen is not gluten-free in any capacity. Not safe for celiac diners.

bio.background: Founded in 1960 (Showa 35), Shinseiken is a Himeji institution that has occupied the same narrow shopfront in the Minamimachi covered arcade — steps from Sanyo-Himeji Station — for over six decades. The counter-only space seats just 17 and is decorated with vintage photographs of old Himeji streetscapes, evoking the yatai (street stall) culture of the era in which it was born.
bio.specialty: Shio ramen (塩ラーメン) — a clear, golden chicken-and-pork-bone broth seasoned with salt and a touch of soy sauce, served with thin custom noodles, chashu pork, bean sprouts, menma, and green onion. Wonton noodles (ワンタン麺) are a long-standing favourite.
bio.philosophy: The original recipe has remained unchanged since the shop opened. The proprietor (referred to by regulars as 大将, the master) finishes each bowl with a shake of white pepper at the counter, a nod to the street-stall tradition the shop grew out of.

anecdotes.text: Locals describe making Shinseiken their final stop of a night out — a bowl of the light, clear soup as the perfect shime (end-of-evening meal) after drinking in the city.
A refrigerator unit added during a renovation took away roughly five seats, yet the shop still routinely draws queues; the quick table-turn keeps waits short.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles are cooked in shared broth and water; no separate preparation area for allergen-free orders exists in this small counter-only kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce (醤油), which contains wheat as a primary fermentation ingredient. Salt ramen style does not mean wheat-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is animal-based (chicken and pork stock, chashu pork, gyoza with meat filling). There is no plant-based preparation track.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, allergy handling protocol, or dietary accommodation has been documented at this shop. Cash-only counter service with no reservation system suggests limited ability to arrange special orders in advance.

caveats: Closed days vary across sources (Thursday confirmed on most; Tuesday also cited on Retty). Hours also vary slightly (11:00 vs 12:00 opening). Call 079-222-5439 before visiting to confirm current schedule. No official website exists.

name_ja: 驛麺 らぁ～めん京 山陽姫路駅店
cuisine: Ramen — Kyoto-style Gion white broth (鶏白湯) with low-temperature aged thin egg noodles
address_ja: 〒670-0912 兵庫県姫路市南町1 山陽姫路駅2F 改札横
geocode_note: Geocoded to 兵庫県姫路市南町 via GSI AddressSearch; coordinates resolve to the Sanyo-Himeji Station area, 491 m from target center — well within 1.15 km radius and inside the target bounding box.
neighborhood: 山陽姫路駅構内 (Inside Sanyo-Himeji Station)
seats_text: Counter seating only, 15 seats
hours_raw: 11:00〜22:00（ラストオーダー21:30）、元旦のみ休業。源の情報（グルナビ）は11:00〜20:00（LO 19:45）と異なり、リニューアル後に変更の可能性あり。要確認。
closed_days: 元旦のみ（January 1st only）; otherwise open daily
cultural_comfort_note: Ticket-machine ordering (券売機), counter-only seating inside a train station; no English menu or staff language support noted in any source — Japanese navigation expected.
vegan_detail: All bowls are built on chicken bone (鶏ガラ) or pork bone broth with soy-sauce tare; standard toppings include chashu pork and soft-boiled egg. No plant-based or vegan option is listed or mentioned in any source. Hidden animal products are present in every menu item.
gf_detail: Standard ramen: wheat-based egg noodles (細麺/卵麺) are the defining product. Soy-sauce tare contains wheat. Gyoza wrappers and rice-bowl sauces add further wheat exposure. No gluten-free noodle substitute or dedicated allergen protocol is mentioned anywhere. Not safe for celiac or wheat-allergic diners.

bio.background: Part of the ラーメン京（みやこ）chain headquartered in Kyoto, with multiple outlets across Hyogo, Kyoto, Nara, and Shiga prefectures. The Sanyo-Himeji station branch operates on the 2nd floor directly beside the ticket gates, catering to commuters seeking a fast, inexpensive bowl.
bio.specialty: 京都熟成細麺 — low-temperature aged thin egg noodles served in a Gion-inspired chicken-bone white broth (ぎをん白湯), topped with chashu pork and a soft-boiled egg. Value set menus (from ¥660) pair a bowl with a rice dish.
bio.philosophy: Quick, affordable Kyoto-style ramen for transit passengers; the broth is described as light yet complex, revealing more depth as you eat.

anecdotes.text: The shop underwent a renovation closure October 6–16 2025 and relaunched on October 17 2025 under the updated name 驛麺 らぁ～めん京, introducing new value-set menus.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles are cooked in shared water; gyoza, ramen, and rice-bowl items with soy-sauce-based sauces all share the same small kitchen. No allergen separation is described anywhere.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy-sauce tare (醤油ダレ) is a base component of the broth; conventional Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. The official site describes the soup as 鶏ガラ豚骨醤油スープ.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every broth contains animal products (chicken bone, pork bone, or dashi); there is no plant-based option and therefore no vegan-safe item on the menu.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Tabelog notes アレルギー表示あり (allergy information available), suggesting customers should inquire directly at the restaurant (TEL: 079-223-5875), but no protocol or allergen sheet is documented in any fetched source.

caveats: Hours conflict across sources: Gnavi lists 11:00–20:00 (LO 19:45) while the official chain site and other listings say 11:00–22:00 (LO 21:30); the post-Oct 2025 renovation may have changed hours — call ahead to confirm. Payment: Tabelog indicates cash only; one Retty review mentions PayPay — verify on site. The geocode resolves to 兵庫県姫路市南町 (chō level), which is the station address block; actual station-platform coordinates may differ slightly but remain well within the 1.15 km target radius.

name_ja: 酒饌亭 灘菊かっぱ亭
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市東駅前町58
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI AddressSearch on 兵庫県姫路市東駅前町58; result lat 34.828747 / lng 134.693069 is approximately 460m from the target center (34.8331, 134.6921), well within the 1.15km radius.
neighborhood: 小溝筋 (Kodomizo-suji) / 東駅前町 — ~4–5 min walk from JR/Sanyo Himeji Station
hours_raw: Mon/Tue/Thu/Fri: 11:30–13:30 (L.O. 13:00), 16:30–21:00 (L.O. 21:00); Sat: 11:30–21:00; Sun/Holidays: 11:30–20:00; Closed Wed. Hours vary slightly across sources; Tabelog and official site are authoritative.
seats_text: 29 seats total: 11 counter, 18 table
cultural_comfort_note: An English menu is available and staff are noted for patience with visitors, but the restaurant is a local izakaya with a primarily Japanese-speaking clientele; basic Japanese greetings will go a long way.
vegan_detail: No vegan options identified. The signature 50-year dashi broth is almost certainly fish-based (katsuobushi/konbu), the black oden is dressed with ginger-soy sauce, and oden ingredients typically include fish cakes (chikuwa, hanpen). The white oden sauce is sake lees (sakekasu) — plant-origin, but the broth underneath is fish-based. No vegan-adapted dishes have been documented in any source.
gf_detail: Standard Japanese soy sauce (shoyu) contains wheat and is the basis for the signature ginger-soy dipping sauce served with black oden. The dashi broth is fish-based and likely contains wheat-containing soy sauce. Many typical oden ingredients (chikuwa, hanpen, satsuma-age fish cakes) contain wheat starch. The sake lees (sakekasu) white oden sauce is made from sake, which is rice-based and generally gluten-free, but the underlying dashi broth and other kitchen preparations are not controlled for gluten. No dedicated GF protocols, separate fryers, or allergen handling procedures have been documented anywhere. Celiac and gluten-sensitive diners should not eat here without direct staff confirmation of every component.

bio.background: Nadagiku Sake Brewery (姫路灘菊酒造) was founded in 1910 (Meiji 43) by Kawashi Shuzosuke in Himeji, celebrated its centenary in 2010, and has been run by the Kawashi family ever since. The brewery operates Kappatei as a showcase for its sake alongside Himeji's signature local cuisine.
bio.specialty: Himeji-style oden in two forms: large-skewer black oden with ginger-soy sauce (a regional custom unique to Himeji) and the house-original white oden — large skewers blanketed in a snowy, umami-rich sauce made from the brewery's own sake lees (sakekasu). The 50-year-old dashi broth is the restaurant's most closely guarded asset.
bio.philosophy: The brewery's guiding principle is 'harmony between sake and food culture.' Almost all Nadagiku sake stays within Himeji, distributed through the brewery's own restaurants so that guests drink local and eat local in the same sitting.

anecdotes.text: Every chair in the dining room is a real festival taiko drum. The brewery president, a passionate festival devotee, sourced the drums from actual processions; protective paper talismans are tucked inside each drum shell so guests don't sit on the drumsticks.

Toji (master brewer) Kawashi Misa, the brewery's third daughter, became in 2010 the first female Nanbu toji in western Japan — joining roughly three women among some 200 members of Japan's most prestigious brewer guild. She trained under veteran master Kamata Shohei and now leads a three-person team. Her motto: 'Perseverance is strength.' The MISA33 sake line bears her name.

The brewery pivoted in 1994 from bulk supply to Nada's major producers toward small-batch (500 kg per batch) artisanal labels. Today roughly 90 percent of all Nadagiku sake is consumed within Himeji itself.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on cross-contamination controls is available from any source. The kitchen prepares wheat-containing items (soy sauce, fish cakes) alongside all oden, making cross-contact likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Ginger soy sauce (生姜醤油) is the traditional Himeji-style dipping condiment for black oden and is central to the dining experience here. Standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat. No wheat-free soy sauce alternative is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The 50-year-old dashi broth is the restaurant's foundation; no plant-based dashi alternative is mentioned in any source. Fish cakes and animal-derived ingredients are standard oden components.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling protocol has been documented. The restaurant accepts reservations via LINE, which could be a channel to communicate dietary needs in advance, but no policy is stated.

positives.text: English menu is available, and staff are described as friendly and patient in explaining recommendations to guests unfamiliar with the menu — a helpful starting point for dietary conversations.

caveats: Hours differ slightly across Tabelog, the official site, and the tourism board; the split-session weekday hours (11:30–13:30 / 16:30–21:00) represent the best consensus. Confirm current hours before visiting. The 50-year-old dashi broth claim appears in multiple independent sources but is unverifiable from ingredient lists. Dietary safety assessments are based on standard Japanese culinary conventions; no allergy menus or staff allergy protocols have been confirmed. Celiac or severely allergic diners must contact the restaurant directly before visiting.

address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市魚町109 ハーバービル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI AddressSearch on 兵庫県姫路市魚町109; result 34.832279, 134.687271 — within target box and well under 1.15km from reference center.
neighborhood: 魚町 (Uocho) — 6–8 min walk from Sanyo Himeji Station
seats_text: 30 seats total; private tatami rooms available; capacity for up to 20 at banquets
cultural_comfort_note: A small, intimate kappo counter with no English signage or menus; staff hospitality is warm but Japanese-language fluency is expected for navigating the seasonal menu and making requests.
vegan_detail: The menu is built around seafood (sashimi, eel, fugu, shrimp), eggs (dashimaki tamago), and meat; fish dashi is foundational to the cuisine. No plant-based or vegan options are offered or indicated on any platform.
gf_detail: Standard Japanese kitchen using wheat-containing soy sauce (shoyu) throughout — in dressings, marinades, kabayaki glaze on eel, and likely dipping sauces. The menu includes tempura and fried shrimp cutlet (wheat batter). Dashimaki tamago is made with dashi. No gluten-free options, dedicated fryer, or allergen menu exist. Celiac diners face high cross-contamination risk; inquire directly and in Japanese before visiting.

bio.background: A small kuzushi kappo — the genre that brings the precision of kaiseki into a relaxed counter setting — tucked inside Harbor Building (ハーバービル) on Uocho, a short walk from Sanyo Himeji Station. The name 福酔 (fukusui, 'fortune-drunk') hints at the convivial spirit of the place.
bio.specialty: Fresh seafood sourced from a live tank in the restaurant, with seasonal sashimi platters, fugu (tessa and tetchiri hot pot in winter), grilled eel (shirayaki and kabayaki), and shrimp cutlet with housemade tartar sauce as crowd favorites.
bio.philosophy: Kappo quality at izakaya informality — a place where you can drop in for a single dish and a cup of sake or settle in for a full seasonal course.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers note the owner will prepare special dishes on advance request — one regular asked for braised pork belly (kakuni) the day before and received a prompt promise to have it ready.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura and fried shrimp cutlet (panko-breaded) are on the regular menu, making shared-fryer cross-contamination a near-certain risk. No dedicated fryer or allergen separation is mentioned on any source.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Wheat-containing soy sauce is a core ingredient in this style of cooking — used in dipping sauces, marinades (eel kabayaki glaze, tataki), and likely in most cooked dishes. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is noted anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish dashi and bonito-based stocks underpin the cuisine (dashimaki tamago, hot pot broths, sauces). There is no plant-based cooking track at this restaurant.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy menu, allergen labelling, or documented staff training for dietary restrictions was found on any platform. The owner does accommodate special food requests made a day in advance, suggesting some flexibility, but this has not been tested for allergy contexts.

positives.text: Small owner-operated counter where direct communication about needs is possible; the owner is noted by reviewers as attentive and accommodating of specific requests when given advance notice.

caveats: Hours conflict across sources: Tabelog shows LO 21:30/close 22:00 while Gnavi/Retty show LO 22:00/close 23:00 — hours_status set to irregular; confirm before visiting. No official website exists; Instagram (@fukusui0206) is the closest to an official presence. Seat count (30) is from Tabelog only. Geocode is at block level (魚町109) not building-level precision.

name_ja: ベジタブルカフェ さくらさく
cuisine: 野菜料理・カフェ（野菜ソムリエ監修）
vegan_detail: The kitchen uses only plant-based dashi (shiitake + konbu — no katsuobushi or saba-bushi) as its flavour base, which makes the side dishes inherently vegan-compatible. The daily lunch is built around four seasonal vegetable side dishes, rice, and clear soup; the meat or fish main can be omitted or swapped for a tofu steak with soy-ginger glaze on request. Staff consult individually with each guest about dietary preferences and restrictions, and the restaurant has a track record of composing fully animal-product-free meals. That said, vegan is not a standing menu item — it requires advance discussion or an on-the-spot request.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or labelling exists. The signature dessert — handmade vegetable chiffon cake — is explicitly made with domestic wheat flour, so it is not GF. Soy sauce is used in cooking (the tofu steak glaze is soy-ginger based) and standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat; tamari substitution has not been confirmed. Dashi is 100 % plant-based (shiitake + konbu), eliminating one common hidden-gluten risk. Cross-contamination risk from shared prep surfaces and the wheat-based baked goods must be assumed. Celiacs should call ahead (079-222-1220) to discuss substitutions; the staff are noted for individual dietary consultations, but GF safety cannot be assured without direct confirmation.
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市本町68（白鷺町92）
geocode_note: GSI AddressSearch on 兵庫県姫路市本町68 returned [134.687363, 34.835873]. Distance to target centre (34.8331, 134.6921) is approximately 531 m — well within the 1.15 km radius.
neighborhood: 本町（姫路城南西・山陽姫路駅北）
seats_text: 古民家を改装した店内に個室（2〜4名）・ソファ席・畳席・掘りごたつ席あり。駐車場なし。
cultural_comfort_note: The owner and staff are known to consult with each guest individually about dietary needs including for international visitors, but the café operates entirely in Japanese with no English menu; pointing at items and using simple Japanese or a translation app is the practical approach.

bio.background: Sakura Saku opened in 2005 in a beautifully renovated old machiya townhouse in Himeji's historic Honmachi district, steps from Himeji Castle. The café grew out of the owner's family business at the Himeji Central Market wholesale produce trade — her husband's decades of work sourcing fresh vegetables became the foundation for a kitchen that insists on seasonal, additive-free cooking. The owner holds a 野菜ソムリエ (Yasai Sommelier / Vegetable Sommelier) certification, a Japan-specific qualification for deep expertise in fruit and vegetable varieties, nutrition, and preparation.
bio.specialty: 旬菜ランチ（季節の野菜惣菜4品・ごはん・お吸い物・デザート）
bio.philosophy: Additive-free, pure Japanese-style home cooking that lets the natural flavour of vegetables speak. All stocks are made from shiitake mushroom and konbu kelp — no animal-derived dashi — so the food can flex to accommodate a wide range of dietary needs without changing its essential character.

anecdotes.text: The café's dashi is made exclusively from shiitake and konbu, with no bonito or mackerel flakes — a deliberate choice that has made vegan and vegetarian accommodation part of the restaurant's everyday practice since opening.
Fresh vegetables from the family's wholesale connections are sold at the entrance at market prices, making the café part grocery stop, part dining room.
The handmade vegetable chiffon cakes — pumpkin, carrot, molokhia, black bean — are baked with beet sugar and no chemical additives, and have become a signature reason to linger past lunch.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The dessert menu includes handmade vegetable chiffon cake made with domestic wheat flour; wheat is actively used in the kitchen and cross-contamination of shared prep surfaces must be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce is used in cooking (confirmed in tofu steak soy-ginger glaze); standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat. Tamari substitution has not been documented for this restaurant.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The daily lunch includes a meat or fish main course prepared in the same kitchen as the vegetable sides; those requesting a fully vegan meal should specify clearly when ordering so the kitchen can plate separately.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff are noted for consulting individually with each customer about dietary preferences and restrictions — including international guests — and can adapt the meal composition accordingly. Phone ahead (079-222-1220) is recommended for specific allergy needs.

positives.text: All stocks (dashi) are made exclusively from shiitake mushroom and konbu kelp — no katsuobushi, saba-bushi, or other animal-derived ingredients — making the flavour base inherently vegan and free from a common hidden-allergen risk.
The kitchen avoids chemical additives entirely, using only natural, whole-food ingredients sourced from the family's wholesale produce connections.

caveats: Hours vary by source (some list LO 17:00, others list close 18:00); the Tabelog listing shows 11:00-18:00 Tue-Sat. Payment accepts electronic money (Suica etc.) but not credit cards or QR codes — confirm current payment options before visiting. The restaurant has no dedicated GF menu; celiacs must call ahead. Vegan meals are available by request but are not a standing printed option.

name_ja: 餃子のかっちゃん 姫路駅前2号店
name_en: Gyoza no Kacchan Himeji Ekimae Branch 2
cuisine: 居酒屋・餃子・唐揚げ
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市南駅前町125 ビエラ姫路 東館 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode on 兵庫県姫路市南駅前町１２５番地 returned [134.690079, 34.826347] — within target box (lat 34.823–34.843, lng 134.679–134.705). Approx. 93 m from JR Himeji Station South Exit, well within 1.15 km radius.
neighborhood: 南駅前町 / JR姫路駅直近 (ビエラ姫路 東館)
seats_text: 53席。個室・半個室あり。掘りごたつ席あり。最大100名の宴会対応可。
vegan_detail: No vegan options identified. Gyoza contain pork and use wheat-flour wrappers. Karaage (fried chicken) and mapo tofu are signature items; dipping sauces and cooking stocks are likely animal-based. No plant-based or vegan alternatives are listed on any menu source.
gf_detail: Gyoza wrappers are standard wheat-flour dough — the core product is not gluten-free. Karaage is wheat-flour coated. Soy sauce (wheat-containing) is used in dipping sauces and almost certainly in cooking. No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergen substitution is mentioned anywhere on the official site, HotPepper, or press materials. This venue is not safe for celiac diners.
cultural_comfort_note: Multilingual menus in English and Simplified Chinese are available, and the ordering system is app/digital-based, making navigation straightforward for non-Japanese speakers.

bio.background: Operated by 株式会社double (founded July 2020, Osaka), 餃子のかっちゃん launched its first location in Umeda, Osaka in 2021. The Himeji Station Front Branch 2 opened on June 29, 2026, inside the Viera Himeji East Building — a complex attached to JR Himeji Station — making it one of the chain's first outposts in Hyogo Prefecture.
bio.specialty: 焼き餃子 — hand-crimped grilled dumplings with crispy skin and juicy pork filling, priced from ¥200
bio.philosophy: The chain's guiding motto is 安くて・早くて・うまい (affordable, fast, delicious), but company materials stress that staff warmth and hospitality matter more than price alone. Representative Matsui Katsuya describes the brand as bringing 'the flavor and energy refined in Osaka's gourmet scene' to new cities.

anecdotes.text: To mark the Himeji 2 opening on June 29–July 3, 2026, draft beer, highballs, and lemon sours were priced at ¥1 per glass (with a minimum appetizer and two-drink order) — a characteristically Osaka-style splash to introduce the brand to a new neighborhood.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Standard wheat-flour gyoza wrappers are made and cooked on-site daily; karaage (fried chicken) is a signature menu item using wheat-flour coating. Wheat contamination risk is pervasive throughout the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce (which contains wheat) is served tableside as a dipping condiment for gyoza and is almost certainly used in cooking sauces. No tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork is present in the core gyoza product and horse meat (馬刺しユッケ) and fried chicken are on the menu; shared cooking surfaces and fryers make vegan cross-contact highly likely.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, allergy-handling protocol, or staff allergy training is mentioned in any available source. Inquiries would need to be made in Japanese by phone (079-280-1202).

caveats: Brand-new venue (opened June 29, 2026) — very few diner reviews exist yet. Hours sourced from HotPepper and owst.jp; verify directly as they may refine post-opening. No allergen menu found in any source; the kitchen works heavily with wheat at every stage.

name_ja: 甲賀米粉たい焼き 姫路城前店
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市綿町104-2
geocode_note: Geocoded to chō level (綿町) via GSI AddressSearch; building number 104-2 not resolved to a finer point. Approx. 154m from target center — well within 1.15km radius.
neighborhood: 綿町 (Wata-cho) — Miyuki-dori shopping arcade, midway between JR Himeji Station and Himeji Castle
hours_raw: 11:00〜19:00、年中無休（不定休の可能性あり）
closed_days: No regular closed days listed (open year-round per Tabelog; irregular closures possible)
seats_text: Counter seating only; primarily a takeaway stall. No table seating.
cultural_comfort_note: Takeaway stall in a high-traffic tourist corridor; ordering is point-and-pay simple, but staff likely speak Japanese only — a basic greeting and pointing at the menu is all you need.
gf_detail: The entire menu uses domestically sourced rice flour exclusively — no wheat flour in any product. Declared allergens on the chain's products are egg and dairy only; wheat is absent from both the ingredient list and allergen declarations. No wheat soy sauce or wheat-based dashi involved — this is a sweet pastry shop. Cross-contamination risk from wheat is very low given the dedicated rice-flour concept, though the shop does not publish a formal shared-kitchen statement. Ideal for wheat-allergy diners; for celiac-level sensitivity, confirming directly with staff is always advisable.
vegan_detail: The rice flour dough contains egg and both cow's milk and soy milk. The custard filling adds egg yolk, egg white, and dairy cream. The anko (red bean) filling's dough base still contains egg and milk. No vegan option exists on the current menu.

bio.background: Opened April 2024 on Miyuki-dori shopping arcade in Himeji by Shirasagi Knit Industrial Co., Ltd. (白鷺ニット工業株式会社), a 50-year-old Himeji-based functional innerwear manufacturer. The company launched a Tourism Business Division to address a pattern they observed: visitors to Himeji Castle rarely spent money locally, preferring to shop in Kobe or Osaka. The taiyaki shop is their declared 'first step' in building tourism revenue for the city.
bio.origin: Franchise affiliate of Koga Komeko Taiyaki, a chain originating in Shiga Prefecture
bio.specialty: Rice flour taiyaki — crispy exterior, chewy mochi-like interior; signature flavors include Tokachi azuki red bean, custard cream, chocolate, and rotating seasonal varieties (sakura, yuzu, chestnut)
bio.philosophy: Creating excitement in Himeji and transforming it into a destination where visitors stay and spend locally. Each taiyaki is made to order rather than pre-baked, prioritizing freshness. The shop pairs taiyaki with premium Japanese tea from Harima-ya Chaho, a local established tea merchant.
roles: Franchise operator: Shirasagi Knit Industrial Co., Ltd. (Tourism Business Division)

anecdotes.text: During the preopening period in March 2024, the shop drew over 500 visitors per day — a strong signal that Himeji's tourists were hungry for a quality local snack stop between the station and the castle.
Shirasagi Knit has made Himeji its home for over 50 years as an innerwear maker; the taiyaki shop represents their bet that the same city can become a tourism destination worth lingering in.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No wheat is used anywhere in the production process — the dough is 100% rice flour. Cross-contamination from wheat within this shop is very low risk by design, though no formal certification is published.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy sauce or wheat-containing condiments are used. This is a sweet pastry shop; soy sauce is not part of any product.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Egg and dairy are present in all products (dough contains egg, cow's milk, and soy milk; custard filling adds more egg and dairy). There is no vegan-safe option on the menu.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No published allergy-handling protocol found for this location. The concept is wheat-free by design, which simplifies wheat-allergy inquiries, but staff are not confirmed to speak English or handle complex allergy queries in writing.

positives.text: Entire menu is wheat-free by concept — rice flour is the only flour used in all products, making this a naturally safe environment for wheat-allergic diners without needing to scrutinize individual items.
Allergen declarations on chain products list only egg and dairy — wheat is absent from the ingredient list entirely.

caveats: Hours on Tabelog (11:00-19:00) differ slightly from the preopening blog (10:00-20:00) and a visitor blog (10:00-17:00); Tabelog is treated as the most current source but hours may have been adjusted since opening. Allergen data is sourced from the chain's frozen retail products (deli-picks.com), not from the fresh in-store version, which may have slightly different ingredients — confirm with staff for celiac-level safety. The geocode resolves only to chō level (綿町), not the exact building.

address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市南駅前町127-1
geocode_note: GSI address-search on 兵庫県姫路市南駅前町127-1 returned [134.691986, 34.825314]. ~870 m from target center — within the 1.15 km radius. Restaurant is inside Viela Himeji, directly beneath elevated JR tracks south of Himeji Station.
neighborhood: JR姫路駅南口すぐ（ビエラ姫路内）
seats_text: 立ち食いカウンター約13席＋奥にテーブル席あり。合計約20席
cultural_comfort_note: Standing sushi counter with no English signage; ordering is done by pointing or simple Japanese; foreign visitors can manage but staff assistance in English is unlikely.
vegan_detail: All items are fish and seafood nigiri; complimentary miso soup is included with sets and typically contains katsuobushi-based dashi and clams. No plant-based alternatives exist on the menu. Not viable for vegans.
gf_detail: Standard Japanese soy sauce (containing wheat) is used at the counter — celiac risk. Shari (vinegared rice) itself is wheat-free (Hyogo Kinuhikari rice with proprietary vinegar blend), but the shoyu applied to nigiri almost certainly contains wheat. No tamari or GF soy sauce option has been documented. Cross-contamination risk is high at a busy standing counter. No dedicated GF accommodation noted anywhere in reviews or on-site materials.

bio.background: The owner (大将) previously worked at Himeji City Central Wholesale Market (姫路市中央卸売市場), giving him direct expertise in evaluating and sourcing fish. He leverages his own unique sourcing routes built during those years to bring premium-grade fish to a casual standing counter at unusually accessible prices.
bio.specialty: 極上一本穴子 — a single whole conger eel pressed into one thick, fluffy nigiri; the signature item that draws queues before opening. Rice is Hyogo-prefecture Kinuhikari with a proprietary sweetened-vinegar blend developed to complement the seafood.
bio.philosophy: High-grade fish at counter-casual prices with rapid turnover; queues form 30–60 minutes before opening and orders are taken while guests wait in line so food is ready almost immediately upon being seated.

anecdotes.text: Regulars from Kobe make the trip specifically for this counter despite typical Saturday waits exceeding one hour.
On a Saturday at noon, one visitor reported a 90-minute wait — the queue begins forming well before the 11:30 opening.
The chef is described by one blogger as resembling a famous musician with an intense presence behind the counter.

gf_cross_contamination.text: High-volume standing counter with multiple fish preparations happening simultaneously; no separation of GF and non-GF prep surfaces documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce is used on nigiri at the counter; no GF soy sauce (tamari) or wheat-free shoyu option has been mentioned in any review or listing.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is seafood-based; no plant-only preparation zones exist. Miso soup served with sets contains fish-based dashi.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling procedures, dedicated allergen menu, or staff allergy training have been documented in any source reviewed.

positives.text: Rice (shari) itself uses only Kinuhikari rice and a vinegar blend — the rice base is wheat-free, which is the foundation of each nigiri.

caveats: Hours are irregular — the kitchen closes when the day's fish runs out, which can be well before the posted closing time. Cash only; no card or e-money. Saturday queues of 60–90 minutes are common; arriving at least 30 minutes before opening is strongly advised. The official domain sushi-totoro.com has lapsed and now redirects to a domain-parking page.

address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市駅前町345 みき正ビルB1F
geocode_note: GSI address-search on 兵庫県姫路市駅前町３４５番地 returned [134.691422, 34.829659]; ~388m from target center, well within 1.15km radius.
neighborhood: 駅前町 / Miyuki-dori shopping arcade, Himeji Station area
seats_text: Approximately 30 seats, all table seating; semi-private partitioned areas available, accommodates groups up to 12.
hours_raw: 月曜定休。火曜ディナーのみ 17:30-21:00。水〜日・祝 ランチ 11:30-14:00（売り切れ次第終了）、ディナー 17:30-21:00。
cultural_comfort_note: Basement izakaya/kappo-style restaurant in a local shopping arcade; English signage or menus are unlikely, though the setting is welcoming to tourists visiting Himeji Castle.
vegan_detail: The entire concept centers on fresh conger eel (穴子/anago). There are no plant-based mains. Dashi is very likely fish-based (katsuobushi or niboshi). No vegan options confirmed or implied across any source.
gf_detail: Anago is naturally gluten-free, but the restaurant prepares conger eel tempura (wheat batter), and Japanese soy sauce (含む小麦) is standard throughout. Dashi is fish-based, not wheat-based. No dedicated gluten-free menu or certification exists. Cross-contamination from shared fryer oil used for tempura is a real concern. Diners with celiac disease should ask specifically before ordering.

bio.background: A small independent specialist restaurant tucked into the basement of the Miki-sei Building on Himeji's Miyuki-dori shopping arcade, just a few minutes' walk from Himeji Station. The restaurant built its identity around a single prized local ingredient: Himeji anago (conger eel), sourced fresh and live daily from the Seto Inland Sea — specifically the prized 'Densuke' (伝助) variety, a large, thick-fleshed eel celebrated in the region.
bio.specialty: Live conger eel (活け穴子) prepared to order in multiple styles — sashimi, seared 'half-killed' (半殺し), tempura, shabu-shabu, and sushi. Live sashimi is limited to 10 servings daily.
bio.philosophy: Make anago an everyday pleasure rather than a special-occasion luxury. The restaurant's tagline emphasizes abundant local sake (播磨の地酒) paired with the region's most celebrated seafood.

anecdotes.text: The signature 'half-killed' (半殺し) seared preparation showcases the eel's natural sweetness and plump texture — a name that sounds alarming but refers to a flash-searing technique that leaves the eel partially raw, maximizing umami.

Live anago sashimi is available only as 10 servings per day and is described by visitors as 'a dish rarely available elsewhere' — advance reservation for dinner courses is strongly recommended to secure it.
The restaurant describes itself as having a 'Showa-era ambiance' (昭和の雰囲気) — a nostalgic, unfussy basement setting that contrasts with the rarity and quality of the ingredient it serves.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura is a core menu item and likely uses the same frying oil as other dishes. No dedicated gluten-free fryer is mentioned anywhere.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (含む小麦) is the default condiment throughout the menu. No wheat-free tamari or soy sauce alternative is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish-based dashi is standard in Japanese creative cuisine at this level. All known dishes contain eel or fish-derived ingredients.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy menu, allergy awareness training, or allergy-friendly service is referenced on any platform. Diners with serious allergies should call ahead (079-288-7714).

positives.text: Anago (conger eel) itself is naturally gluten-free when served raw or grilled without batter or sauce. Sashimi and plain-grilled preparations are the safest starting point for a GF inquiry.

caveats: Cash only confirmed by HotPepper and Retty; Tabelog may reflect outdated or incorrect card information. Hours on Monday vary across sources — HotPepper and himeji-kanko.jp agree Monday is fully closed; confirm by phone before visiting. Lunch is limited-quantity (sells out, especially anago sashimi at 10 portions/day). Smoking is permitted during dinner hours. A ¥350 otoshi (table charge) applies per person at dinner.

neighborhood: 生野町 (Ikuno-cho) — Kyoguchi Station area
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市生野町1-16
geocode_note: Coordinates from Yahoo Maps place record (lat 34.840142, lng 134.700703). Calculated distance to target center ~1,107m, within the 1.15km radius. GSI geocoder returned chō-level only (生野町１番地); Yahoo Maps pin used as more precise.
hours_raw: 月・火・木〜日 12:00〜22:00、水曜定休（祝日の場合は翌日休）
closed_days: 水曜日（Wednesdays; if Wednesday falls on a public holiday, the following day is closed instead）
seats_text: 24 seats: counter 4, table 4, ground-floor tatami 8, second-floor tatami 8. Shoes off on the second floor. Smoking permitted throughout.
cultural_comfort_note: A well-worn, smoky neighborhood yakiniku den with a joking owner and mostly local regulars; English menus and non-Japanese-speaking staff are not expected here.
vegan_detail: Hormon Tatsu is a meat-specialist restaurant — the menu is built entirely around beef and pork offal, yakiniku cuts, hormone hot pot, and grilled liver. There are no plant-based mains or sides documented.
gf_detail: Yakiniku restaurants rely on tare (dipping sauce) and marinades that almost universally contain wheat-based soy sauce. The side menu includes okonomiyaki and hormone udon — both wheat-heavy. No gluten-free labeling, dedicated fryer, or allergy information is mentioned anywhere. Celiac diners should not eat here.

bio.background: Hormon Tatsu has a layered history unusual even among old-school Himeji joints: the space originally operated as a candy shop (駄菓子屋), evolved into an okonomiyaki restaurant, then added hormone udon, and eventually became a full yakiniku and grilled-offal specialist. It has built a loyal local following over the years, with weekend reservations now recommended.
bio.specialty: 厚切り塩タン (thick-cut salt tongue) — sliced to roughly 1 cm, three pieces per person, and limited to one order at the start of the meal due to demand. The house hormone (offal) is praised for retaining fat and avoiding the gaminess that poorly-handled offal can carry.
bio.philosophy: Old-fashioned ambiance, generous cuts, and an affable owner whose banter with regulars is part of the atmosphere — 'showa-era' comfort over trendy presentation.

anecdotes.text: Enka singer Oka Midori raved about the thick-cut salt tongue on the Nippon TV variety programme 沸騰ワード10, triggering a spike in visitors to the unassuming shopfront.
A blogger described the exterior as '外見、瀕死じゃないですか' (the place looks like it's on its last legs) — yet found the meat quality more than compensated for the humble shopfront.
The owner is known for dry humor with regulars, including a jest about 夜逃げ (doing a runner), signalling the relaxed, local atmosphere.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Yakiniku tare and marinades at this type of restaurant contain wheat-based soy sauce. The menu also includes okonomiyaki and hormone udon — both made with wheat flour. Cross-contamination risk is high across the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (含む小麦) is the foundation of yakiniku dipping sauce and most marinades used here; no wheat-free soy sauce alternative has been mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every item on the menu is meat or offal-based. There are no vegan dishes and the entire cooking surface and kitchen is devoted to animal products.

caveats: Hours flagged as irregular because Wednesdays become the next day off when Wednesday is a public holiday, and year-end/New Year hours vary. Geocode used Yahoo Maps pin (more precise than GSI chō-level result). Distance to target center ~1.1km — within the 1.15km limit but close to the boundary; confirm on a map before routing. The restaurant is entirely smoke-permitted, which may be relevant to some visitors.

address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市朝日町58 メゾンソレイユ 1F
geocode_note: GSI AddressSearch on 兵庫県姫路市朝日町58 returned [134.69693, 34.828014]. ~716m from target center (34.8331, 134.6921), well within 1.15km radius.
neighborhood: 朝日町 (Asahi-cho) — ~8 min walk east of JR Himeji Station
seats_text: 12 seats total — 6 counter seats and a 6-seat private room
hours_raw: Lunch 12:00〜 (last seating 12:30), Dinner 18:00〜 (last seating 19:00–20:00); closed days irregular (不定休)
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular); confirm by phone before visiting
cultural_comfort_note: A 12-seat intimate counter kaiseki with a Japanese-only online presence; phone reservations expected and English assistance is not advertised.
vegan_detail: Cuisine is built around seasonal seafood, fish-based dashi (likely katsuo/konbu), and Harima-region meats — no vegan menu is listed. The tightly curated omakase format means dietary needs must be communicated well in advance by phone; hidden animal-derived stock in every course is the default assumption.
gf_detail: Traditional kaiseki routinely uses wheat-based soy sauce (koikuchi shoyu) and mirin, and some courses may include tempura batter or wheat-thickened sauces. No gluten-free menu or dedicated allergen protocol is listed on any source. Celiac-level safety cannot be assumed; contact the restaurant by phone before booking.

bio.background: Born 1983 in Takasago, Hyogo Prefecture, Nakae discovered cooking at 18 through part-time work at a chicken restaurant. At 22 he moved to Australia alone, then worked his way through NOBU Melbourne, London, and Dubai — eventually rising to sous chef — before a crisis of confidence sent him back to Japan to master the Japanese culinary foundation he felt he lacked. He apprenticed at a prestigious Akasaka restaurant, then joined the three-Michelin-star Ginza Kojuu (銀座小十), where mentor chef Okuda reshaped his ambition from personal success to the long-term preservation of Japanese cuisine. He became head chef there, returned to Hyogo to lead the kitchen at Ginpaiso ryokan in Ako, and took a detour into sake brewing at Okada Honke (岡田本家) in Kakogawa before opening Tanryu in April 2019.
bio.specialty: Seasonal Harima omakase kaiseki — grilled sweetfish (鮎), cuttlefish-ink squid, scattered sushi and other dishes built around direct relationships with local fishermen, farmers, and sake brewers
bio.philosophy: The restaurant's name 淡流 (tanryu — 'gentle flowing water') comes from a teaching about moving quietly and connecting past traditions to future possibilities. Chef Nakae serves as an Omotesenke tea-ceremony instructor and personally prepares matcha and house-made sweets to close each meal. A formative regret — losing access to a local farmer he had initially turned away — drives his current mission to benefit the surrounding community of producers through every dish he serves.

anecdotes.text: Chef Nakae published a visual book, '日本料理 淡流の一期一会' (June 2023, Kinmokuseisha), pairing each seasonal dish with ceramics by artist Bonshutsu Tetsunobu and Zen philosophy — underscoring the restaurant's tea-ceremony-inflected aesthetic.

A pivotal regret shaped his cooking: a local Harima farmer he had initially declined as a supplier later went out of business. That loss now drives his commitment to sourcing from and promoting local producers via social media and regional tourism projects.

Tanryu has appeared in Gault & Millau (rated 16/20 — 'excellent') for four consecutive years, 2023–2026, and won the 2026 Tabelog Award Bronze and was named to Tabelog 100 (2025, Japanese cuisine, Western Japan).

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on cross-contamination controls is available from any source reviewed. The small open-counter kitchen format means multiple courses with wheat-containing ingredients are prepared in the same space.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional Japanese kaiseki at this level standardly uses wheat-containing koikuchi shoyu and mirin throughout — in marinades, glazes, and sauces. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dashi is the foundational liquid in kaiseki cuisine; at this restaurant it is almost certainly fish-based (katsuo/konbu). No plant-based dashi alternative is listed or implied by any reviewed source.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling protocol or staff allergen training is documented on any platform. Phone reservations are standard and the restaurant explicitly recommends calling ahead to discuss meal preparation — this is the appropriate channel to raise dietary needs.

positives.text: 12-seat intimate format with an owner-chef who cooks every course means direct dialogue about dietary needs is possible when flagged well in advance by phone.

caveats: Hours listed reflect the consistent pattern across Tabelog, Gurunavi, and Hitosara (lunch seating 12:00, dinner from 18:00) but exact last-order varies by source (19:00 vs 20:00). Closed days are irregular — always confirm by phone (079-240-8872) before visiting. The official site tanryu.com was unreachable during research (connection refused); all data sourced from third-party listings and the Himeji city profile. Gluten-free and vegan assessments are inferred from standard kaiseki practices; no direct confirmation or denial from the restaurant itself was obtainable.

name_ja: 姫路の地酒と姫路おでん本舗
name_en: Himeji no Jizake to Himeji Oden Honpo
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市豆腐町222 ピオレ姫路おみやげ館1F
geocode_note: GSI address-search on 兵庫県姫路市豆腐町222 returned [134.688904, 34.826965]; within target box (lat 34.823–34.843, lng 134.679–134.705). Secondary address 駅前町188-1 also geocodes within box at [134.690842, 34.827621]. Both addresses refer to the same Piole Himeji Omiyage-kan building.
neighborhood: ピオレ姫路 / JR姫路駅直結
vegan_detail: Himeji oden is simmered in a fish-based dashi broth and served with ginger soy sauce (which contains wheat). No vegan menu options are listed or advertised. The signature dish is inherently non-vegan.
gf_detail: Oden broth is dashi-based (fish stock); the house dipping sauce is ginger soy sauce, which contains wheat. No gluten-free options are advertised. Cross-contamination risk is high in a casual food-court oden environment. Not safe for celiac diners without direct staff inquiry, and no evidence of any GF accommodation.
hours_raw: 11:00–20:00 (LO 19:45) daily; follows Piole Himeji Omiyage-kan mall hours. Tabelog lists 11:00–21:30 (LO 21:00) but the official Piole page shows 11:00–20:00.
closed_days: No fixed closed days; follows Piole Himeji shopping complex schedule
seats_text: Food-court style shared seating; no fixed seat count published. Casual drop-in format inside the Piole Himeji souvenir hall.
cultural_comfort_note: Station-building food-court setting is accessible and tourist-friendly, but menus and signage are in Japanese with no evidence of English-language staff or materials.

bio.background: Opened in March 2016 inside the Piole Himeji Omiyage-kan (souvenir hall) adjoining JR Himeji Station, the shop was established as a flagship venue for Himeji oden — the city's signature dish, eaten with ginger soy sauce rather than the mustard typical elsewhere. It also serves sake from all seven of Himeji's local breweries (jizake), making it a one-stop showcase of Banshu regional food culture. The location and food-court format target commuters, day-trippers, and visitors to Himeji Castle.
bio.specialty: Himeji oden (local hot-pot simmered in dashi, dipped in ginger soy sauce) and jizake from all seven Himeji-area breweries
bio.philosophy: Provide an accessible, anchor venue where visitors can reliably experience authentic Himeji oden and local sake without needing to navigate the city's izakaya scene.

anecdotes.text: The shop serves oden with konnyaku shaped like Himeji Castle, a small local touch that has become a talking point among visitors.
Positioned as the flagship venue of the Himeji Oden Association's effort to promote ginger soy sauce oden as a recognized regional cuisine.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Food-court oden environment with shared cooking surfaces; no information on separation of allergens. High cross-contamination risk assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature ginger soy sauce dipping condiment contains wheat. Standard Japanese soy sauce (shoyu) used in oden broth also contains wheat. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All oden items are simmered in fish-based dashi broth. No vegan broth alternative is indicated. Cross-contact with fish and meat products throughout the menu.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling procedures or allergen menu have been documented across any source reviewed. Direct inquiry required before dining.

positives.text: Fully non-smoking interior.

caveats: Hours conflict between Tabelog (11:00–21:30) and the official Piole shop page (11:00–20:00); the mall operator page is treated as authoritative but hours may vary seasonally. Two address formats circulate (豆腐町222 and 駅前町188-1); both refer to the same Piole Himeji Omiyage-kan building and both geocode within the target area. No English sources confirmed; all information is from Japanese-language pages.

name_ja: 居酒屋 遊膳 本店
cuisine: 居酒屋・日本料理・海鮮
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市呉服町33 WOFビル1F
geocode_note: Original listing (Tabelog 28029489) was at 紺屋町80, which geocoded to (34.830376, 134.693542) — within target box. That building caught fire April 2024; restaurant relocated September 2024 to 呉服町33 WOFビル1F, geocoded via GSI to (34.830929, 134.69223), also within target box. Both coordinates verified in-range. Tabelog now lists the business under 28070635 at the new address.
neighborhood: 呉服町・みゆき通り商店街
seats_text: カウンター席、掘りごたつ席（テーブル4卓）、約22席
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional Japanese izakaya with shoe-removal entry and tatami/sunken seating; menus are in Japanese, staff interaction is typical of a local neighbourhood pub with little expected English.
vegan_detail: No vegan-designated items on the menu. The kitchen centres on fresh seafood, Banshu jidori chicken, and local produce. Dashi is almost certainly katsuobushi- or niboshi-based, and soy sauce is standard wheat-containing shoyu. Individual requests may be possible for simple vegetable dishes but cross-contact with fish and meat should be assumed throughout.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or allergen labelling documented. Soy sauce used throughout the kitchen is standard wheat-containing shoyu. Tempura appears on the menu (wheat batter). Dashi base likely contains wheat-bearing soy sauce marinades. Dedicated fryer status unknown. Celiac diners should inquire carefully before ordering — safe options cannot be assumed.

bio.background: Kataoka Keisuke opened the original Yuzen in Himeji in 2011, building a neighbourhood izakaya around Harima-region sake and locally sourced seafood. On April 15, 2024, a fire broke out on the second floor of the building housing the restaurant; the first-floor shop suffered extensive damage and could not reopen at that location. Rather than closing permanently, Kataoka rebuilt and reopened on September 1, 2024 as 遊膳 本店 (Main Store) in a new space on Miyuki-dori, roughly a three-minute walk from the original site.
bio.specialty: Daily-changing sashimi featuring fish sourced same-day from local Himeji fisheries, paired with rare Harima-district sake such as Honda Shoten's 龍力 (Ryuriki) and Okubanshu labels
bio.philosophy: Hyper-local sourcing — seafood from daytime nets, lotus root from Hirohata district, Banshu jidori chicken — presented in a relaxed, counter-heavy izakaya that regulars describe as feeling unchanged despite the move.

anecdotes.text: After the April 2024 fire destroyed the original space, Kataoka preserved the dark-brown colour scheme, the counter arrangement, and the shoe-removal entrance style at the new location, so returning guests would feel at home. Customers greeted him on reopening with 'Thank you for coming back,' and reservations filled daily.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No allergen menu or GF protocol is documented. Tempura is offered and the fryer's dedication is unknown, making cross-contamination from wheat batter a real risk.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard wheat-containing shoyu is used throughout; no tamari or GF soy sauce alternative has been mentioned in any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen handles seafood, chicken, and other animal proteins continuously; no plant-only prep area is documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling protocol or allergen chart has been reported. Phone reservations are available (079-287-8872) and would be the appropriate channel to discuss dietary needs before visiting.

positives.text: Menu is highly seasonal and made-to-order from daily-sourced local ingredients, which means the kitchen may be able to accommodate specific requests if contacted in advance.

caveats: The Tabelog listing supplied in the brief (28029489, 紺屋町80) is tagged 移転 (relocated) — it reflects pre-fire/pre-move information and is no longer the operating address. The restaurant now trades as 居酒屋 遊膳 本店 at 呉服町33 WOFビル1F under Tabelog ID 28070635. Hours (17:00-22:30 Mon–Sat) are consistent across sources as of late 2024 but should be confirmed before visiting as a newly relocated venue. No GF or vegan menu documentation exists; celiac and vegan diners should call ahead.

name_ja: アジアンダイニング 金魚蘭
cuisine: インドネシア料理・アジア料理（タイ・ベトナム・インドネシア・創作アジア）
address_ja: 兵庫県姫路市紺屋町77 KJビル1F
geocode_note: GSI AddressSearch on 兵庫県姫路市紺屋町77 returned [134.693542, 34.830376]. Approx 330m from target centre — within 1.15km radius.
neighborhood: 紺屋町・おみぞ筋商店街（山陽姫路駅から約6分徒歩）
